The change in conductivity due to light is: $$\Delta\sigma = q(\mu_n \Delta n + \mu_p \Delta p)$$ where $\Delta n$ and $\Delta p$ are the excess electron and hole concentrations generated by light. *(2 marks for principle, 2 marks for EHP explanation, 2 marks for conductivity equation, 1 mark for steady-state discussion.)* **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Procedure for R vs L Characteristic:** 1. **Setup:** Place the LDR and a lux meter side by side at a fixed, measured distance from a variable-intensity light source (typically $10$ to $20\ \text{cm}$). 2. **Circuit:** Connect the LDR, a DC milliammeter, a DC source, and a rheostat in **series**. Connect a voltmeter in **parallel** with the LDR. 3. **Constant Excitation:** Set a fixed DC voltage across the LDR (e.g., $V = 10\ \text{V}$) using the rheostat. Hold this voltage constant throughout the experiment. 4. **Vary Illuminance:** Gradually increase the light source intensity in steps. For each step, record the lux meter reading ($L$), voltmeter reading ($V$), and ammeter reading ($I$). 5. **Compute Resistance:** Calculate $R = V / I$ for each illuminance value. 6. **Tabulate** the values of $L$, $V$, $I$, and $R$. 7. **Plot** $R$ vs $L$ on **log-log graph paper**. The expected curve is a straight line with negative slope. 8. **Compute** the constants $a$ and $b$ from the slope ($= -b$) and intercept ($= \log a$). **Four Practical Applications of LDRs:** 1. **Automatic Street Light Control:** LDR detects daylight and automatically switches off streetlights; switches them on at dusk. 2. **Camera Light Meters:** LDRs measure scene brightness for automatic exposure control in film/digital cameras. 3. **Security Alarm Systems:** LDR-based sensors detect interruption of a light beam, triggering alarms in intruder detection. 4. **Automatic Headlamp Dimmers in Automobiles:** LDRs sense ambient light and switch headlights between high and low beam automatically. *(3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for circuit/setup, 2 marks for applications.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign of Sensitivity:** Students frequently write $S = +dR/dL$ and lose a full mark. Always show $S$ is **negative**. > 2. **Plot Scale:** Drawing R vs L on **linear graph paper** loses 1 mark. Always specify and use **log-log** scale. > 3. **Unit Errors:** Reporting sensitivity without units ($\Omega/\text{lux}$) or with wrong units costs a mark. > 4. **Confusing Illuminance with Luminous Flux:** Illuminance $L$ is in **lux** (lumens per m²), not lumens. Many students interchange these. > 5. **Forgetting Photon Energy Condition:** The photon must have $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap). Saying "any light can change resistance" is technically incomplete. > 6. **Ammeter-Voltmeter Position Errors:** Ammeter must be **in series**, voltmeter **in parallel** with LDR. Drawing them reversed is a common but expensive mistake. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **LDR** = Light Dependent Resistor; a passive photoconductive transducer whose resistance **decreases** as incident light intensity **increases**. - The two most common materials are **CdS** ($\lambda_{peak} \approx 550\ \text{nm}$) and **CdSe** ($\lambda_{peak} \approx 690\ \text{nm}$). - The core physical phenomenon is **photoconductivity** — generation of electron-hole pairs by absorbed photons. - The empirical characteristic equation is the **power law**: $R = a \cdot L^{-b}$, where $b \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$ for CdS. - A **log-log plot** of R vs L yields a **straight line** with slope $= -b$ and intercept $= \log(a)$. - The **static sensitivity** is $S = dR/dL = -a \cdot b \cdot L^{-(b+1)}$ — always **negative** for an LDR. - The **V-I characteristic** at constant illuminance is **linear (Ohmic)**, with steeper slopes at higher illuminances. - Required apparatus: LDR, DC source, voltmeter, ammeter, variable light source, lux meter, rheostat. - **Units to remember:** Illuminance → **lux**; Resistance → $\Omega$; Sensitivity → $\Omega/\text{lux}$. - **Applications:** automatic street lights, camera light meters, security alarms, automotive headlamp dimmers. - **Exam must-do:** State the equation, plot on **log-log** paper, calculate $a$ and $b$ from two data points, and report sensitivity with **correct sign and units**. ---

topics.title: Characteristics of Thermistor and RTD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Thermistor and RTD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Characteristics of Thermistor and RTD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Transducers** are passive electronic devices whose electrical resistance changes predictably and reproducibly with variation in temperature. They convert a thermal energy input into a measurable electrical resistance output, making them primary sensing elements in industrial and laboratory temperature measurement systems.

The two most widely studied temperature transducers in the PCAEL307 laboratory syllabus are:

- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** A positive temperature coefficient (PTC) device fabricated from pure metals (commonly Platinum, Nickel, or Copper), whose resistance increases approximately linearly with temperature.
- **Thermistor (Thermal Resistor):** A semiconductor-based temperature sensor (usually made from metal oxides like Mn, Ni, Co, or Fe oxides sintered together), exhibiting a strong non-linear temperature-resistance relationship. Available as **NTC** (Negative Temperature Coefficient) and **PTC** (Positive Temperature Coefficient) variants.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two Personalities of Resistance"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine two people reacting differently to a fever (temperature rise).
>
> 1. **The RTD behaves like a calm, reliable adult:** As temperature rises, the adult's resistance to the world (electrical resistance) goes up steadily and predictably — a small, linear, trustworthy change. You can trust their behavior over a long conversation (wide temperature range).
>
> 2. **The NTC Thermistor behaves like a dramatic child:** As temperature rises even slightly, the child's resistance to the world drops dramatically — a huge, highly sensitive but non-linear change. They are very accurate in a narrow mood range (limited temperature range) but become unpredictable if pushed too far.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard RTD Reference Resistance:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ (for **PT100**) and $R_0 = 1000\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ (for **PT1000**).
- **Platinum Mean Temperature Coefficient (DIN/IEC 60751):** $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Standard Temperature Range (RTD):** $-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+850^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Standard Temperature Range (Thermistor NTC):** $-55^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+150^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typical lab range $0$–$100^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- **Resistance Tolerance:** PT100 Class A: $\pm 0.15^{\circ}\text{C}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Thermistor Beta Constant (β):** Typical $3000\text{K}$ to $5000\text{K}$, encapsulating the semiconductor material's thermal activation energy.

### 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **CO2** | Analyze the static and dynamic characteristics of various transducers | L4 – Analyze |
| **CO3** | Evaluate the performance parameters of measurement systems experimentally | L5 – Evaluate |
| **CO4** | Interpret measurement data and prepare laboratory reports with graphical analysis | L4 – Analyze |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Families of Temperature Transducers

### 2.1.1 RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector

The operating principle is based on the **scattering of conduction electrons by lattice vibrations (phonons)**. As temperature rises:

- Atomic lattice vibrates with greater amplitude.
- Electron mean free path decreases.
- Drift velocity decreases.
- Resistance **increases linearly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pure Platinum?** Platinum is the industrial standard because it offers:
> - Highest stability and repeatability
> - Near-linear $R$–$T$ relationship
> - Chemical inertness (corrosion resistant)
> - Wide operating range
> - High melting point ($1768^{\circ}\text{C}$)

### 2.1.2 Thermistor — Thermal Resistor

The operating principle is based on **semiconductor band-gap thermal activation**. As temperature rises:

- More charge carriers (electrons and holes) are thermally excited across the band gap.
- Carrier concentration increases exponentially.
- Conductivity increases.
- Resistance **decreases exponentially** (NTC type).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NTC stands for **Negative Temperature Coefficient** — resistance *decreases* as temperature *increases*. This is the dominant type used in lab experiments.

## 2.2 Mathematical Models

### 2.2.1 RTD Mathematical Model

For an ideal linear RTD, the relationship is given by:

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T\right)$$

For higher accuracy over wide range, the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** is used (for $T < 0^{\circ}\text{C}$, a 4th-degree polynomial is required):

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3\right)$$

For the positive temperature range ($T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$), the equation simplifies to:

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2\right)$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (in °C)
- $R_0$ = resistance at $T = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typically $100\ \Omega$)
- $\alpha$ = mean temperature coefficient ($\approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $\beta$ = second-order non-linearity coefficient ($\approx -5.85 \times 10^{-7}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}^2$)

### 2.2.2 Thermistor Mathematical Model

The resistance-temperature relationship for an NTC thermistor is governed by the **Steinhart-Hart equation** (full form):

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R_T) + C [\ln(R_T)]^3$$

Where temperatures are in **Kelvin** and $A, B, C$ are calibration constants.

For simplified lab analysis, the **Beta (β) equation** is used:

$$R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in K)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference absolute temperature $T_0$ (typically $298.15\ \text{K} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typically $3000$–$5000\ \text{K}$)
- $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Linear RTD model | $T$ in °C |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ | Quadratic RTD model | $T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | NTC Thermistor (β model) | $T, T_0$ in Kelvin |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R_T) + C[\ln(R_T)]^3$ | Steinhart-Hart (full NTC) | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 5 | $\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}$ | Mean temperature coefficient | $R_{100}$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 6 | $S_{RTD} = \frac{dR}{dT} = \alpha R_0$ | Sensitivity of RTD (linear region) | $\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 7 | $S_{Th} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} R_T$ | Sensitivity of NTC Thermistor | $\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ (negative) |
| 8 | $P = I^2 R$ | Self-heating power (must be kept low) | Watts |
| 9 | $\tau = \frac{m \cdot c}{h \cdot A}$ | Thermal time constant (dynamic) | Seconds |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \frac{P}{h \cdot A}$ | Self-heating temperature rise | °C or K |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications:**
> - **PT100 RTDs:** Used in process industries (petrochemical, power plants, HVAC), pharmaceutical manufacturing, food processing, and cryogenics.
> - **NTC Thermistors:** Used in inrush current limiters, battery temperature monitoring, automotive engine coolant sensing, medical equipment (incubators, dialysis machines), and precision laboratory thermometry within narrow ranges.

## 2.5 Static Characteristics of Measurement Systems

For both transducers, the following static characteristics are evaluated experimentally:

| Parameter | Definition | Thermistor (NTC) | RTD (PT100) |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness to true value | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.15$ to $\pm 0.3^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Sensitivity** | $\Delta R / \Delta T$ | Very high | Moderate |
| **Linearity** | $R$ vs $T$ graph shape | Highly non-linear | Approximately linear |
| **Range** | Usable temperature span | Narrow | Wide |
| **Stability / Drift** | Long-term resistance change | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Response Time** | Time to reach 63.2% of final value | Fast (small size) | Moderate |
| **Self-heating** | Internal heat dissipation | Significant (low mass) | Negligible |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RTD Sensitivity ($S_{RTD}$)

**Step 1 — Start with the linear RTD equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T\right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature $T$:**

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \cdot \frac{d}{dT}\left(1 + \alpha T\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constant multiplication rule:**

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \cdot \left(0 + \alpha\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the derivative result:**

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = \alpha \cdot R_0$$

**Step 5 — Define the result as Sensitivity:**

$$S_{RTD} = \alpha R_0 = (3.85 \times 10^{-3}) \times 100\ \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{S_{RTD} = 0.385\ \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The PT100 RTD sensitivity is constant and temperature-independent in the linear approximation. This makes instrumentation and signal conditioning circuits (such as Wheatstone bridges) significantly simpler to design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NTC Thermistor Sensitivity ($S_{Th}$)

**Step 1 — Start with the Beta equation for NTC thermistor:**

$$R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of differentiation:**

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \cdot \frac{d}{dT}\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the inner derivative:**

$$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] = \beta \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{T^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the chain rule result:**

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_T \cdot \left(-\frac{\beta}{T^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Express as Sensitivity (with negative sign indicating NTC):**

$$\boxed{S_{Th} = \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} R_T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign confirms the **NTC behavior** — resistance *decreases* as temperature *increases*. Crucially, $S_{Th}$ is **temperature-dependent** (inversely proportional to $T^2$), which is the root cause of strong non-linearity. Sensitivity at $T = 298\ \text{K}$ ($\beta = 4000$, $R_T = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$):

$$S_{Th} = -\frac{4000}{(298)^2} \times 10000 \approx -450\ \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$$

This is over **1000× more sensitive** than the PT100 RTD.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linearization of Thermistor Using Parallel Resistor

A common lab technique to obtain an approximately linear response from a thermistor over a small range is to place a fixed resistor $R_P$ in parallel.

**Step 1 — The parallel combination:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_T \cdot R_P}{R_T + R_P}$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse for differentiation convenience:**

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_T} + \frac{1}{R_P}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:**

$$-\frac{1}{R_{eq}^2}\frac{dR_{eq}}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R_T^2}\frac{dR_T}{dT} + 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $dR_{eq}/dT$:**

$$\frac{dR_{eq}}{dT} = \left(\frac{R_{eq}}{R_T}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dR_{eq}}{dT} = \left(\frac{R_P}{R_T + R_P}\right)^2 \cdot S_{Th}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The square factor $(R_P / (R_T + R_P))^2$ is slowly varying with temperature, providing quasi-linearity. This is the foundation of thermistor-based linear temperature transducers.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}$. Find its resistance at $T = 80^{\circ}\text{C}$ using both linear and quadratic models. Given $\beta = -5.85 \times 10^{-7}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}^2$.

**Solution using Linear Model:**

$$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T) = 100 \left(1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 80\right)$$

$$R_T = 100 \left(1 + 0.308\right) = 100 \times 1.308$$

$$\boxed{R_{T,\text{linear}} = 130.8\ \Omega}$$

**Solution using Quadratic Model:**

$$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$$

$$R_T = 100 \left(1 + 0.308 + (-5.85 \times 10^{-7}) \times 6400\right)$$

$$R_T = 100 \left(1 + 0.308 - 0.003744\right)$$

$$R_T = 100 \times 1.304256$$

$$\boxed{R_{T,\text{quadratic}} = 130.43\ \Omega}$$

**Error introduced by linear approximation:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{130.8 - 130.43}{130.43} \times 100 = 0.284\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quadratic correction is small at $80^{\circ}\text{C}$ but becomes significant at extremes (e.g., at $600^{\circ}\text{C}$ it is over $4\%$), which is why industrial-grade RTD instruments use the full Callendar-Van Dusen model.

## 3.5 Experimental Data Analysis Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Lab Procedure (10 Steps):**
>
> 1. Note the RTD/thermistor reference resistance $R_0$ and $\alpha$/$\beta$ value.
> 2. Connect the device in a Wheatstone bridge or use a digital ohmmeter.
> 3. Immerse the sensor in a temperature-controlled water bath or oil bath.
> 4. Use a calibrated mercury-in-glass thermometer (or PT100 reference) for true temperature.
> 5. Allow thermal equilibrium ($\geq 3$ minutes) at each set point.
> 6. Record resistance at temperature increments of $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ from $30^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$.
> 7. Tabulate $T$ (°C) vs $R$ (Ω).
> 8. Plot $R$ vs $T$ (linear scale for RTD; semi-log $R$ vs $1/T$ for thermistor).
> 9. Compute sensitivity = slope of best-fit line.
> 10. Compare with theoretical $\alpha R_0$ (RTD) or $-\beta R_T / T^2$ (Thermistor).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermistor/RTD Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heater + Stirrer] --> B[Water/Oil Bath]
    B --> C[Reference Thermometer]
    B --> D[Thermistor Sensor]
    B --> E[RTD PT100 Sensor]
    D --> F[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Signal Amplifier]
    G --> H[ADC and Microcontroller]
    H --> I[Digital Display]
    F --> J[Digital Ohmmeter / DMM]
    J --> K[Data Acquisition and PC]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Graph Plotting R vs T]
    K --> M[Slope and Sensitivity Calculation]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T1[Thermal Domain]
        A1[Process Temperature T]
    end
    subgraph T2[Transduction Domain]
        A2[Sensor Element: RTD or Thermistor]
        A3[Resistance R T changes]
    end
    subgraph T3[Signal Conditioning Domain]
        A4[Wheatstone Bridge Excitation]
        A5[Differential Amplifier]
        A6[Linearization and Cold Junction Comp]
    end
    subgraph T4[Output Domain]
        A7[ADC Conversion]
        A8[Digital Readout or 4-20 mA Output]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flowchart — RTD vs Thermistor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Temperature Sensor] --> B{Temperature Range Required}
    B -->|Wide minus 200 to 850 C| C[Use RTD - PT100]
    B -->|Narrow minus 55 to 150 C| D{Sensitivity Priority}
    D -->|Very High Sensitivity Needed| E[Use NTC Thermistor]
    D -->|Linearity Priority| F[Use RTD - PT100]
    C --> G[Final Selection]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Calibrate and Test in Lab]
```

## 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge for RTD Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vex DC Excitation] --> R1[R1 Fixed]
    V --> R2[R2 Fixed]
    R1 --> N1[Node A]
    R2 --> N3[Node B]
    N1 --> R3[R3 Fixed]
    N3 --> RTD[RTD PT100]
    R3 --> G1[Ground]
    RTD --> G1
    N1 --> AMP[Diff Amplifier Input 1]
    N3 --> AMP2[Diff Amplifier Input 2]
    AMP --> AMP2
    AMP2 --> OUT[Vout Proportional to delta T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheatstone Bridge Operating Principle:** When the bridge is balanced (no temperature change), $V_{out} = 0$. As temperature changes, $R_{RTD}$ changes, unbalancing the bridge and producing a small differential voltage proportional to $\Delta T$. This is the standard instrumentation technique used in KTU lab experiments for high-precision temperature measurement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Temperature Coefficient of Resistance" for an RTD. What is its standard value for a Platinum RTD?**

**Model Answer:**

The **temperature coefficient of resistance ($\alpha$)** is defined as the fractional change in resistance per degree change in temperature, measured between $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $100^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}\quad \text{(units: } /^{\circ}\text{C)}$$

For a standard Platinum RTD (PT100), the DIN/IEC 60751 standard specifies:

$$\boxed{\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

Higher purity platinum corresponds to $\alpha = 3.926 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ITS-90 reference).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors. Give one example application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Full form | Negative Temperature Coefficient | Positive Temperature Coefficient |
| $R$ vs $T$ | Resistance decreases with $\uparrow T$ | Resistance increases with $\uparrow T$ |
| Material | Metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co, Fe) | Barium titanate doped |
| Application | Inrush current limiter, medical probes | Motor protection, self-resetting fuses |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD). Discuss its advantages over thermocouples and thermistors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}$.
   (i) Calculate the resistance at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ using the linear approximation.
   (ii) If the same RTD is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms equal to $100\ \Omega$ and excitation voltage $V = 5\ \text{V}$, find the output voltage at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of RTD:**

- **Construction:** A PT100 RTD consists of a fine platinum wire (typically $0.05$ mm diameter) wound in a helical coil on a ceramic or glass bobbin. The coil is protected by a stainless-steel sheath filled with ceramic powder for vibration resistance and thermal conductivity.

- **Working Principle:** As the temperature of the RTD changes, the vibration amplitude of platinum atoms in the lattice increases. This causes increased electron scattering, raising the electrical resistance nearly linearly with temperature.

- **Advantages over Thermocouples:** Higher accuracy, better stability, no cold-junction compensation required.

- **Advantages over Thermistors:** Better linearity, wider temperature range, better long-term stability, interchangeable.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Resistance at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$:**

$$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T) = 100 \left(1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 50\right)$$

$$R_T = 100 (1 + 0.1925) = 119.25\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{50^{\circ}\text{C}} = 119.25\ \Omega}$$

**(ii) Bridge Output Voltage:**

The Wheatstone bridge with three fixed arms of $100\ \Omega$ and the RTD in the fourth arm produces a differential output:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_{RTD}}{R_1 + R_{RTD}} - V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

For a symmetric bridge with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 100\ \Omega$ and $R_{RTD} = 119.25\ \Omega$:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{119.25}{100 + 119.25} - 5 \cdot \frac{100}{100 + 100}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{119.25}{219.25} - 5 \cdot 0.5$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \times 0.5439 - 2.5 = 2.7196 - 2.5$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.2196\ \text{V} \approx 219.6\ \text{mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **linear** and **quadratic** models. Use linear only when explicitly asked.
> 2. Bridge output requires a **loaded or unloaded** assumption. State "assuming ideal high-impedance voltmeter" if not given.
> 3. Unit of $\alpha$ is **per °C** — many students incorrectly omit it.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an NTC thermistor. Derive the expression for its sensitivity. State the limitations of thermistors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $\beta = 4000\ \text{K}$.
   (i) Calculate its resistance at $75^{\circ}\text{C}$.
   (ii) If this thermistor is connected in a voltage divider with a $5.6\ \text{k}\Omega$ series resistor and a $5\ \text{V}$ supply, find the output voltage at $75^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NTC Thermistor Working Principle and Sensitivity Derivation:**

**Working Principle:** NTC thermistors are made from sintered mixtures of metal oxides (Mn$_3$O$_4$, NiO, CoO, Fe$_2$O$_3$). At low temperatures, very few charge carriers are thermally excited across the semiconductor band gap. As temperature rises, exponentially more carriers become available, sharply increasing conductivity and decreasing resistance.

**Sensitivity Derivation:**

Starting from the Beta equation:

$$R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ using the chain rule:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_T \cdot \left(-\frac{\beta}{T^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{S_{Th} = \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} R_T}$$

**Limitations:**
1. Highly non-linear response — requires linearization.
2. Limited temperature range (typically $<150^{\circ}\text{C}$).
3. Self-heating errors at high measurement currents.
4. Less stable and less interchangeable than RTDs.
5. Fragile — susceptible to thermal shock and mechanical damage.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Resistance at $75^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($T = 348.15\ \text{K}$, $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$):**

$$R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{348.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.0028724 - 0.0033540 = -0.0004816\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Exponent value:

$$\beta \times (-0.0004816) = 4000 \times (-0.0004816) = -1.9264$$

Now compute:

$$R_T = 10000 \times e^{-1.9264} = 10000 \times 0.14555$$

$$\boxed{R_{75^{\circ}\text{C}} \approx 1455.5\ \Omega \approx 1.46\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

**(ii) Voltage Divider Output:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_S}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{1455.5}{1455.5 + 5600}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{1455.5}{7055.5} = 5 \times 0.2063$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 1.031\ \text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting Kelvin:** The Beta equation **requires** absolute temperature in Kelvin — converting $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $298.15\ \text{K}$ and $75^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $348.15\ \text{K}$ is mandatory.
> 2. **Sign convention:** Many students drop the negative sign in sensitivity. State clearly: "Sensitivity is negative, indicating NTC behavior."
> 3. **Significant figures:** Report answers to at least 3 significant figures for full marks.
> 4. **No units in $e$ exponent:** The term $\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)$ must be dimensionless — verify units cancel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):**
>
> ✅ **RTD (PT100):**
> - $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - Linear model: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$
> - Quadratic model: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$, $\beta \approx -5.85 \times 10^{-7}\ /^{\circ}\text{C}^2$
> - Sensitivity = $0.385\ \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ (constant)
> - Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC behavior)
> - Range: $-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+850^{\circ}\text{C}$
>
> ✅ **NTC Thermistor:**
> - $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ — $T$ in Kelvin!
> - Sensitivity: $S_{Th} = -(\beta/T^2) R_T$ (negative, temperature-dependent)
> - $\beta$ typically $3000$–$5000\ \text{K}$
> - Range: $-55^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+150^{\circ}\text{C}$ (limited)
> - Highly non-linear — linearize using parallel resistor or Steinhart-Hart
> - High sensitivity (1000× greater than RTD) but poor stability
>
> ✅ **Lab Procedure Essentials:**
> - Use Wheatstone bridge or 4-wire Kelvin connection for accuracy
> - Avoid self-heating: keep excitation current $\leq 1\ \text{mA}$ for PT100
> - Allow thermal equilibrium ($\geq 3$ min) before recording
> - Plot $R$ vs $T$ for RTD (linear); $R$ vs $1/T$ (semi-log) for thermistor
> - Compute slope = sensitivity, compare with theoretical
>
> ✅ **Comparison Keywords to Use in Answers:**
> - "RTDs offer **better linearity and stability** over a wider range"
> - "Thermistors offer **higher sensitivity** in a **narrower range** with strong non-linearity"
> - "PT100 is the **industrial standard** for platinum RTDs"
> - "The Callendar-Van Dusen equation provides **higher accuracy** at temperature extremes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Viva Questions Asked in KTU Lab Exam:**
> 1. Why is Platinum used as the sensing element in RTDs?
> 2. What is the difference between 2-wire, 3-wire, and 4-wire RTD connections?
> 3. Why are thermistors more sensitive than RTDs?
> 4. What is self-heating error and how do you minimize it?
> 5. Explain the term "interchangeability" with reference to RTDs.
> 6. Why is the Wheatstone bridge preferred over direct ohmmeter reading for RTDs?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Thermocouple
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Thermocouple

# 🧪 Characteristics of Thermocouple — Lab Module Experiment 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Thermocouple — Definition (KTU Standard)**
> A **thermocouple** is a *passive, thermoelectric temperature transducer* consisting of two dissimilar conductors (thermoelements) joined at one end to form a **measuring (hot) junction**, while the other ends are connected to a measuring instrument forming the **reference (cold) junction**. It converts a temperature difference between the two junctions into a measurable **Seebeck EMF**, in accordance with the **Seebeck Effect (1821)**.

The functional relationship governing the generated EMF is given by the **Seebeck equation**:

$$E_{AB}(T, T_{ref}) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$

Where:
- $E_{AB}$ → Generated thermoelectric EMF in **millivolts (mV)**
- $S_{AB}(T)$ → **Seebeck Coefficient** (Sensitivity) in **μV/°C**
- $T$ → Temperature at hot junction in **°C** (or K)
- $T_{ref}$ → Temperature at cold junction in **°C** (or K)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Friend Thermometer"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine two friends (Metal A and Metal B) standing at a doorway. When the room on one side is hot, Friend A gets energetic and pushes forward, while Friend B stays calm. The difference in their "push" creates a tiny voltage — exactly like a thermocouple generating EMF due to the *temperature difference* across two dissimilar metals. The **hot junction** is the heated doorway, and the **cold junction** is the cooler end where we measure the push (EMF) with a voltmeter.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

> [!NOTE] **Key Standards & Constants**
> - **Standard Reference Temperature (Ice Point):** $T_{ref} = 0\,°C = 273.15\,K$
> - **Seebeck Coefficient of K-Type (Chromel-Alumel):** $S \approx 41\,\mu V/°C$ at **25 °C**
> - **Seebeck Coefficient of J-Type (Iron-Constantan):** $S \approx 51\,\mu V/°C$ at **25 °C**
> - **Seebeck Coefficient of T-Type (Copper-Constantan):** $S \approx 40\,\mu V/°C$ at **25 °C**
> - **NIST Standard Reference Tables (ITS-90)** are used for thermocouple calibration in laboratories.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Junction Formation:** Two wires of dissimilar metals (e.g., **Chromel** and **Alumel** for K-type) are welded, brazed, or twisted at one end to form the **hot (measuring) junction**.
2. **Temperature Exposure:** The hot junction is exposed to the process whose temperature $T$ is to be measured.
3. **Cold Junction Maintenance:** The open ends are held at a stable reference temperature $T_{ref}$ (commonly $0\,°C$ ice bath in lab, or electronically compensated in industrial setups).
4. **Seebeck EMF Generation:** Due to the temperature gradient, charge carriers (electrons/holes) diffuse from hot to cold ends at different rates in each metal. This creates a net contact potential and a thermoelectric EMF.
5. **EMF Measurement:** A high-impedance **millivoltmeter** or **digital temperature indicator (DTI)** measures the generated EMF without loading the circuit.
6. **Calibration Lookup:** The measured EMF is converted to temperature using **standard thermocouple reference tables (ITS-90)**.

### 2.2 Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Law | Statement | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Law of Homogeneous Circuit** | An EMF is generated only by a temperature difference across *dissimilar* metals — not by a uniform temperature gradient in a homogeneous wire. | Allows use of extension wires and connectors of intermediate materials without introducing spurious EMFs. |
| 2 | **Law of Intermediate Metals** | Inserting a third metal into a thermocouple circuit does not affect the EMF, provided its two junctions are at the *same temperature*. | Permits the use of solder, copper lead wires, and measuring instruments in the circuit. |
| 3 | **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** | $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$ | Allows calibration at one temperature and correction/extension for another. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| Formula | Description | Units | Lab Application |
|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear approximation of Seebeck EMF | mV | Used when $S$ is approximately constant |
| $S = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta T}$ | Sensitivity (Seebeck Coefficient) | μV/°C | Calculated from graph slope |
| $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$ | Temperature gradient | °C | Driving quantity for EMF |
| $\% \text{Linearity Error} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{ideal}}{E_{FS}} \times 100$ | Non-linearity deviation | % | Measures transducer linearity |
| $\% \text{Hysteresis} = \dfrac{E_{heat} - E_{cool}}{E_{FS}} \times 100$ | Heating vs cooling EMF difference | % | Quantifies repeatability |
| $T_{actual} = T_{measured} + CJC_{correction}$ | Cold Junction Compensation | °C | Required for practical field use |

> **Note:** The table uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values to preserve markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial Furnaces & Boilers:** Continuous high-temperature monitoring (up to **1260 °C** for K-type).
- **Aircraft Engine Exhaust (Jet Engines):** Type K & Type S thermocouples.
- **HVAC Systems:** Air temperature measurement.
- **Nuclear Reactors:** WRe/W thermocouples for harsh radiation environments.
- **Food & Pharmaceutical Industry:** Sanitary temperature probes (T-type).
- **Automotive Exhaust Systems:** Lambda sensor integration.
- **Cryogenics & Refrigeration:** T-type for low-temperature applications.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Seebeck EMF (Full Steps)

Starting from the basic definition of the **thermoelectric effect**, the EMF generated by two dissimilar metals A and B is derived as follows.

**Step 1: Define the Peltier and Thomson Effects**

The total EMF in a thermocouple loop is the sum of the Peltier EMFs at the two junctions and the Thomson EMFs along the conductors.

$$E_{AB} = (\pi_{AB})_T - (\pi_{AB})_{T_{ref}} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left( \sigma_A - \sigma_B \right) dT$$

Where:
- $\pi_{AB}$ → Peltier coefficient at a junction (V)
- $\sigma_A, \sigma_B$ → Thomson coefficients of metals A and B (V/K)

**Step 2: Apply the Kelvin Relation**

Kelvin derived the relationship between Peltier and Seebeck coefficients using thermodynamics:

$$\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$E_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB} - T_{ref} \cdot S_{AB} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left( \sigma_A - \sigma_B \right) dT$$

**Step 3: Combine Terms**

$$\boxed{E_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} S_{AB}(T)\, dT}$$

This is the **fundamental Seebeck integral** governing thermocouple operation.

**Step 4: Linear Approximation for Small Temperature Ranges**

For lab experiments where $S_{AB}(T)$ is approximately constant (e.g., **0 °C to 100 °C** for a water bath experiment):

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$

For $T_{ref} = 0\,°C$:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot T$$

This is the equation students plot during the lab — a straight line through the origin with slope $S_{AB}$.

### 3.2 Sample Numerical Calculation (KTU Model)

> **Given:** A K-type thermocouple with cold junction at $T_{ref} = 0\,°C$. The following readings were obtained during the experiment:

| Observation # | Hot Junction Temperature $T$ (°C) | Measured EMF $E$ (mV) |
|--------------:|:----------------------------------:|:----------------------:|
| 1 | 30  | 1.203 |
| 2 | 40  | 1.612 |
| 3 | 50  | 2.023 |
| 4 | 60  | 2.436 |
| 5 | 70  | 2.851 |
| 6 | 80  | 3.268 |
| 7 | 90  | 3.685 |
| 8 | 100 | 4.096 |

**Calculation of Average Sensitivity (ΔEMF/ΔT):**

Using observation 1 and observation 8:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T} = \frac{E_8 - E_1}{T_8 - T_1}$$

$$S_{avg} = \frac{4.096 - 1.203}{100 - 30} = \frac{2.893}{70}$$

$$S_{avg} \approx 0.04133\, mV/°C = 41.33\,\mu V/°C$$

**Verification with K-Type Standard:** The standard Seebeck coefficient for K-type at **100 °C** is approximately **41.37 μV/°C**. Our experimental value is within **0.1 %** — excellent agreement! ✅

**Linearity Check (using Least-Squares Method):**

The slope of the best-fit line $E = m \cdot T + c$ is calculated as:

$$m = \frac{N \sum T_i E_i - \sum T_i \sum E_i}{N \sum T_i^2 - \left( \sum T_i \right)^2}$$

With $N = 8$ observations and substituting the tabulated values, the slope $m \approx 0.0413\,mV/°C$, confirming a highly linear response.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Thermocouple Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Temperature T_hot] --> B[Hot Junction / Measuring Junction]
    B --> C[Thermocouple Wires - Metal A and Metal B]
    C --> D[Cold Junction / Reference Junction at T_ref]
    D --> E[Ice Bath 0 deg C or Electronic CJC]
    E --> F[Millivoltmeter or Digital Temperature Indicator]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    G --> H[ADC and Microcontroller]
    H --> I[Display Unit or Data Acquisition System]
    
    J[Seebeck Effect] -.-> B
    K[Law of Intermediate Metals] -.-> C
    L[Law of Intermediate Temperatures] -.-> D
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#5F9EA0,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Expected Calibration Graph (Temperature vs EMF)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HeatingCurve["HEATING CURVE - Forward Path"]
        H1[30 deg C, 1.20 mV] --> H2[50 deg C, 2.02 mV]
        H2 --> H3[70 deg C, 2.85 mV]
        H3 --> H4[90 deg C, 3.69 mV]
        H4 --> H5[100 deg C, 4.10 mV]
    end
    
    subgraph CoolingCurve["COOLING CURVE - Reverse Path"]
        C1[100 deg C, 4.10 mV] --> C2[80 deg C, 3.27 mV]
        C2 --> C3[60 deg C, 2.44 mV]
        C3 --> C4[40 deg C, 1.61 mV]
        C4 --> C5[30 deg C, 1.21 mV]
    end
    
    H5 -.transitions to.-> C1
    
    style H1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style H2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style H3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style H4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style H5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style C1 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
    style C2 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
    style C3 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
    style C4 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
    style C5 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B
```

### 4.3 Circuit Schematic — Lab Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HotZone["HOT ZONE - Heating Mantle or Water Bath"]
        TCJ[Thermocouple Hot Junction]
        HTR[Heating Element with Variac Control]
        ST[Standard Mercury Thermometer for Reference]
    end
    
    subgraph ColdZone["COLD ZONE - Reference Ice Bath"]
        REF[Cold Junction in Ice Filled Dewar Flask]
        ICE[Crushed Ice + Water Mixture at 0 deg C]
    end
    
    subgraph MeasurementZone["MEASUREMENT ZONE"]
        MV[Millivoltmeter or DTI]
        SW[Selector Switch for Multiple Thermocouples]
    end
    
    TCJ -->|Metal A Wire| MV
    TCJ -->|Metal B Wire| MV
    REF --> MV
    ST -->|Reference Temperature Reading| OBSERVER[Student Observer]
    MV -->|EMF Reading in mV| OBSERVER
    
    style TCJ fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style REF fill:#00CED1,stroke:#00688B,stroke-width:2px
    style MV fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    style OBSERVER fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Seebeck Effect. Mention any two laws of thermocouples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Seebeck Effect (1 Mark):** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form two junctions and these junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an electromotive force (EMF) is generated in the circuit. This phenomenon is known as the Seebeck Effect, discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in **1821**.

**Two Laws (2 Marks — 1 Mark each):**

1. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** The insertion of a third metal into a thermocouple circuit will not affect the generated EMF, provided that the two junctions formed by the third metal are at the *same temperature*. This law permits the use of measuring instruments, solder joints, and copper connecting wires.

2. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ is the algebraic sum of the EMFs corresponding to junctions at $T_1, T_2$ and $T_2, T_3$. Mathematically:

$$E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a thermocouple? List any four types of thermocouples with their temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A thermocouple is a temperature-measuring device consisting of two dissimilar metal wires joined at one end (hot junction) and connected to a measuring instrument at the other end (cold junction), based on the Seebeck Effect.

**Four Types (2 Marks — 0.5 Mark each):**

| Type | Metals | Range (°C) |
|------|--------|:----------:|
| **K** | Chromel–Alumel | −200 to **1260** |
| **J** | Iron–Constantan | 0 to **760** |
| **T** | Copper–Constantan | −200 to **370** |
| **S** | Platinum–Platinum/Rhodium (10 %) | 0 to **1480** |

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a thermocouple. State the Seebeck Effect and derive the relationship between EMF and temperature gradient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the experimental procedure to obtain the calibration curve (Temperature vs EMF) of a K-type thermocouple in the range **0 °C to 100 °C**. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be taken. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle & Derivation (7 Marks):**

A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires (say A and B) welded at one end to form the **hot (measuring) junction** at temperature $T$, while the other ends are maintained at a **cold (reference) junction** at temperature $T_{ref}$, typically **0 °C** in a laboratory ice bath.

**(1 Mark)** — **Seebeck Effect Statement:**
When two dissimilar metals are joined and the junctions are kept at different temperatures, a continuous current flows in the circuit, indicating the generation of an EMF. This is the Seebeck Effect (1821).

**(2 Marks)** — **Block Diagram Explanation:**
The block diagram (refer Section 4.1) shows the hot junction exposed to the process, the thermocouple wires conducting the EMF to the cold junction maintained at 0 °C in an ice bath, and the millivoltmeter displaying the generated EMF. The signal is conditioned, digitized, and displayed.

**(4 Marks)** — **Derivation:**

The thermoelectric EMF arises from two fundamental effects:

**Step 1 — Peltier Effect at junctions:**

$$E_{Peltier} = \pi_{AB}(T) - \pi_{AB}(T_{ref})$$

**Step 2 — Thomson Effect along conductors:**

$$E_{Thomson} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left( \sigma_A - \sigma_B \right) dT$$

**Step 3 — Total EMF:**

$$E_{AB} = \pi_{AB}(T) - \pi_{AB}(T_{ref}) + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left( \sigma_A - \sigma_B \right) dT$$

**Step 4 — Applying Kelvin Relation $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$:**

$$E_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB} - T_{ref} \cdot S_{AB} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left( \sigma_A - \sigma_B \right) dT$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{E_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} S_{AB}(T)\, dT}$$

For small temperature ranges where $S_{AB}$ is nearly constant:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$

This linear relationship is verified experimentally by plotting **Temperature vs EMF**.

---

**(b) Experimental Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Required (2 Marks):**
1. K-type thermocouple (Chromel-Alumel)
2. Heating mantle with **variac** (variable autotransformer)
3. Standard mercury thermometer (0 °C to 110 °C, accuracy ±0.5 °C)
4. Digital millivoltmeter / DTI (range 0–10 mV, resolution 0.01 mV)
5. Ice bath setup (Dewar flask, crushed ice, distilled water)
6. Connecting copper wires, beaker, stirrer, tongs

**Procedure (3 Marks):**

1. Set up the **ice bath** with crushed ice and distilled water in the Dewar flask. Insert the cold junction of the thermocouple into the ice bath. Verify $T_{ref} = 0\,°C$ with the standard thermometer.
2. Connect the cold junction leads to the millivoltmeter through copper extension wires.
3. Immerse the hot junction and the standard thermometer in the water bath simultaneously, ensuring they are at the same depth.
4. Initially, both junctions are at room temperature. Record the room temperature and corresponding EMF.
5. Slowly increase the variac voltage to heat the water bath in steps of approximately **10 °C**.
6. At each step, stir the water thoroughly, wait for thermal equilibrium (~2 minutes), record the thermometer reading ($T$) and the corresponding EMF ($E$).
7. Continue up to **100 °C** (boiling point of water at 1 atm).
8. **Reverse procedure:** Allow the water bath to cool naturally in steps of 10 °C, recording EMF readings during cooling to study **hysteresis**.
9. Plot the calibration graph: Temperature ($T$) on X-axis vs EMF ($E$) on Y-axis.
10. Determine the **sensitivity (Seebeck coefficient)** from the slope of the best-fit line.

**Precautions (2 Marks):**
1. The cold junction must be fully immersed in the ice bath — incomplete immersion causes reference temperature errors.
2. The hot junction and standard thermometer must be at the same depth for accurate comparison.
3. Stir water continuously to avoid temperature gradients.
4. Use **high-impedance** voltmeter to avoid loading effects.
5. Allow sufficient time for thermal equilibrium before recording readings.
6. Use proper shielding to avoid **thermoelectric EMFs** from stray junctions.
7. Avoid contamination of the thermocouple junction by oxidation or mechanical stress.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Law of Intermediate Metals** and the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** with suitable examples. How are these laws useful in practical thermocouple measurements? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A K-type thermocouple with cold junction at **0 °C** produces an EMF of **4.096 mV** at **100 °C** and **1.203 mV** at **30 °C**. Calculate: **(i)** the average Seebeck coefficient, **(ii)** the expected EMF at **75 °C**, and **(iii)** the percentage linearity error if the actual reading at 75 °C is **3.05 mV** (ideal reference value = 3.075 mV). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Laws of Thermocouples (7 Marks):**

**Law of Intermediate Metals — 3 Marks:**

*Statement:* The EMF developed in a thermocouple circuit is independent of any additional metal(s) introduced into the circuit, provided that the two junctions formed by the intermediate metal are at the *same temperature*.

*Mathematical Expression:*

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AC}(T_1, T_2) + E_{CB}(T_1, T_2)$$

where the intermediate metal is C, and its two junctions with A and B are both at the same temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.

*Example:* A copper wire connected between the Chromel wire and the millivoltmeter terminal does not introduce any error because both copper-Chromel junctions are at the same temperature (room temperature).

*Practical Significance:* This law allows the use of **extension wires**, **terminal blocks**, **soldered joints**, and **measuring instruments** in thermocouple circuits without compensation.

**Law of Intermediate Temperatures — 3 Marks:**

*Statement:* If a thermocouple produces EMF $E_1$ between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, and EMF $E_2$ between $T_2$ and $T_3$, then the EMF between $T_1$ and $T_3$ is:

$$E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$$

*Example:* If a K-type thermocouple produces:
- $E(0, 100) = 4.096$ mV
- $E(100, 200) = 4.359$ mV

Then:

$$E(0, 200) = 4.096 + 4.359 = 8.455\,mV$$

*Practical Significance:* This law enables the use of **reference tables calibrated at 0 °C** even when the cold junction is at a different reference temperature. It forms the basis of **software-based reference junction compensation**.

**Practical Importance (1 Mark):** These laws are the theoretical foundation of cold junction compensation techniques used in industrial temperature transmitters and software linearization algorithms.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $E_1 = 1.203\,mV$ at $T_1 = 30\,°C$
- $E_2 = 4.096\,mV$ at $T_2 = 100\,°C$
- $T_{ref} = 0\,°C$
- Actual EMF at 75 °C: $E_{actual} = 3.05\,mV$
- Ideal EMF at 75 °C: $E_{ideal} = 3.075\,mV$
- Full-scale EMF: $E_{FS} = 4.096\,mV$ (from 0 to 100 °C)

**(i) Average Seebeck Coefficient — 3 Marks:**

$$S_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$S_{avg} = \frac{4.096 - 1.203}{100 - 30} = \frac{2.893}{70}$$

$$\boxed{S_{avg} = 0.04133\,mV/°C = 41.33\,\mu V/°C}$$

This closely matches the **standard K-type Seebeck coefficient of 41.37 μV/°C** at 100 °C.

**(ii) Expected EMF at 75 °C — 2 Marks:**

$$E_{75} = S_{avg} \cdot (T - T_{ref}) = 0.04133 \times (75 - 0)$$

$$\boxed{E_{75} = 3.0998\,mV \approx 3.10\,mV}$$

**(iii) Percentage Linearity Error — 2 Marks:**

$$\% \text{ Linearity Error} = \frac{\vert E_{actual} - E_{ideal} \vert}{E_{FS}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Linearity Error} = \frac{\vert 3.05 - 3.075 \vert}{4.096} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Linearity Error} = \frac{0.025}{4.096} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Linearity Error} \approx 0.610\,\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 Marks):** Students often forget that for Seebeck EMF, the cold junction temperature $T_{ref}$ must be **subtracted**, not added. Always use $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$.
>
> 2. **Wrong Reference Point (–2 Marks):** Do NOT assume the EMF is zero at room temperature. EMF is zero only when $T_{hot} = T_{cold} = T_{ref}$. Many students plot graphs from room temperature, losing 2 marks for an incorrect intercept.
>
> 3. **Units Confusion (–1 Mark):** Seebeck coefficient $S$ is in **μV/°C** when expressed physically, but in **mV/°C** for lab calculations. Convert properly: $1\,mV = 1000\,\mu V$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Cold Junction Compensation (–2 Marks):** In viva questions, if you don't mention CJC explicitly, you will lose marks. Always state: *"The cold junction is maintained at 0 °C using an ice bath."*
>
> 5. **Wrong Use of Linearity Formula (–2 Marks):** Linearity error uses **Full-Scale output ($E_{FS}$)**, NOT the reading itself. Using $E_{actual}$ in the denominator is a frequent and costly mistake.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Thermocouple**

- ⭐ **Thermocouple** = Two dissimilar metals joined at a hot junction; passive thermoelectric transducer based on the **Seebeck Effect (1821)**.
- ⭐ **EMF Equation:** $E_{AB} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} S_{AB}(T)\, dT \approx S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$
- ⭐ **Sensitivity (Seebeck Coefficient):** Calculated from the **slope** of the Temperature vs EMF calibration curve. For K-type: $\approx 41\,\mu V/°C$.
- ⭐ **Three Fundamental Laws:**
  - Law of Homogeneous Circuit
  - Law of Intermediate Metals
  - Law of Intermediate Temperatures
- ⭐ **Reference Junction:** Must be held at a **known, stable temperature** — typically **0 °C ice bath** in lab setups or **electronic cold junction compensation (CJC)** in industrial instruments.
- ⭐ **Calibration Curve:** A **straight line** through origin (when $T_{ref} = 0\,°C$) — high linearity is a key advantage of thermocouples.
- ⭐ **Hysteresis Study:** Always record EMF during **heating AND cooling** to determine % hysteresis.
- ⭐ **High-Impedance Voltmeter:** Always use a **millivoltmeter with high input impedance** (≥10 MΩ) to avoid circuit loading.
- ⭐ **Apparatus Memorization:** Thermocouple + Ice bath + Heating mantle + Standard thermometer + Millivoltmeter + Variac + Stirrer.
- ⭐ **Precautions:** Complete ice immersion · Same depth for hot junction and thermometer · Continuous stirring · Thermal equilibrium wait · Avoid contamination.
- ⭐ **Standard Tables:** Always reference **ITS-90 thermocouple tables** for converting mV to °C during analysis.
- ⭐ **K-Type is the most common** lab thermocouple: Chromel-Alumel, range −200 to **1260 °C**, sensitivity ~41 μV/°C.
- ⭐ **Industrial Applications:** Furnaces, jet engines, HVAC, automotive exhaust, nuclear reactors, cryogenics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Speed using Opto Coupler/Stroboscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Speed using Opto Coupler/Stroboscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Speed Measurement** is the quantification of the rotational (angular) or linear velocity of a moving body. In laboratory practice, two prominent non-contact optical methods are employed:

- **Opto-Coupler (Slotted Optical Switch / Photo-Interrupter):** A non-contact electro-optical transducer in which a light beam emitted by an LED is interrupted or reflected by a slotted/perforated disc coupled to the rotating shaft. The photodetector on the receiving side generates a train of electrical pulses whose frequency is directly proportional to the rotational speed of the shaft.

- **Stroboscope:** An instrument that emits precisely controlled, high-intensity, short-duration flashes of light at an adjustable known frequency. When the flash frequency is made equal to (or an integer multiple/fraction of) the rotational frequency of a moving object, the object appears optically **stationary** to the human eye due to the *Persistence of Vision* effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL307 – Experiment 2):**
> Students are expected to **set up the rig, vary the motor speed, observe the output waveform on a CRO/DSO, count pulses per unit time, and compute RPM**. Graphical plotting of *Applied Voltage vs. Measured Speed* is a compulsory viva question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Opto-Coupler Analogy — "The Coin-Tunnel Turnstile":**
Imagine a turnstile at a metro station that has exactly **four rotating arms**. Every time a passenger pushes through, the turnstile produces a single *"click"* sound. If you stand with a stopwatch and count **40 clicks in 20 seconds**, you know the turnstile rotated 40/4 = 10 times in 20 s, so its speed is **30 rotations per minute (rpm)**. An opto-coupler works *exactly* the same way: the **light beam is the passenger**, the **slotted disc is the turnstile**, and the **photodetector is your ear counting the clicks**.

**Stroboscope Analogy — "The Flickering Film Projector":**
Have you ever watched a movie scene where a car's wheels appear to spin backward or stand still while the car is moving fast? That happens because the **film frame rate (e.g., 24 frames/s) momentarily matches the wheel's rotation**. A stroboscope is essentially a *controllable movie projector* — by tuning the "frame rate" (flash rate) until the wheel *visually freezes*, the flash rate numerically equals the wheel's true rotation rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Both techniques convert a *mechanical rotation* into an *electrical frequency* (Hz) or *visual perception of stillness*. Frequency is the universal language of measurement in this experiment.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

- **Speed (N):** Measured in **Revolutions Per Minute (rpm)** or **rev/s** in SI.
- **Flash / Pulse Frequency (f):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** where **1 Hz = 1 pulse/s**.
- **Conversion factor:** **60 s = 1 min** (always appears in the rpm formula).
- **Persistence of Vision (Human Eye):** Approximately **1/16 s ≈ 62.5 ms** — the minimum flash interval for the eye to merge consecutive flashes as continuous illumination.
- **Standard DC Motor Specimen (typical lab):** **12 V / 0.5 A, 0–10,000 rpm** range.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Opto-Coupler Speed Measurement

The system is built from **four functional blocks**:

1. **Slotted Disc (Perforated Wheel):** Mechanically coupled to the motor shaft. Contains a known number of slots, denoted **S** (typically 10, 20, 30, 60, or 100 slots).
2. **LED (Light Source):** Emits **infrared (IR) or visible red light** continuously. Usually driven by a constant-current source to avoid intensity drift.
3. **Photodetector (Phototransistor / Photodiode):** Placed coaxially opposite the LED across the slot gap. Output is a **LOW voltage when light passes**, and **HIGH voltage when light is blocked** (or vice-versa, depending on pull-up configuration).
4. **Signal Conditioning + Counter / CRO:** A Schmitt trigger (e.g., IC 7414) cleans the analog pulses into clean **square waves**, which are then fed to a **frequency counter** or visualized on a **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)**.

**Logic Flow (Why it works):**
- As the disc rotates by **one slot pitch**, the photodetector experiences *one light-on* and *one light-off* transition.
- Each complete rotation therefore generates exactly **S pulses**.
- The pulse frequency **f** (in Hz) captured by the counter is proportional to the rotational frequency **n** (in rev/s):

$$f = S \times n$$

where **S** = number of slots and **n** = rotational frequency in rev/s.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Stroboscope

The stroboscope is fundamentally a **calibrated variable-frequency flasher**, often using a **Xenon flash tube** or **high-brightness LED array** triggered by an **astable multivibrator** (e.g., IC 555) or a **microcontroller (Arduino/8051)** in modern rigs.

**Three Working Conditions:**

- **Exact Synchronism (Fundamental):** Flash frequency = Rotational frequency. The object appears *frozen at one position*.
- **Sub-Multiple Synchronism:** Flash frequency = Rotational frequency ÷ k (k = 2, 3, 4…). Object still appears stationary, but observed at every k-th flash. *Examiner loves asking this.*
- **Apparent Backward/Forward Motion:** If the flash frequency is *slightly less* than the rotation frequency, the eye sees *slow backward motion*. If *slightly more*, the eye sees *slow forward motion*. This is the basis for **slip measurement** in textile/printing machines.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Rotational speed in rpm (Opto) | $N = \dfrac{f \times 60}{S}$ | rpm | **f** = pulse frequency in Hz, **S** = number of slots |
| 2 | Rotational frequency in rev/s (Opto) | $n = \dfrac{f}{S}$ | rev/s | Direct output of frequency counter divided by S |
| 3 | Linear speed at disc rim | $v = \pi D \times n = \dfrac{\pi D \times f}{S}$ | m/s | **D** = diameter of slotted disc in metres |
| 4 | Stroboscope speed (fundamental) | $N = f_{flash} \times 60$ | rpm | Single-flash frozen image |
| 5 | Stroboscope speed (sub-multiple) | $N = k \times f_{flash} \times 60$ | rpm | k = integer ≥ 2; always verify with fundamental |
| 6 | Angular velocity | $\omega = 2\pi n = \dfrac{2\pi f}{S}$ | rad/s | Used for torque/power calculations |
| 7 | Percentage error in speed | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert N_{true} - N_{measured} \vert}{N_{true}} \times 100$ | % | Use `\vert` not pipe inside markdown tables |
| 8 | Pulse period on CRO | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | s | Measured peak-to-peak on DSO time-base |
| 9 | Disc resolution (slots/rev) | $S = \dfrac{60 \times f}{N_{rpm}}$ | slots/rev | Used when designing the disc |
| 10 | Maximum countable speed | $N_{max} = \dfrac{f_{counter\,max} \times 60}{S}$ | rpm | Limited by counter bandwidth (typ. 1 MHz) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If the lab manual says the disc has **60 slots**, then each pulse = **1° of rotation**, and **f (in Hz) is numerically equal to rpm ÷ 60** (wait — re-check; actually 60 slots ⇒ 1 rev = 60 pulses ⇒ 1 pulse = 1/60 rev, so rpm = f × 60/60 = f × 1 ⇒ **f in Hz = rpm / 60**). This simplification is a frequent viva trick.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Opto-Coupler:** Used in **CNC spindle feedback**, **computer mouse scroll wheels**, **tachometers in automotive dashboards**, **printer paper-feed encoders**, and **industrial conveyor-belt RPM meters**. Embedded in **incremental rotary encoders** for closed-loop motor control.
- **Stroboscope:** Used in **textile mills** (checking loom spindle speed), **printing presses** (checking print roller synchronisation), **fan-blade balancing**, **helicopter rotor inspection**, and **medical diagnostics** (studying vocal-cord vibration).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Pulse Count to RPM (Opto-Coupler)

**Given:**
- A DC motor drives a slotted disc having **S slots** uniformly distributed on its circumference.
- In a measurement window of **T seconds**, a digital counter records **N_p** pulses.

**Step 1 — Define the pulse rate (frequency):**

The frequency is the number of complete cycles per second. Since **N_p** pulses occur in **T** seconds:

$$f = \dfrac{N_{p}}{T}$$

This is the standard definition of frequency (pulses per second = Hz).

**Step 2 — Convert frequency to revolutions per second:**

Because the disc has **S** slots, one complete revolution produces exactly **S** pulses. Therefore, the number of revolutions executed in time **T** is:

$$n = \dfrac{N_{p}}{S}$$

(equivalently, $n = f / S$).

**Step 3 — Convert revolutions per second to revolutions per minute:**

Since **1 minute = 60 seconds**, multiplying by **60** converts rev/s to rev/min:

$$N = n \times 60 = \dfrac{N_{p} \times 60}{S \times T}$$

> **Final Compact Formula:**

$$\boxed{N \; [\text{rpm}] = \dfrac{60 \, f}{S} = \dfrac{60 \, N_{p}}{S \, T}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A 30-slot disc is coupled to a motor. The frequency counter reads **f = 250 Hz**. Compute the rpm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{60 \times f}{S} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{60 \times 250}{30} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{15\,000}{30} \\[4pt]
  &= 500 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (dimensional check):** Hz = 1/s. So (60 × 1/s)/slots = (1/min)/slots. Multiply by 60/s inside gives rev/min. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stroboscope RPM From Apparent Freeze

**Given:**
- A rotating fan blade has **P identical blades** (this affects the visual freeze, but not the formula for a *marked* point on the disc).
- A tachometer mark (white dot) is painted on the rotor.
- The stroboscope flash frequency is varied until the dot appears **stationary** at frequency **f_1** Hz, and again **stationary** at a higher flash frequency **f_2 = 2 f_1** Hz (single-jump pattern).

**Step 1 — Apply the condition of apparent stationarity:**

For the human eye, the rotor appears stationary if it has rotated by *exactly one full revolution* (or an integer multiple k of it) between two consecutive flashes. Thus the time between flashes equals $k / n$:

$$T_{flash} = \dfrac{k}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Invert to find rotation frequency:**

$$n = \dfrac{k}{T_{flash}} = k \, f_{flash}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to rpm:**

$$N = 60 \, n = 60 \, k \, f_{flash}$$

> **Final Compact Formula:**

$$\boxed{N \; [\text{rpm}] = 60 \, k \, f_{flash}}$$

where **k = 1, 2, 3, …** is the *order* of the apparent freeze (k = 1 ⇒ fundamental; k ≥ 2 ⇒ harmonic/sub-multiple).

**Numerical Example:**
The rotor dot freezes at flash frequencies **f₁ = 50 Hz** and **f₂ = 100 Hz**. The true rpm is the *lowest* corresponding speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{true} &= 60 \times k \times f_1 \quad \text{(with k=1 for the smallest frozen frequency)} \\[4pt]
         &= 60 \times 1 \times 50 \\[4pt]
         &= 3000 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why use the *smallest* frozen frequency?** Because at 100 Hz, the dot could also be frozen if the rotor is doing 6000 rpm with k = 1, or 3000 rpm with k = 2. The smallest k = 1 hypothesis gives the fundamental speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Tip (KTU Board Examiner's favourite):** Always halve the highest freezing flash frequency successively (f, f/2, f/3 …) and pick the **lowest value that still produces a stationary image** as the *true* flash frequency.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Opto-Coupler vs. Stroboscope

| Parameter | Opto-Coupler | Stroboscope |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Principle** | Pulse counting | Visual synchronism via flash |
| **Output** | Electrical square wave | Visual perception (frozen image) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 % (with 60-slot disc + good counter) | ±1–2 % (operator-dependent) |
| **Contact type** | Non-contact (light) | Non-contact (light) |
| **Range** | 1 rpm to 100,000 rpm | 100 rpm to 100,000 rpm |
| **Best suited for** | Continuous, real-time logging | Quick spot checks, inaccessible parts |
| **Display** | Numerical readout (Hz → rpm) | Visual + dial reading |
| **Disadvantage** | Requires slotted disc, alignment | Cannot read below ~100 rpm; needs high-quality flash |
| **Cost** | Low (IC-based) | Moderate (Xenon tube + HV trigger) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Opto-Coupler Tachometer

The physical drawing of a slotted disc is adapted into a **block-level functional architecture** for Mermaid compatibility, showing the *flow of energy and signal* from shaft to display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[DC Power Supply 0 to 12 V] --> MOTOR[DC Shunt Motor]
    MOTOR --> SHAFT[Rotating Shaft]
    SHAFT --> DISC[Slotted Disc with S slots]
    DISC -->|Interrupts IR beam| OPTOSLOT[Opto Coupler U shaped slot]
    LED_SRC[Constant Current LED Driver 20 mA] --> OPTOSLOT
    OPTOSLOT --> COND[Schmitt Trigger SN7414]
    COND --> DSO[Digital Storage Oscilloscope Display]
    COND --> FC[Digital Frequency Counter]
    FC --> RPM[RPM Display Indicator]
    DSO --> RPM
```

**Reading the diagram:** The motor's mechanical rotation mechanically drags the slotted disc. The disc's slots alternately pass and block the IR beam inside the opto-coupler. The phototransistor's analog pulses are squared up by the Schmitt trigger, and simultaneously displayed on the DSO and counted by the frequency counter, which finally computes and displays rpm.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Stroboscope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VCO[Variable Frequency Oscillator IC 555 Astable] --> TRG[Trigger and Driver Circuit]
    TRG --> XF[Xenon Flash Tube or LED Array]
    XF -->|Flashing light| ROTOR[Rotating Rotor with Marked Dot]
    ROTOR -->|Reflected light| EYE[Human Eye Persistence of Vision]
    EYE -->|Perceived Stationary| OBS[Observer adjusts f until freeze]
    OBS -->|Reads f from dial| DIAL[Calibrated Flash Frequency Dial]
    DIAL --> RPMCALC[RPM Calculator N equals 60 times f times k]
    VCO -.->|Same f| DIAL
```

**Reading the diagram:** The user rotates the calibrated dial, which changes the astable oscillator's frequency. Each output pulse triggers a Xenon flash. When the flash rate matches the rotor's rotation rate, the eye perceives the marked dot as stationary, and the rpm is read directly from the calibrated dial.

---

### 4.3 Signal Waveform Schematic (Square-Wave Output of Opto-Coupler)

Although a Mermaid node cannot draw a true oscilloscope trace, the following *state-machine flow* represents the pulse-train evolution from the photodetector and its cleaned-up Schmitt-trigger output:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LightPasses: Slot aligns with beam
    LightPasses --> LightBlocked: Disc rotates by half slot pitch
    LightBlocked --> LightPasses: Next slot aligns
    LightPasses: Logic LOW at Schmitt output
    LightBlocked: Logic HIGH at Schmitt output
    note right of LightPasses: Period T equals 1 over f
    note left of LightBlocked: One full rev yields S such transitions
```

Each *HIGH → LOW → HIGH* transition corresponds to **one slot passing**, and the time-period **T** between two identical transitions is the **inverse of frequency f**. The DSO displays this as a clean square wave.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define an opto-coupler. State its use in non-contact speed measurement of a rotating shaft.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **opto-coupler** is an electro-optical transducer consisting of a **light source (LED)** and a **photodetector (phototransistor or photodiode)** housed in a single package, separated by a gap through which a slotted disc can pass.

**Use in speed measurement (2 marks):**
A slotted disc with **S** slots, mechanically coupled to the rotating shaft, is placed in the opto-coupler's slot. As the shaft rotates, the slots alternately allow and block the light beam, producing a **pulse train** at the photodetector output. The **pulse frequency f** is measured with a frequency counter, and the speed is computed as:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \, f}{S} \; \text{rpm}$$

Because there is no mechanical contact between the disc and the opto-coupler, the measurement is **non-contact, frictionless, and highly reliable** for long-term use.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple Paper]**
What is a stroboscope? How does it use the **persistence of vision** principle to measure the speed of a rotating object?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stroboscope** is an instrument that produces **precisely timed, short-duration flashes of light** at a calibrated, variable frequency **f**, used to make a rotating object appear stationary.

**Persistence of vision principle (2 marks):**
The human eye retains an image for approximately **1/16 s (62.5 ms)** after the stimulus is removed. If a rotor spinning at frequency **n** rev/s is illuminated by flashes at exactly the same frequency, then between two consecutive flashes the rotor completes *one full revolution*, returning to the same position. The eye thus integrates the flashes and perceives the rotor as **optically frozen**. At this instant, **f = n**, and the speed is:

$$N = 60 \, f \; \text{rpm}$$

If **f is slightly less than n**, the rotor appears to rotate *slowly backward*; if **f is slightly more than n**, it appears to rotate *slowly forward*, which can also be used for slip analysis.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice between A and B; 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

**Q.3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **opto-electronic tachometer** using a slotted disc. Derive the expression for rpm in terms of the measured pulse frequency and the number of slots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, a **40-slot** disc is coupled to a motor shaft. The frequency counter records **800 pulses in 2 seconds**. Calculate: (i) the rotational speed in **rpm**, (ii) the **linear speed** at the rim if the disc diameter is **10 cm**, and (iii) the **percentage error** if a mechanical tachometer reads **610 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A DC motor whose speed is to be measured is mechanically coupled (via a flexible coupling) to a thin aluminium disc on which **S = 40** equi-spaced slots have been cut at the periphery. The disc passes through the **U-shaped slot of an opto-coupler**, which has an **IR LED** on one arm and a **phototransistor** on the opposite arm. The phototransistor output is fed to a **Schmitt trigger (SN 7414)** to obtain clean **TTL-compatible square waves**, which are simultaneously displayed on a **DSO** and fed to a **digital frequency counter** calibrated to display rpm directly.

**Working:** As the disc rotates, each slot allows the IR beam to fall on the phototransistor, causing its collector voltage to fall to a **LOW** level. The solid portion of the disc blocks the beam, causing the collector voltage to rise to a **HIGH** level. Thus **one slot passing = one electrical pulse**. Over time, a continuous train of square waves is produced whose frequency **f** is measured by the counter.

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1, replicated compactly):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulse frequency, } f &= \frac{N_p}{T} \\[4pt]
\text{Since 1 rev = S pulses, revolutions per second, } n &= \frac{f}{S} \\[4pt]
\text{Speed in rpm, } N &= 60 \times n = \frac{60 \, f}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** S = 40 slots, N_p = 800 pulses, T = 2 s, D = 10 cm = 0.1 m, N_mech = 610 rpm.

**(i) Rotational speed in rpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{N_p}{T} = \frac{800}{2} = 400 \;\text{Hz} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{60 \, f}{S} = \frac{60 \times 400}{40} = \frac{24\,000}{40} = 600 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Linear speed at the rim:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \pi D \times n = \pi \times 0.1 \times \frac{600}{60} \\[4pt]
  &= \pi \times 0.1 \times 10 = \pi \;\text{m/s} \approx 3.142 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Error} &= \frac{\vert N_{true} - N_{mech} \vert}{N_{true}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\vert 600 - 610 \vert}{600} \times 100 = \frac{10}{600} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 1.667\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q.3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supple, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a stroboscope**. Derive the relation between the rotational speed of a rotor and the flash frequency at which the rotor appears stationary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a stroboscopic test on a motor, the marked dot on the rotor appears **stationary** when the stroboscope flash rate is set to **f₁ = 75 Hz** and again when set to **f₂ = 150 Hz** and again at **f₃ = 225 Hz**. Determine the **true speed** of the rotor in rpm. Also explain the role of **persistence of vision** in this observation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A stroboscope consists of a **variable-frequency pulse generator** (typically a 555 astable multivibrator or a microcontroller PWM), a **trigger and driver circuit**, and a **Xenon flash tube** (or high-power LED array). The pulse-generator frequency is varied using a **calibrated rotary knob** with a digital LCD or analog dial showing the **flash frequency in Hz**.

**Working:** The astable multivibrator produces a square wave of frequency **f**. Each positive edge triggers the driver circuit, which fires the Xenon tube, producing a flash of **1–3 µs** duration. The rotor under test is illuminated by this flashing light. The observer adjusts **f** until the rotor's marked point appears frozen. At this point, the time between consecutive flashes equals the time for one (or an integer number of) rotations.

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{flash} &= \frac{k}{n} \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = k \, f_{flash} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad N &= 60 \, k \, f_{flash} \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

where **k** is the integer order of the apparent freeze (k = 1 ⇒ fundamental).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Frozen-image flash frequencies = 75 Hz, 150 Hz, 225 Hz.

**Step 1 — Identify the fundamental:** The smallest frozen frequency corresponds to the *fundamental* (k = 1). All higher values are harmonics (k = 2, 3 …). Check:

$$
\begin{aligned}
75 \times 1 &= 75 \;\text{Hz} \quad (\text{fundamental}) \\[4pt]
75 \times 2 &= 150 \;\text{Hz} \quad (\text{matches f}_2\text{ ✓}) \\[4pt]
75 \times 3 &= 225 \;\text{Hz} \quad (\text{matches f}_3\text{ ✓})
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the true flash frequency corresponding to k = 1 is **f = 75 Hz**.

**Step 2 — Compute true rpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 60 \times k \times f_{flash} = 60 \times 1 \times 75 \\[4pt]
  &= 4500 \;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of persistence of vision (3 marks):**
