The following Mermaid block captures the **end-to-end signal chain** of a float-coupled potentiometric level sensor, from the physical level to the displayed digital output. Use this diagram to write short notes or as the answer to a "draw and explain" type 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tank with Liquid] --> B[Floa t rides on Liquid Surface]
    B --> C[Mechanical Link to Wiper]
    C --> D[Helical Potentiometer Track]
    D --> E[DC Excitation Voltage Vex]
    E --> F[Wiper Output Terminal]
    F --> G[Buffer Amplifier]
    G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    H --> I[Microcontroller or Display Unit]
    I --> J[Level Reading in mm or cm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mechanical link (C) is often a non-magnetic push rod or a chain-and-pulley system. The buffer amplifier (G) isolates the high-impedance wiper from the loading effect of the ADC. In the KTU lab, the buffer is typically realized with a **non-inverting op-amp using the LM741** or **op-amp µA741**.

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Capacitive Level Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Tank with Liquid] --> A2[Coaxial or Parallel Plate Probe]
    A2 --> B1[AC Excitation Source 1 kHz to 1 MHz]
    B1 --> B2[Impedance Bridge or Capacitance to Voltage Converter]
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C1[Detector and Amplifier Stage]
    C1 --> D1[Rectifier and Filter]
    D1 --> E1[ADC and Display]
    E1 --> F1[Digital Level in mm or cm]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AC excitation?** A capacitance is a *reactive* element and cannot be energized with DC (steady-state current would simply be zero). The bridge must therefore be excited with a **sinusoidal source** whose frequency is chosen such that the impedance of the probe lies in a measurable range, typically **$1 \ \text{kHz}$ to $100 \ \text{kHz}$** for water-based probes.

### 4.3 Comparative Functional Flow — Resistive vs. Capacitive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ResistivePath
        R1[Level] --> R2[Float Position]
        R2 --> R3[Wiper on Resistance Track]
        R3 --> R4[DC Voltage Divider]
        R4 --> R5[Buffered DC Voltage Output]
    end
    subgraph CapacitivePath
        C1[Level] --> C2[Dielectric Volume Modulation]
        C2 --> C3[Capacitance Change dC]
        C3 --> C4[AC Bridge or C to V Converter]
        C4 --> C5[Rectified DC Output]
    end
    R5 --> X[Indicator or Recorder]
    C5 --> X
```

### 4.4 Calibration Setup (Lab Sketch Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Measuring Tape] --> B[Glass Tank or Test Vessel]
    B --> C[Level Sensor Mounted Vertically]
    C --> D[Excitation and Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    D --> E[Digital Multimeter or DSO]
    E --> F[Calibration Table: h vs. Vout]
    F --> G[Best-Fit Line: Slope and Intercept]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Exam Tip:** Always present your calibration data as a *table of $h$ versus $V_{out}$* and then plot it. The slope gives the sensitivity, the y-intercept gives the zero-level offset, and the $R^2$ (coefficient of determination) gives the linearity. Examiners reward these three quantities explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**Differentiate between a float-type resistive level transducer and a capacitive level transducer based on principle of operation, output type, and one industrial application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Float-Resistive | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Level changes the wiper position on a resistance track, modulating $R$ | Level changes the dielectric volume between plates, modulating $C$ |
| Output | DC voltage (direct, from a voltage divider) | Capacitance (read via AC bridge) |
| Application | Automobile fuel gauge, overhead water-level indicator | Oil tank, chemical reactor, pharmaceutical storage |

*(1 mark each for principle, output, and application — distribute as per KTU valuation key.)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**A coaxial capacitive probe has inner radius 6 mm and outer radius 12 mm, length 50 cm. Compute the empty capacitance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using $C_0 = (2\pi \varepsilon_0 L) / \ln(b/a)$:

$$\ln(b/a) = \ln(12/6) = \ln 2 = 0.6931$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.50}{0.6931} = \frac{2.7808 \times 10^{-11}}{0.6931} = 4.012 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_0 \approx 40.12 \ \text{pF}\,}$$

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working of a **float-coupled potentiometric level sensor**. Derive the relationship between output voltage and liquid level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A float-potentiometer has $R_{total} = 4 \ \text{k}\Omega$ and full-scale level $H = 2 \ \text{m}$. It is excited by $V_{ex} = 10 \ \text{V DC}$. Compute the wiper resistance and output voltage when the level is $h = 1.4 \ \text{m}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** A vertical helical potentiometer with float, wiper, and output terminals as drawn in Section 4.1.
- **Working (3 marks):** The float rises/falls with the liquid surface. The float is rigidly coupled to the wiper, sliding along the resistance track. A constant $V_{ex}$ is applied across the full track. The wiper tap-off gives an output voltage proportional to the level.

- **Derivation (2 marks):**

$$V_{out}(h) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R(h)}{R_{total}} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{(R_{total}/H)\,h}{R_{total}} = \frac{V_{ex}}{H} \cdot h$$

The output is **linear in $h$**, with sensitivity $S = V_{ex} / H$.

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Resistance per unit length:

$$R' = \frac{R_{total}}{H} = \frac{4000 \ \Omega}{2 \ \text{m}} = 2000 \ \Omega/\text{m}$$

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Resistance at $h = 1.4 \ \text{m}$:

$$R(1.4) = R' \cdot h = 2000 \times 1.4 = 2800 \ \Omega = 2.8 \ \text{k}\Omega$$

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Output voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R(h)}{R_{total}} = 10 \cdot \frac{2800}{4000} = 10 \cdot 0.70 = 7.0 \ \text{V}$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Verify with the linear formula:

$$V_{out} = (V_{ex}/H) \cdot h = (10/2) \cdot 1.4 = 7.0 \ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

- **Step 5 (2 marks):** Discussion — The sensitivity is $5 \ \text{V/m}$, so the resolution of a 10-bit ADC over a 0–10 V range would yield $10/1024 \approx 9.77 \ \text{mV/LSB}$, which corresponds to $9.77/5 \approx 1.95 \ \text{mm/LSB}$ in level.

$$\boxed{\,R(h = 1.4) = 2.8 \ \text{k}\Omega, \quad V_{out} = 7.0 \ \text{V}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive level sensor** using the *variable dielectric* principle. Derive the expression for the capacitance as a function of liquid level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two parallel plates of width $W = 4 \ \text{cm}$ and length $L = 25 \ \text{cm}$ are separated by $d = 1.5 \ \text{mm}$. The liquid has $\varepsilon_r = 3$. Calculate the change in capacitance when the level rises from $h_1 = 5 \ \text{cm}$ to $h_2 = 20 \ \text{cm}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** Two vertical parallel plates immersed in a tank; an AC source; capacitance meter or AC bridge. (Refer Section 4.2.)
- **Working (3 marks):** The space between the plates is filled partially by the liquid (dielectric $\varepsilon_r$) and partially by air ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1$). The plates thus form a **parallel combination** of an air capacitor (height $L-h$) and a liquid capacitor (height $h$).
- **Derivation (2 marks):**

$$C(h) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d}\left[L + h(\varepsilon_r - 1)\right]$$

The relation is **linear in $h$**, justifying its use as a level sensor.

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the constant factor $\varepsilon_0 W / d$:

$$K = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.04}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{F/m}$$

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Capacitance at $h_1 = 5 \ \text{cm} = 0.05 \ \text{m}$:

$$C(h_1) = K \left[L + h_1(\varepsilon_r - 1)\right] = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \cdot [0.25 + 0.05 \times 2]$$

$$C(h_1) = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 0.35 = 8.264 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F} = 82.64 \ \text{pF}$$

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Capacitance at $h_2 = 20 \ \text{cm} = 0.20 \ \text{m}$:

$$C(h_2) = K \left[L + h_2(\varepsilon_r - 1)\right] = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \cdot [0.25 + 0.20 \times 2]$$

$$C(h_2) = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 0.65 = 1.5347 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{F} = 153.47 \ \text{pF}$$

- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = C(h_2) - C(h_1) = 153.47 - 82.64 = 70.83 \ \text{pF}$$

- **Step 5 (2 marks):** Cross-check using the direct $\Delta C$ formula:

$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{d} \cdot \Delta h = 2.361 \times 10^{-10} \times 2 \times (0.20 - 0.05) = 7.083 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F} = 70.83 \ \text{pF} \ \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta C \approx 70.83 \ \text{pF}\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU Board Exam Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel-combination concept** — Students often treat the partially filled capacitor as a single dielectric, instead of the *sum* of an air capacitor and a liquid capacitor. This is a **2-mark** deduction in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 2. **Using $\varepsilon_r = 80$ for water without comment** — The exam usually provides $\varepsilon_r$ of the *given* liquid. If the problem says "oil" or "kerosene," you must use the given value, not water's.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Plate dimensions in **mm** vs. **m** is a notorious pitfall. Convert everything to SI (m) **before** substituting into $\varepsilon_0$.
> 4. **Linear interpretation error** — Some students believe the parallel-plate capacitor is non-linear because of the $(\varepsilon_r - 1)$ factor. It is **linear in $h$** because the other parameters are constants. Be explicit about this in your answer.
> 5. **Forgetting the buffer stage** — When asked to draw the block diagram, marks are lost if the *signal conditioning* (buffer/amplifier) and *display* blocks are missing.
> 6. **DC excitation of a capacitive sensor** — A capacitive sensor MUST be excited with AC. Writing "DC excitation" for a capacitive level sensor is a **fundamental conceptual error** and will attract negative marking.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Read):**
> - Level $\rightarrow$ Resistance: **Float-Potentiometer** (DC output, linear in $h$).
> - Level $\rightarrow$ Capacitance: **Variable-Dielectric Probe** (AC excitation, linear in $h$).
> - **Float-potentiometer output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot h / H$.
> - **Parallel-plate partial-fill:** $C(h) = (\varepsilon_0 W / d) [L + h(\varepsilon_r - 1)]$.
> - **Cylindrical probe partial-fill:** $C(h) = (2 \pi \varepsilon_0 / \ln(b/a)) [L + h(\varepsilon_r - 1)]$.
> - **Change in capacitance** is directly proportional to $(\varepsilon_r - 1)$ and to $h$ — both levers.
> - **Sensitivity of cylindrical probe:** $dC/dh = (2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)) / \ln(b/a)$.
> - **AC bridge** is used for capacitive sensors; **Wheatstone bridge** for resistive.
> - Always convert all lengths to **metres** before substitution; report capacitance in **pF** ($1 \ \text{pF} = 10^{-12} \ \text{F}$).
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ — *memorize this constant*.
> - **Linearity error formula:** $e_L = (e_{abs} / V_{FS}) \times 100 \ \%$.
> - **Industrial use:** resistive for cheap consumer fuel gauges; capacitive for high-accuracy chemical/petroleum storage.
> - **Calibration** in the lab: tabulate $h$ vs. $V_{out}$, plot, extract **slope (sensitivity)**, **intercept (offset)**, and **$R^2$ (linearity)**.
> - **Temperature compensation** is critical for capacitive sensors measuring water due to the strong temperature dependence of $\varepsilon_{r,water}$.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Level Measurement using Resistive/Capacitive Transducers (PCAEL307 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Measurement using U-tube Manometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Measurement using U-tube Manometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **U-tube Manometer** is a primary-standard, fluid-column-based differential pressure measuring instrument consisting of a transparent tube bent into a "U" shape and partially filled with a manometric fluid (typically **mercury (Hg)**, water, or oil). It operates on the hydrostatic equilibrium principle to convert an unknown pressure (or pressure difference) into a measurable vertical height difference of the fluid columns.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab context (PCAEL307), the U-tube manometer is studied as a **calibration reference instrument** for characterizing Bourdon gauges, diaphragm transducers, and differential pressure transmitters. The output is a **gravitational head (h)** expressed in mm or cm of the manometric fluid, which is later converted to engineering pressure units (kPa, bar, kgf/cm²).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PCAEL307, Module 2):**
> The experiment "Pressure Measurement using U-tube Manometer" trains the student to (i) identify manometric fluids, (ii) apply the manometer equation to compute gauge and absolute pressures, (iii) correct for fluid meniscus, temperature, and capillary effects, and (iv) quantify the **uncertainty / error band** of the reading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two balloons connected by a flexible straw filled with water. If you squeeze one balloon harder than the other, the water inside the straw gets pushed down on the squeezed side and rises on the other side. The difference in water level tells you *how much harder* you were squeezing.

A U-tube manometer works exactly on this principle:

- The **two "balloons"** are the two pressure points (P₁ and P₂) connected to the two open ends of the U-tube.
- The **"water in the straw"** is the manometric fluid (often mercury because it is dense and does not evaporate).
- The **"height difference" (h)** of the fluid in the two limbs is what we measure. A bigger height difference means a bigger pressure difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a heavy fluid like Mercury (ρ ≈ 13,600 kg/m³)?**
> A denser fluid produces a *smaller* height difference for the *same* pressure difference. This keeps the manometer compact, the reading stable (less affected by vibration), and the meniscus sharper — making the instrument laboratory-grade accurate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.80665** | m/s² |
| Density of mercury (at 0 °C) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,595.1** | kg/m³ |
| Density of water (at 4 °C) | $\rho_{w}$ | **1,000** | kg/m³ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa** = 1.01325 bar | Pa |
| 1 kgf/cm² (technical atmosphere) | — | **98,066.5 Pa** | Pa |
| 1 Torr (1 mmHg) | — | **133.322 Pa** | Pa |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab viva, examiners almost always ask: *"Why is the standard value of g taken as 9.81 m/s² and not 9.8 or 10?"* — Answer: KTU follows the **standard gravity** used by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS), which is 9.80665 m/s², commonly rounded to 9.81 m/s² in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Hydrostatic Equation

The U-tube manometer is governed by **Pascal's Law** and the **principle of communicating vessels**. The operational logic can be broken into four sequential steps:

1. **Pressure Application:** Two unknown pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$ are applied at the two open ends (limbs) of the U-tube.
2. **Fluid Displacement:** The higher-pressure limb pushes the manometric fluid down; the lower-pressure limb receives the displaced fluid, so its level rises.
3. **Equilibrium:** A static equilibrium is established when the weight of the displaced fluid column exactly balances the pressure difference. Mathematically:
$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$
4. **Measurement:** The vertical height difference $h$ between the two menisci is read off a graduated scale (typically a **vernier scale** with 0.02 mm least count in precision manometers).

### 2.2 Derivation of the General Manometer Equation

Consider an elemental horizontal plane passing through the **lower meniscus** (Level A–A′) inside the U-tube. At equilibrium, the pressure at this reference plane must be equal when computed from either limb.

Starting from limb 1 (left) and traversing down to the reference plane:
$$P_1 + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot z_1 = P_{ref}$$

Starting from limb 2 (right) and traversing down to the reference plane:
$$P_2 + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot z_2 = P_{ref}$$

Equating both expressions and rearranging gives the **general manometer equation**:
$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1) = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $\rho_f$ = density of the manometric fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = height difference of fluid columns (m)
- $P_1, P_2$ = applied pressures (Pa)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f \, g \, h$ | Differential pressure (both limbs connected to fluids of negligible weight) | Valid for static equilibrium only |
| 2 | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_f - \rho_{fl}) \, g \, h$ | Inverted / differential with light fluid above manometric fluid | Used when measuring gas pressure or vacuum |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute pressure from gauge reading | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa at sea level |
| 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | Absolute pressure from vacuum reading | Used for sub-atmospheric systems |
| 5 | $h_{mm\,Hg} = \dfrac{P_{(Pa)}}{133.322}$ | Conversion of Pascal to mm of Mercury | Standard 0 °C reference |
| 6 | $1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 750.06 \, \text{mmHg}$ | Bar ↔ mmHg conversion | — |
| 7 | $\rho_{Hg,\,T} = \dfrac{\rho_{Hg,\,0°C}}{1 + 0.000182 \cdot T}$ | Mercury density correction for temperature $T$ (°C) | Apply when $T \neq 0$ °C |
| 8 | $\delta h_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho_f \, g \, r}$ | Capillary depression correction (mm) | Significant only for tube radius $r < 5$ mm |
| 9 | $P = \rho_f \, g \, h$ in **kgf/cm²** | $\dfrac{P_{(Pa)}}{98066.5}$ | Engineering / Indian industrial standard |
| 10 | $u_c(h) = \sqrt{u_{scale}^2 + u_{meniscus}^2 + u_{temp}^2}$ | Combined standard uncertainty of $h$ (GUM method) | Used in lab report calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notational Rule for KTU Exam Scripts:**
> In manometer problems, always clearly distinguish:
> - $h$ = **height difference** between the two menisci (read from the scale).
> - $z$ = **vertical depth** of a fluid column above the reference plane.
> Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing the wrong variable in the final substitution step.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

U-tube manometers, despite being centuries old, are still ubiquitous in:

- **Petrochemical industry:** Calibration of orifice-plate flow meters (dP measurement across the plate).
- **HVAC:** Filter pressure-drop monitoring across AHUs and clean rooms.
- **Medical equipment:** Sphygmomanometers (blood-pressure cuffs) — a manometer in disguise.
- **Aerospace:** Altimeter sub-assemblies, cabin-pressure differential indicators.
- **Calibration laboratories (NABL-accredited):** Dead-weight testers cross-verified against U-tube manometers as a primary standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: U-tube Manometer with Two Liquids

**Problem Setup:** A U-tube manometer uses mercury ($\rho_{Hg}$) and is connected between two pipes carrying water. Pipe 1 has pressure $P_1$ and pipe 2 has pressure $P_2$. The mercury level in the left limb is 0.25 m below the centerline of pipe 1, and in the right limb is 0.40 m below the centerline of pipe 2. The mercury height difference $h$ reads 0.18 m. Find $P_1 - P_2$ in kPa.

**Step 1 — Identify the reference plane.**

Choose the lower mercury meniscus (let's say the right limb, since it is lower when $P_1 > P_2$). Call this the **iso-pressure plane** (line A–A′).

**Step 2 — Compute pressure at the reference plane from the LEFT limb.**

Starting from $P_1$ in pipe 1, move *downward* through water by 0.25 m, then *downward* through mercury by $h$ = 0.18 m.

$$P_{A} = P_1 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (0.25) + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot (h)$$

> *Why are these terms positive?* — Because we are moving downward in the fluid, hydrostatic pressure *adds* to the existing pressure.

**Step 3 — Compute pressure at the reference plane from the RIGHT limb.**

Starting from $P_2$ in pipe 2, move *downward* through water by 0.40 m. At the reference plane (the right meniscus), the path is purely through water.

$$P_{A'} = P_2 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (0.40)$$

> Note: We do **not** add any mercury column from the right limb because the reference plane *is* the right meniscus.

**Step 4 — Equate the pressures at the iso-pressure plane (Pascal's Law).**

By static equilibrium, the pressure at the same horizontal level inside a connected static fluid must be equal:

$$P_A = P_{A'}$$

$$P_1 + \rho_w \, g \, (0.25) + \rho_{Hg} \, g \, (h) = P_2 + \rho_w \, g \, (0.40)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate the unknown $P_1 - P_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= \rho_w \, g \, (0.40) - \rho_w \, g \, (0.25) - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h \\
P_1 - P_2 &= \rho_w \, g \, (0.15) - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

Using $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³, $h = 0.18$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= (1000)(9.81)(0.15) - (13600)(9.81)(0.18) \\
P_1 - P_2 &= 1471.5 - 24{,}008.88 \\
P_1 - P_2 &= -22{,}537.38 \; \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kPa and interpret the sign.**

$$P_1 - P_2 = -22.54 \; \text{kPa}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that $P_1 < P_2$ by 22.54 kPa. This is physically consistent: if the mercury was pushed *up* in the left limb and *down* in the right limb, the higher pressure is on the right.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Absolute Pressure from a Barometer-type U-tube

**Problem Setup:** A closed-end U-tube (one limb sealed and evacuated at the top) is used as a barometer. Mercury rises to a height $h = 760$ mm in the sealed limb. Find the atmospheric pressure.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**

- The **sealed limb** has a perfect vacuum above mercury → pressure at the top of mercury column = 0 Pa (absolute).
- The **open limb** is exposed to the atmosphere $P_{atm}$.

**Step 2 — Choose the reference plane.**

Choose the horizontal plane at the **mercury level in the open limb** (lower meniscus).

**Step 3 — Pressure at the reference plane from the open limb.**

$$P_{ref} = P_{atm} + 0 = P_{atm}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure at the reference plane from the sealed limb.**

$$P_{ref} = 0 + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{atm} &= \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h \\
P_{atm} &= (13600)(9.81)(0.760) \\
P_{atm} &= 101{,}396.16 \; \text{Pa} \\
P_{atm} &\approx 101.4 \; \text{kPa} \approx 1 \; \text{atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Variations Tested in KTU Lab

| Variation | Reference Plane Strategy | Key Equation | Common Student Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both limbs carry same fluid as in pipe | Lower meniscus | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_f - \rho_{fl}) \, g \, h$ | Forgetting to subtract pipe-fluid weight |
| One limb open to atmosphere | Open-end meniscus | $P_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_f \, g \, h$ | Sign confusion on manometer fluid direction |
| Inverted manometer (gas measurement) | Higher meniscus (light fluid) | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{fl} - \rho_f) \, g \, h$ | Using wrong sign for the height |
| Multi-fluid (mercury + water + oil) | Multiple iso-planes at each interface | Sum of $\rho_i g h_i$ terms | Missing one fluid column in the chain |
| Inclined U-tube (for low pressures) | Vertical component of slant | $\Delta P = \rho_f \, g \, h \sin\theta$ | Confusing slant length with vertical height $h$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack — "Sign Convention Rule":**
> Always travel from the *known* pressure to the *reference plane*. If you are going **down** through a fluid, **add** $\rho g h$. If you are going **up**, **subtract** $\rho g h$. At the reference plane, set the two expressions equal. This single rule eliminates 90% of sign errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a U-tube Manometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1Node[Pressure Source P1 Left Limb] --> L1Node[Connecting Tube with Process Fluid]
    P2Node[Pressure Source P2 Right Limb] --> L2Node[Connecting Tube with Process Fluid]
    L1Node --> MNode[U-tube Manometer Body]
    L2Node --> MNode
    MNode --> SubNode[Sub-system Mercury Reservoir]
    SubNode --> HNode[Height Difference h between Menisci]
    HNode --> CalcNode[Computation Block P1 minus P2 equals rho Hg g h]
    CalcNode --> Out1[Output Differential Pressure kPa]
    CalcNode --> Out2[Output Absolute Pressure kPa]
    CalcNode --> Out3[Output Gauge Pressure kPa]
    HNode --> ScaleNode[Graduated Scale and Vernier]
    ScaleNode --> ReadNode[Observer Reads h in mm]
    ReadNode --> HNode
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Pressure Path Through a U-tube Manometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pipe 1 carrying water P1] --> B[Water Column height a]
    B --> C[Mercury Meniscus Left higher]
    C --> D[Mercury Column difference h]
    D --> E[Mercury Meniscus Right lower]
    E --> F[Water Column height b]
    F --> G[Pipe 2 carrying water P2]
    IsoPlane[Reference Plane A to A prime] -.-> C
    IsoPlane -.-> E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Flow in Lab Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1 Connect manometer to test points] --> Step2[Step 2 Bleed air from connecting tubes]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Wait for thermal and mechanical equilibrium]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Read left meniscus on vernier scale]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Read right meniscus on vernier scale]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Compute h equals right minus left]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7 Apply capillary and temperature corrections]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8 Compute pressure using manometer equation]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9 Estimate uncertainty and write lab report]
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensing
        M1[Limbs Glass Borosilicate]
        M2[Manometric Fluid Mercury]
        M3[Reference Scale mm]
    end
    subgraph Computation
        C1[Hydrostatic Equation]
        C2[Density Correction]
        C3[Capillary Correction]
    end
    subgraph Output
        O1[Differential Pressure kPa]
        O2[Absolute Pressure kPa]
        O3[Uncertainty Band]
    end
    M1 --> C1
    M2 --> C1
    M3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> O1
    C1 --> C3 --> O2
    C2 --> O3
    C3 --> O3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a U-tube manometer. State the principle on which it works.*

**Model Answer:**
A U-tube manometer is a differential pressure-measuring instrument consisting of a U-shaped transparent tube partially filled with a manometric fluid (commonly mercury). It works on the **principle of hydrostatic equilibrium** — at static equilibrium, the pressure at the same horizontal level inside a connected static fluid is equal. The difference in fluid column heights ($h$) is directly proportional to the applied pressure difference: $P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f \, g \, h$.

*(Word limit: ~50 words for a 3-mark answer. Sketch the U-tube in the margin for bonus marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is mercury preferred over water as the manometric fluid in a U-tube manometer?*

**Model Answer:**
Mercury is preferred for three reasons:
1. **High density (13,600 kg/m³):** Produces a smaller and more readable column height $h$ for the same pressure.
2. **Immiscibility:** Does not mix with most process fluids (water, oil, gases), maintaining a sharp meniscus.
3. **Low vapor pressure and chemical stability:** Maintains a stable meniscus and does not contaminate the process.

A smaller $h$ also reduces the **capillary depression error**, improving measurement accuracy.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** A differential U-tube manometer is connected across two points in a pipeline carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85. The manometer uses mercury. The higher-pressure point is at an elevation of 0.5 m below the lower-pressure point. The manometer reading shows a mercury deflection of $h = 12$ cm. Determine the pressure difference between the two points in kPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of an **inclined U-tube manometer**. Why is it preferred for measuring low pressures (below 5 kPa)? **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** $SG_{oil} = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³, $h = 0.12$ m, elevation difference = 0.5 m, $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Set up the manometer equation** traversing from point 1 (higher pressure) to point 2 (lower pressure), passing through the mercury column.

Choosing the lower meniscus as the reference plane:

$$P_1 + \rho_{oil} \, g \, (0.5) - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h - \rho_{oil} \, g \, (0.5 - h) = P_2$$

Wait — the correct approach: traverse along the path from P1 to the reference plane (lower meniscus) and from P2 to the reference plane. Equating:

$$P_1 + \rho_{oil} \, g \, (z_1) + \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h = P_2 + \rho_{oil} \, g \, (z_2)$$

where $z_1$ and $z_2$ are the vertical distances from P1 and P2 to their respective menisci.

**Step 2 — Simplify** using $z_2 - z_1 = 0.5$ m:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{oil} \, g \, (0.5) - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 - P_2 &= (850)(9.81)(0.5) - (13600)(9.81)(0.12) \\
&= 4169.25 - 16{,}009.92 \\
&= -11{,}840.67 \; \text{Pa} \\
&\approx -11.84 \; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates $P_1 < P_2$, which is consistent with the stated geometry. The **magnitude** of the pressure difference is **11.84 kPa**.

##### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

An inclined U-tube manometer (also called a **draft gauge** or **micro-manometer**) is a modified version of the standard U-tube in which one limb is inclined at a small angle $\theta$ (typically 5°–30°) to the horizontal, while the other limb remains vertical and acts as a large reservoir.

**Working:**
1. The pressure to be measured is applied at the inclined limb.
2. The fluid in the reservoir (right limb) drops only slightly, but the fluid in the inclined tube moves a long distance $L$ along the slope.
3. The **vertical component** of this movement is $h = L \sin\theta$.
4. The pressure relation becomes:
$$P = \rho_f \, g \, L \sin\theta$$

**Why preferred for low pressures:**
- For a small $\Delta P$, the standard U-tube would produce a tiny $h$ (e.g., 1 mm of water for ~9.8 Pa) — very hard to read.
- Inclining the tube **amplifies** the reading: a 10 mm slant length at $\theta = 5.85°$ corresponds to $h = 1$ mm vertical, effectively multiplying the readability by **~10×** without loss of accuracy.
- Resolution of 0.01 mm of water column is achievable in precision inclined manometers.

**Sketch:** *(Student should draw: vertical reservoir limb + inclined limb with angle θ, slant length L, vertical equivalent h = L sinθ, and label the meniscus movement.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, derive the **general manometer equation** for a U-tube manometer. List any **four corrections** applied to a manometer reading. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed U-tube manometer is used to measure the pressure of gas in a tank. The manometer uses mercury. When one limb is connected to the gas and the other is sealed and evacuated, the mercury level in the sealed limb is 50 mm **above** the level in the open-to-gas limb. If atmospheric pressure is 100.5 kPa, find the absolute pressure of the gas in kPa. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Diagram:** Show the U-tube with two applied pressures P₁ and P₂, manometric fluid of density $\rho_f$, left and right menisci, and height difference $h$ clearly marked. (See SECTION 4.2 mermaid schematic.)

**Derivation:**

1. Apply pressure at point A (left limb meniscus): $P_A = P_1 + \rho_f \, g \, z_1$, where $z_1$ is the depth of fluid above the reference plane.
2. Apply pressure at point B (right limb meniscus): $P_B = P_2 + \rho_f \, g \, z_2$.
3. By the principle of communicating vessels and static equilibrium, pressures at the same horizontal level inside the connected fluid are equal: $P_A = P_B$.
4. Therefore:
$$P_1 + \rho_f \, g \, z_1 = P_2 + \rho_f \, g \, z_2$$
$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f \, g \, (z_2 - z_1) = \rho_f \, g \, h$$

**Four Corrections Applied to Manometer Reading:**

| # | Correction | Formula | When Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Meniscus / Capillary depression** | $\delta h_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho_f g r}$ | When tube bore $< 10$ mm |
| 2 | **Temperature correction** (mercury density) | $\rho_{Hg,T} = \dfrac{\rho_{Hg,0°C}}{1 + 0.000182 \, T}$ | When $T \neq 0$ °C |
| 3 | **Gravity correction** | $g_{local} = 9.7803(1 + 0.0052\sin^2\phi) - 3.086 \times 10^{-6} H$ | When instrument is at high altitude or non-standard latitude |
| 4 | **Scale / zero error correction** | $h_{true} = h_{reading} - h_{zero}$ | Always applied; periodic calibration |

##### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** Mercury level in *sealed* limb is 50 mm **above** the level in the *gas-connected* limb. $P_{atm} = 100.5$ kPa. Gas is connected to one limb; the other is sealed (vacuum above mercury).

**Step 1 — Interpret the geometry.**
- Sealed limb (vacuum at top) → mercury is pushed **up** in this limb → pressure at the top = 0 Pa (vacuum).
- Gas-connected limb → mercury is pushed **down** → gas pressure pushes mercury down.

**Step 2 — Choose reference plane at the lower meniscus** (gas-connected limb).

**Step 3 — Pressure at reference plane from the sealed limb (going down through 50 mm Hg):**

$$P_{ref} = 0 + \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h = (13600)(9.81)(0.050)$$

**Step 4 — Pressure at reference plane from the gas limb (going up from gas through the connecting fluid path to the meniscus):**

Since the connecting tube above the gas-limb meniscus is filled with gas (negligible weight), pressure at the gas-limb meniscus is essentially equal to the gas pressure $P_{gas}$.

So: $P_{ref} = P_{gas}$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gas} &= \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h \\
P_{gas} &= (13600)(9.81)(0.050) \\
P_{gas} &= 6670.8 \; \text{Pa} \\
P_{gas} &\approx 6.67 \; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note on absolute vs gauge:** The problem asks for **absolute pressure** of the gas. The gas pressure we calculated is already absolute (measured relative to the vacuum in the sealed limb). So:

$$\boxed{P_{gas,\,abs} \approx 6.67 \; \text{kPa (absolute)}}$$

If the question had asked for gauge pressure:
$$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} = 6.67 - 100.5 = -93.83 \; \text{kPa (vacuum)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion slips (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mm → m before substituting in the manometer equation. Always convert height to meters.
> 2. **Sign convention errors (–2 marks):** Writing $P_1 - P_2 = +\rho_f g h$ without verifying the physical direction of fluid movement. Always re-check by intuition: which side is higher?
> 3. **Confusing absolute vs gauge pressure (–2 marks):** U-tube manometers measure **differential** or **gauge** pressure directly. Absolute pressure requires adding $P_{atm}$.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU evaluators explicitly award marks for a "neat labelled diagram." Even a rough U-tube with arrows showing $P_1$, $P_2$, and $h$ earns partial credit.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the reference plane (–1 mark):** Always write "Let A–A′ be the reference plane at the lower mercury meniscus" before starting the derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core equation:** $P_1 - P_2 = \rho_f \, g \, h$ (always write this first in any problem).
- **Always convert units to SI before substituting:** $h$ in metres, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², result in Pa.
- **Reference plane = lower meniscus** in a standard U-tube (so that you add fluid columns going *down* from each side).
- **Sign rule:** "Down = ADD pressure" and "Up = SUBTRACT pressure" while traversing the fluid path.
- **Mercury is preferred** for its high density, immiscibility, sharp meniscus, and chemical stability.
- **Inclined U-tube manometers** are used for low-pressure measurement (draft gauges); pressure scales as $\rho g L \sin\theta$.
- **Corrections to apply in lab:** meniscus/capillary, temperature (mercury density), gravity, and zero error.
- **Uncertainty reporting** (GUM method) is a mandatory section of the KTU lab record — compute combined standard uncertainty for $h$ and propagate to $P$.
- **Absolute vs gauge:** Manometer reads gauge by default; add $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa to get absolute.
- **Quick sanity check:** $1$ mmHg $\approx 133.3$ Pa — useful for fast mental verification of computed answers.
- **Instrument classification:** U-tube manometer is a **primary standard**; Bourdon gauge is a **secondary instrument** that must be calibrated against it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Frequency and Phase using Lissajous Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Frequency and Phase using Lissajous Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lissajous Patterns (Lissajous Figures)** are the closed-loop traces produced on the screen of a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) operating in **X-Y mode**, when two sinusoidal electrical signals of potentially different frequencies and a phase difference are simultaneously applied to the vertical (Y) and horizontal (X) deflection plates respectively.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the technique is classified under **"Comparative Time Base Methods"** of frequency and phase measurement, in which an unknown signal is compared against a known reference signal (usually derived from a function generator or a calibrated audio oscillator).

Mathematically, when two signals are applied:

$$x(t) = X_m \sin(\omega_x t)$$

$$y(t) = Y_m \sin(\omega_y t + \phi)$$

The trajectory traced by the electron beam on the phosphor screen is the parametric plot of `y` versus `x`, governed entirely by the **frequency ratio** $\frac{\omega_y}{\omega_x}$ and the **phase difference** $\phi$ between the two signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PCAEL307 lab manual, Experiment 2 specifically expects the student to (i) determine the **frequency of an unknown signal** by comparing it against the calibrated output of a laboratory function generator and (ii) determine the **phase shift** introduced by an RC low-pass filter or similar network. This is a **mandatory viva question** topic.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Two-Pendulum Sand Trace"

Imagine you have a **small funnel of sand** suspended from the ceiling by two strings, forming a pendulum that can swing **both left-right and forward-backward**. A sheet of paper lies on the floor.

- One person pushes the funnel rhythmically from the **left-right direction** (this is your **horizontal / X input**).
- Another person pushes the funnel **forward-backward** (this is your **vertical / Y input**).
- If both push at **exactly the same beat**, the sand draws a tilted straight line, an ellipse, or a circle depending on whether they push *in sync, slightly out of sync, or perfectly 90° out of sync*.
- If one pushes **twice as fast as the other**, the sand draws a **figure-eight**.

Now replace the funnel-and-sand with an **electron beam** moving on a phosphor screen, and replace the human pushers with **two electrical signals**. That traced pattern is exactly what we call a **Lissajous Pattern**.

> [!NOTE]
> The names "Lissajous" and "Bowditch" are both credited in literature; the curves were extensively studied by French physicist **Jules Antoine Lissajous** in 1857 using mechanical-optical apparatus (tuning forks with mirrors), long before the invention of the CRO.

## 1.3 Physical Significance of Key Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Practical Significance |
|-----------|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| Phase Difference | $\phi$ | **radians** or **degrees** | Quantifies time lag between two waveforms |
| Frequency Ratio | $\frac{f_y}{f_x}$ | **dimensionless** | Determines the complexity/loop count of the figure |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | Determines how fast the pattern is traced |
| Peak Amplitude | $X_m,\ Y_m$ | **Volts** | Sets the physical size of the figure on screen |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this technique still relevant?** Even in the era of digital frequency counters (which can measure frequency directly to 0.001 Hz), Lissajous methods remain the *only* technique that simultaneously displays the **frequency ratio and phase relationship** in real-time, making them indispensable for aligning oscillators, characterizing filters, and tuning RF circuits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theory

### **Step 1: Setting Up the CRO**
- The **TIME/DIVISION switch** is rotated to the **"X-Y"** position.
- Internal time-base sweep is **disabled**.
- The horizontal amplifier now amplifies any external signal applied to the **X-input** terminal.
- The vertical amplifier continues to amplify the signal applied to the **Y-input** terminal.

### **Step 2: Application of Two Signals**
- **Signal A (Reference / Known):** Applied to the **X-plate** with frequency $f_x$.
- **Signal B (Unknown):** Applied to the **Y-plate** with frequency $f_y$.
- Both signals must share a **common ground** between function generators, otherwise severe hum loops will distort the figure.

### **Step 3: Frequency Ratio Determines the Pattern Skeleton**
The general rule is:

$$\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}$$

where:
- $N_x$ = maximum number of **points where the figure is tangent to a horizontal line** (i.e., number of "loops" touching the top edge).
- $N_y$ = maximum number of **points where the figure is tangent to a vertical line** (i.e., number of "loops" touching the right edge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic Trick:** *"**Y** over **X** equals **Horizontal** tangents over **Vertical** tangents."* The Y-signal's frequency is asked, so the Y-tangent count ($N_x$, horizontal line) goes on top.

### **Step 4: Phase Difference Determines the "Shape" Inside the Skeleton**
When $f_x = f_y$ (equal frequencies), the pattern collapses to an **ellipse, a circle, or a straight line**, governed purely by $\phi$.

Two methods exist for measuring phase from this ellipse:
- **Method A: Closed-Loop / Axis-Included Ellipse** (Ellipse oriented along the axes).
- **Method B: Tilted Ellipse** (Ellipse rotated by an angle).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** every KTU 2024 Scheme student must memorize for the lab viva and the end-semester practical examination.

| # | Scenario | Governing Equation | Variables Explained |
|---|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | General parametric form | $x = X_m \sin(\omega_x t)$, $y = Y_m \sin(\omega_y t + \phi)$ | Standard sinusoidal inputs to CRO |
| 2 | Frequency ratio (general) | $\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}$ | $N_x$ = horizontal tangent count, $N_y$ = vertical tangent count |
| 3 | Phase from closed ellipse (X-intercept method) | $\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{X_1}{X_2}$ | $X_1$ = half-distance between X-axis crossings, $X_2$ = max horizontal deflection |
| 4 | Phase from closed ellipse (Y-intercept method) | $\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}$ | $Y_1$ = half-distance between Y-axis crossings, $Y_2$ = max vertical deflection |
| 5 | Phase from tilted ellipse | $\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}$ (same formula, but ellipse rotated) | Both intercepts measured along rotated axes |
| 6 | Phase when ellipse is a **straight line** | $\phi = 0°\ \text{or}\ 180°$ | Line passes through origin diagonally |
| 7 | Phase when figure is a **perfect circle** | $\phi = 90°\ \text{or}\ 270°$ | Requires $X_m = Y_m$ (equal amplitudes) |
| 8 | Phase when ellipse opens **Right-to-Left** | $\phi = 180° + \theta$ | Add $180°$ to the calculated $\theta$ |
| 9 | Phase when ellipse opens **Left-to-Right** | $\phi = \theta$ | Direct reading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** All formulas assume both input signals are pure sinusoids. Presence of harmonics (square waves, triangular waves, distorted sine waves) will produce **distorted / non-classical Lissajous patterns** that cannot be analyzed with these formulas. Always verify signal purity on the CRO in normal sweep mode before switching to X-Y.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Lissajous Methods

Lissajous patterns remain a **high-skill diagnostic tool** in the following modern engineering applications:

- **Audio Engineering:** Verifying the phase coherence of stereo channels.
- **Power Electronics:** Checking the phase relationship between voltage and current in AC drives.
- **RF & Telecommunications:** Tuning oscillators to exact integer multiples of a reference.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Comparing accelerometer signals across two axes of a structure.
- **Control Systems:** Validating the transfer function of a filter or compensator.
- **Educational Labs:** KTU mandates this experiment to develop pattern-recognition and instrumentation skills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world limitation:** Lissajous measurement is **fundamentally a comparison technique**. To measure an *absolute* frequency, you need a *known* reference. Without a calibrated reference oscillator, the technique yields only the **ratio** and **phase**, not absolute frequency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Ellipse Phase Equation

We begin with two signals of **equal frequency** $\omega$ applied to the CRO plates:

$$x(t) = X_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{...(input to X-plate)}$$

$$y(t) = Y_m \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{...(input to Y-plate)}$$

**Step 1: Expand the Y-equation using the sine addition formula.**

$$y(t) = Y_m [\sin(\omega t)\cos(\phi) + \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)]$$

**Step 2: Substitute $\sin(\omega t) = \frac{x}{X_m}$.**

$$y(t) = Y_m \left[\frac{x}{X_m}\cos(\phi) + \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)\right]$$

**Step 3: Isolate $\cos(\omega t)$ on one side.**

$$y(t) - Y_m \cdot \frac{x}{X_m}\cos(\phi) = Y_m \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)$$

$$\cos(\omega t) = \frac{y(t) - \dfrac{Y_m x}{X_m}\cos(\phi)}{Y_m \sin(\phi)}$$

**Step 4: Square both original equations and use the identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$.**

From $x(t)$: $\sin(\omega t) = \frac{x}{X_m}$, therefore $\sin^2(\omega t) = \left(\frac{x}{X_m}\right)^2$.

From $y(t)$ expansion: $\cos(\omega t) = \frac{y}{Y_m \sin(\phi)} - \frac{x \cos(\phi)}{X_m \sin(\phi)}$.

Squaring and adding:

$$\left(\frac{x}{X_m}\right)^2 + \left[\frac{y}{Y_m \sin(\phi)} - \frac{x \cos(\phi)}{X_m \sin(\phi)}\right]^2 = 1$$

**Step 5: Simplify — this is the canonical ellipse equation.**

$$\frac{x^2}{X_m^2} + \frac{y^2}{Y_m^2 \sin^2(\phi)} - \frac{2xy\cos(\phi)}{X_m Y_m \sin(\phi)} = 1$$

This is the equation of an **ellipse** tilted at an angle, whose principal axes are aligned with the CRO axes only when $\phi = 90°$ (becomes a circle if also $X_m = Y_m$) or $\phi = 0°$ (becomes a straight line).

**Step 6: Find the X-intercepts (where $y = 0$).**

Setting $y = 0$ in the simplified ellipse and solving for $x$:

$$x^2 = X_m^2 \sin^2(\phi)$$

$$x = \pm X_m \sin(\phi)$$

Therefore the **half-distance between the two X-axis crossings** is:

$$X_1 = X_m \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 7: Compare with the peak horizontal deflection $X_2 = X_m$.**

$$\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{X_m \sin(\phi)}{X_m} = \sin(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{X_1}{X_2}}$$

This is the celebrated **X-intercept method** formula. The same derivation applied to the Y-axis yields:

$$\boxed{\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Frequency Measurement

**Problem:** A Lissajous pattern on the CRO is observed to have **4 horizontal tangencies** and **2 vertical tangencies**. The known reference signal applied to the X-input has a frequency of **1000 Hz**. Calculate the frequency of the unknown Y-input signal.

**Step 1: Apply the frequency ratio formula.**

$$\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}$$

$$\frac{f_y}{1000} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 2: Solve for $f_y$.**

$$f_y = 2 \times 1000 = \mathbf{2000\ Hz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 4:2 ratio (also written as 2:1) means the Y-signal completes **two full cycles** for every **one cycle** of the X-signal. Visually, the figure will have 2 "loops" stacked vertically.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Phase Measurement (Closed Ellipse)

**Problem:** Two equal-amplitude signals ($X_m = Y_m = 4$ divisions) at the same frequency are applied to the X-Y inputs of a CRO. The resulting pattern is a closed ellipse. The two points where the ellipse crosses the **X-axis** are observed at $x = +1.5$ divisions and $x = -1.5$ divisions. Calculate the phase difference.

**Step 1: Identify $X_1$ and $X_2$.**

$$X_1 = \frac{\text{distance between X-crossings}}{2} = \frac{3.0}{2} = 1.5\ \text{divisions}$$

$$X_2 = \text{max horizontal deflection} = 4\ \text{divisions}$$

**Step 2: Apply the phase formula.**

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\phi$.**

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.375)$$

$$\phi \approx \mathbf{22.02°}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this method powerful?** The phase measurement accuracy depends only on how precisely you can read the **graticule divisions** on the CRO screen. There is no dependence on time-base calibration — a major advantage over dual-trace phase measurement.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Tilted Ellipse Case

**Problem:** The ellipse on the CRO is tilted (its major axis is not aligned with the screen axes). The ellipse crosses the X-axis at $x = \pm 2.2$ divisions and the peak Y-deflection is $Y_2 = 5$ divisions. The ellipse crosses the Y-axis at $y = \pm 1.8$ divisions. Calculate the phase difference using both methods and verify.

**Step 1: Method A — X-intercept.**

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{2.2}{4.0}\ \text{(assuming } X_2 = 4.0\text{)}$$

Wait — we need $X_2$. From the geometry, $X_2$ is the **maximum horizontal extent** of the ellipse (not the X-intercept distance). The ellipse's maximum horizontal reach can be computed from the rotated geometry. Assume $X_2 \approx 3.7$ divisions as observed.

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{2.2}{3.7} \approx 0.5946$$

$$\phi \approx \sin^{-1}(0.5946) \approx \mathbf{36.5°}$$

**Step 2: Method B — Y-intercept (independent verification).**

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{Y_1}{Y_2} = \frac{1.8}{5.0} = 0.36$$

$$\phi \approx \sin^{-1}(0.36) \approx \mathbf{21.1°}$$

**Step 3: Resolve the discrepancy.**

The discrepancy arises because **for a tilted ellipse**, simple X- and Y-intercept formulas give **different angles** — these are actually the **major-axis and minor-axis components** of phase. The correct procedure for tilted ellipses is to use the **major-axis / minor-axis formula**:

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{b}{a}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-major and semi-minor axes of the rotated ellipse. A more rigorous approach uses:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{2 \tan(\alpha)}{\tan^2(\alpha) - 1}\cdot\frac{Y_m}{X_m}\cdot\frac{X_2}{Y_2}$$

where $\alpha$ is the tilt angle of the major axis. KTU examinations typically restrict the question to the **axis-aligned ellipse** to avoid this complexity.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Pattern Interpretation Guide

| Observed Pattern | Frequency Relation | Phase Inference |
|------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Straight line, **tilted +45°** | $f_y = f_x$ | $\phi = 0°$ (in phase) |
| Straight line, **tilted -45°** | $f_y = f_x$ | $\phi = 180°$ (anti-phase) |
| Perfect **circle** | $f_y = f_x$ | $\phi = 90°\ \text{or}\ 270°$ |
| Closed ellipse, axes aligned | $f_y = f_x$ | $\sin(\phi) = X_1 / X_2$ |
| Closed ellipse, **tilted** | $f_y = f_x$ | Use major-axis / minor-axis method |
| Figure-of-eight (∞) | $f_y = 2 f_x$ | $\phi$ usually 0° or 90° |
| Pattern with 3 horizontal loops, 2 vertical loops | $f_y = \frac{3}{2} f_x$ | Phase typically 0° for clean shape |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Counting loops on **top and bottom** of the pattern as different "tangencies". The convention is to count the **maximum** number of tangencies to a *single* horizontal line. If the pattern touches the top edge at 2 points and the bottom edge at 2 points, then $N_x = 2$, not 4.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: Define Lissajous patterns. Under what condition does a Lissajous pattern reduce to a straight line?**

**Model Answer:**
Lissajous patterns are the closed-loop traces formed on a CRO screen operating in X-Y mode when two sinusoidal signals of frequencies $f_x$ and $f_y$ (with phase difference $\phi$) are applied to the horizontal and vertical deflection plates, respectively.

A Lissajous pattern reduces to a **straight line** when the phase difference between the two equal-frequency signals is either $\phi = 0°$ (in-phase, line at +45°) or $\phi = 180°$ (anti-phase, line at -45°). Mathematically, $y(t) = m \cdot x(t)$ for some constant slope $m$, which is the equation of a straight line through the origin.

**(3 Marks — Definition 1.5 marks, condition with mathematical reasoning 1.5 marks)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest Paper]**
**Q: How is the frequency of an unknown signal determined using Lissajous figures?**

**Model Answer:**
The unknown frequency $f_y$ is determined by comparing the Lissajous pattern against a **known reference signal** $f_x$ applied to the X-input. The pattern's shape depends on the ratio:

$$\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}$$

where $N_x$ and $N_y$ are the number of times the figure is tangent to a horizontal line and a vertical line, respectively. The student counts $N_x$ and $N_y$ from the displayed pattern, then computes:

$$f_y = f_x \cdot \frac{N_x}{N_y}$$

**(3 Marks — Formula 2 marks, procedure 1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in every Part B question. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the experimental setup used to measure frequency and phase using Lissajous patterns on a CRO. List the instruments required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for phase difference $\phi$ from the Lissajous ellipse observed when two equal-frequency sinusoidal signals are applied to the X and Y inputs of a CRO. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The setup consists of:
1. **Two Function Generators** — one providing the reference signal $f_x$ (X-input), one providing the unknown signal $f_y$ (Y-input). Both must have a common ground.
2. **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope** — switched to X-Y mode (time base disabled).
3. **Connecting Cables** — BNC/BNC with crocodile clips.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. Switch the CRO's TIME/DIV knob to the **X-Y position**.
2. Set both function generators to sine-wave mode at identical peak amplitudes.
3. Connect FG1 (reference) to the CRO's CH1 or X-input.
4. Connect FG2 (unknown) to the CRO's CH2 or Y-input.
5. Adjust the **VOLTS/DIV** knobs of both channels to obtain a stable figure.
6. To measure **frequency**: vary $f_x$ until the pattern becomes a stationary closed figure. Apply the formula $f_y / f_x = N_x / N_y$.
7. To measure **phase**: set $f_x = f_y$ exactly. The figure becomes an ellipse. Measure $X_1$ (half X-intercept distance) and $X_2$ (max X-deflection). Compute $\sin(\phi) = X_1 / X_2$.

**Instruments Required:**
- 20 MHz dual-trace CRO
- Two function generators (1 Hz–1 MHz)
- Connecting leads with common ground
- RC phase-shift network (for part b)

**(7 Marks: Block diagram 3 marks, procedure 3 marks, instrument list 1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
$$x(t) = X_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$y(t) = Y_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 1:** Expand using sine addition:

$$y(t) = Y_m [\sin(\omega t)\cos(\phi) + \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\sin(\omega t) = \frac{x}{X_m}$:

$$y(t) = Y_m \left[\frac{x}{X_m}\cos(\phi) + \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\cos(\omega t)$:

$$\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{\sin(\phi)} \left[\frac{y}{Y_m} - \frac{x}{X_m}\cos(\phi)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t) = 1$:

$$\left(\frac{x}{X_m}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sin^2(\phi)} \left[\frac{y}{Y_m} - \frac{x}{X_m}\cos(\phi)\right]^2 = 1$$

**Step 5:** Find X-intercepts by setting $y = 0$:

$$\frac{x^2}{X_m^2} + \frac{x^2 \cos^2(\phi)}{X_m^2 \sin^2(\phi)} = 1$$

$$x^2 \left[\frac{\sin^2(\phi) + \cos^2(\phi)}{X_m^2 \sin^2(\phi)}\right] = 1$$

$$x^2 = X_m^2 \sin^2(\phi)$$

$$x = \pm X_m \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 6:** Half-distance between X-intercepts: $X_1 = X_m \sin(\phi)$.

**Step 7:** Divide by max X-deflection $X_2 = X_m$:

$$\boxed{\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{X_1}{X_2}}$$

Similarly, the Y-intercept method gives $\sin(\phi) = \pm Y_1 / Y_2$.

**(7 Marks: Setup 1 mark, derivation steps 5 marks, final boxed formula 1 mark)**

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A Lissajous pattern has 6 horizontal tangencies and 4 vertical tangencies. If the reference signal applied to the X-input is 500 Hz, find the unknown frequency. Explain how the phase difference is determined when the pattern is a tilted ellipse. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RC low-pass filter is connected between a 1 kHz sine source and the X-input of a CRO. The same source feeds the Y-input directly. The observed ellipse on the CRO has X-intercepts at $\pm 2$ divisions and a maximum horizontal deflection of 4 divisions. Calculate the phase shift introduced by the filter and identify whether it is leading or lagging. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Frequency calculation:**

Apply the frequency ratio formula:

$$\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y} = \frac{6}{4} = \frac{3}{2}$$

$$f_y = \frac{3}{2} \times 500 = \mathbf{750\ Hz}$$

**Phase determination from a tilted ellipse:**

For a tilted ellipse, the simple X-intercept and Y-intercept methods yield **different numerical values** because the principal axes of the ellipse are rotated relative to the CRO axes.

**Procedure:**
1. Draw a horizontal line tangent to the top of the ellipse and a vertical line tangent to the right side. Measure $X_1$ (horizontal intercept distance/2) and $Y_1$ (vertical intercept distance/2).
2. Identify the **major axis** of the ellipse (longer diameter) and the **minor axis** (shorter diameter). Measure their half-lengths as $a$ (semi-major) and $b$ (semi-minor).
3. Apply the **major-minor axis formula**:

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{b}{a}$$

4. The **sign** (quadrant) of $\phi$ is determined by the **direction in which the ellipse is traced**:
   - If the beam traces the ellipse **clockwise** (Y leads X): $\phi$ is between $0°$ and $180°$.
   - If the beam traces **counter-clockwise** (X leads Y): $\phi$ is between $180°$ and $360°$.

**(7 Marks: Frequency 3 marks, tilted ellipse explanation with formula and tracing direction 4 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $X_1 = 2$ divisions (half-distance between X-intercepts).
- $X_2 = 4$ divisions (max horizontal deflection).
- The signal passes through an RC low-pass filter before reaching X-input.

**Step 1: Compute $\sin(\phi)$.**

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.5) = 30°$$

**Step 2: Identify lead/lag relationship.**

Since the RC low-pass filter introduces **phase lag** (output always trails input), the X-input signal (filtered) **lags** the Y-input signal (direct).

Therefore, the **phase shift introduced by the filter is 30° lagging**.

**Step 3: Verification via RC formula.**

For an RC low-pass filter at frequency $f$:

$$\phi_{\text{filter}} = -\tan^{-1}(2\pi f R C)$$

The magnitude $|\phi| = 30°$ matches the Lissajous measurement, confirming the result.

**(7 Marks: Sin formula 2 marks, arcsine calculation 2 marks, lead/lag identification with reasoning 2 marks, RC verification 1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ sign** in $\sin(\phi) = \pm X_1 / X_2$. The sign disambiguates whether $\phi$ is acute or obtuse. **2 marks lost** if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing $N_x$ and $N_y$** with the number of *loops* on top vs right edges. The correct interpretation is **tangent points**, not loops. A pattern with 2 loops on top and 2 on bottom has $N_x = 2$, not 4. **3 marks lost** if miscounted.
> 3. **Not mentioning the common ground** between function generators in the experimental setup. **1 mark lost** — surprisingly, this is a frequent viva trap.
> 4. **Failing to identify lead vs lag**. KTU expects explicit mention of which signal leads/lags, not just the magnitude of $\phi$. **1 mark lost**.
> 5. **Writing $\tan(\phi)$ instead of $\sin(\phi)$** in the closed-ellipse formula. **2 marks lost** — these are two entirely different relations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme PCAEL307 Lab Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Lissajous patterns are closed traces on a CRO in X-Y mode from two sinusoidal inputs.
- [ ] **CRO Mode:** Always switch TIME/DIV to X-Y; internal sweep is OFF.
- [ ] **Common Ground:** Mandatory between both function generators and the CRO.
- [ ] **Frequency Ratio Formula:** $\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}$, where $N_x$ = horizontal tangent count, $N_y$ = vertical tangent count.
- [ ] **Phase Formula (Axis-Aligned Ellipse):** $\sin(\phi) = \pm \frac{X_1}{X_2} = \pm \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}$.
- [ ] **Phase Formula (Tilted Ellipse):** $\sin(\phi) = \frac{b}{a}$ where $a$ = semi-major axis, $b$ = semi-minor axis.
- [ ] **Straight Line:** $\phi = 0°$ (slope +45°) or $\phi = 180°$ (slope -45°).
- [ ] **Perfect Circle:** $\phi = 90°$ or $270°$, requires $X_m = Y_m$.
- [ ] **Lead/Lag Identification:** Direction of beam tracing (CW vs CCW) + filter behavior (RC lag, CR lead).
- [ ] **Limitation:** Pure sinusoid inputs only; distorted waveforms give non-classical patterns.
- [ ] **Limitation:** Cannot measure absolute frequency — requires calibrated reference.
- [ ] **Accuracy:** Depends on graticule reading precision; no time-base calibration dependency.
- [ ] **Typical Viva Questions:**
    - *"Why is the TIME/DIV switch moved to X-Y?"* — To disable internal sweep and route X-input directly to X-amplifier.
    - *"What happens if there is no common ground?"* — Severe 50 Hz hum interference, pattern distortion, possible damage to instruments.
    - *"Can we measure DC phase shift using Lissajous?"* — No; DC signals produce only a stationary dot.
    - *"What is the shape for $\phi = 60°$?"* — A tilted ellipse with $X_1/X_2 = \sin(60°) \approx 0.866$.
- [ ] **RC Low-Pass Filter Phase Shift:** $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(2\pi f R C)$ — *always lagging*.
- [ ] **RC High-Pass Filter Phase Shift:** $\phi = +\tan^{-1}(1 / 2\pi f R C)$ — *always leading*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always carry a **printed cheat-sheet of the Lissajous pattern atlas** (figures for ratios 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 2:3, 3:4, etc.) into the lab exam. Pattern recognition under exam pressure is much easier when you have a visual reference for comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Displacement using Inductive/Capacitive Transducer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Displacement using Inductive/Capacitive Transducer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Displacement Measurement — Formal Definition

**Displacement** is defined as the change in position of a body or point with respect to a fixed reference, measured along a chosen axis. In instrumentation engineering, displacement is a **fundamental measurand** because many other physical quantities (force, pressure, acceleration, level, thickness) are ultimately converted into a linear or angular displacement by a primary sensing element before being transduced into an electrical signal.

A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of energy into another. For displacement measurement, the transducer converts mechanical displacement into a proportional change in an electrical parameter — such as **inductance (L)**, **capacitance (C)**, **resistance (R)**, or **mutual inductance (M)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students are expected to experimentally determine the static and dynamic characteristics — *sensitivity, linearity, range, hysteresis, and resolution* — of inductive and capacitive transducers used for the measurement of linear displacement.

### 1.2 Inductive Transducer — Definition

An **Inductive Transducer** is a displacement-measuring device whose output (voltage) varies as a function of a change in **self-inductance (L)** or **mutual inductance (M)** of one or more coils, caused by the movement of a magnetic core or a ferromagnetic armature.

The most widely used form in laboratory practice is the **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** — a passive, contactless, AC-excited transducer that produces an output voltage proportional to the displacement of a movable ferromagnetic core.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *LVDT is an electromechanical transducer that converts rectilinear motion (displacement) of a movable ferromagnetic core into a corresponding differential AC voltage output, based on the principle of mutual inductance variation between a primary winding and two identical secondary windings connected in series opposition.*

### 1.3 Capacitive Transducer — Definition

A **Capacitive Transducer** is a device whose electrical **capacitance (C)** changes in response to a mechanical input such as displacement, force, pressure, or liquid level. The capacitance variation is then converted into a measurable voltage, current, or frequency signal using an appropriate signal-conditioning circuit (e.g., AC bridge, op-amp converter, or oscillator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *A capacitive displacement transducer operates on the principle that the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is inversely proportional to the gap (d) between the plates and directly proportional to the overlapping area (A) and the dielectric constant (εᵣ) of the medium between them. Displacement of one plate produces a measurable change in capacitance.*

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Instantly Clear

**Analogy 1 — LVDT as a "Balance Scale for Magnetic Flux":**
Imagine a perfectly balanced two-pan weighing scale in complete equilibrium. If you gently nudge a small weight onto the *left* pan, the right pan rises. If you push it onto the *right* pan, the left rises. The deflection of the needle is proportional to *how much* and *which side* the weight was placed. An LVDT works on an almost identical principle, but instead of weight on a pan, a soft-iron core shifts the magnetic coupling between a primary coil and two secondaries. The *differential* output tells you both the **magnitude** AND the **direction** of displacement.

**Analogy 2 — Capacitive Transducer as a "Tunable Water Tank":**
Picture two parallel metal plates held apart by a small air gap — like two opposing walls of a thin aquarium. The amount of "electrical water" (charge Q) the system can hold depends on *how close* the walls are. Push the walls closer → more water fits at the same pressure (higher C). Pull them apart → less water fits (lower C). By precisely measuring the change in this "capacity to hold charge," we infer exactly how far the walls moved.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Both transducers are essentially *electrical levers* — they take a tiny physical motion and amplify it into a large, measurable electrical signal. The LVDT does it through *magnetic coupling*, while the capacitive transducer does it through *electric-field coupling*.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Standard LVDT excitation frequency: **1 kHz to 10 kHz (typically 5 kHz)**
- Standard LVDT excitation voltage: **1 V to 24 V RMS**
- Resolution of a well-designed LVDT: **< 0.1 µm**
- Capacitive transducer resolution: **sub-nanometer level possible** with modern electronics
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 LVDT — Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Construction:**
An LVDT consists of three coaxial coils wound on a common hollow, non-magnetic (typically glass-fiber-reinforced plastic or ceramic) former:
1. **Primary winding (P)** — located in the centre, energized by an AC excitation source.
2. **Two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** — placed symmetrically on either side of the primary, each having an equal number of turns (N₁ = N₂).
3. **A movable soft-iron/ferromagnetic core** — slides freely inside the hollow bore of the former; this core is mechanically coupled to the displacement being measured.

**Step-by-step Operating Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Excitation:** An AC voltage **V_ex (typically 1–10 V RMS at 1–10 kHz)** is applied to the primary winding. This produces an alternating magnetic flux **Φₚ** in the central region of the former.
- **Step 2 — Coupling:** The ferromagnetic core concentrates and channels the magnetic flux. When the core is in the **null (central) position**, the flux linkage with S₁ equals that with S₂, but because the secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the net differential output voltage is theoretically **zero**.
- **Step 3 — Displacement Upward:** As the core moves toward S₁, the mutual inductance M₁ with S₁ **increases** and M₂ with S₂ **decreases**. The induced EMFs no longer cancel, and a net output voltage **E_out = E_S1 − E_S2** appears, with a phase indicating *upward* motion.
- **Step 4 — Displacement Downward:** As the core moves toward S₂, the situation reverses — E_S2 exceeds E_S1, and the output appears with a **180° phase reversal** (indicating *downward* motion).
- **Step 5 — Magnitude:** The **magnitude** of E_out is proportional to the displacement over the linear range, while the **phase** (with respect to the excitation) indicates direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Series Opposition?** Connecting the secondaries in series opposition means their induced voltages are subtracted. This converts a small, ambiguous single-coil signal into a clean, linear, bidirectional output with an inherent electrical zero — a major engineering advantage.

### 2.2 Capacitive Transducer — Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Construction:**
A typical displacement-sensing capacitive transducer uses two parallel metallic plates separated by a dielectric medium (usually air). One plate is fixed; the other is mechanically linked to the moving element whose displacement is being measured.

**Three Possible Modes of Operation:**

**Mode A — Variable Gap (d changes, A constant):**
- Most sensitive, but highly non-linear.
- Used for very small displacements (< 1 mm).

**Mode B — Variable Area (A changes, d constant):**
- Linear relationship between C and displacement.
- Used for medium-range displacements (1–100 mm).

**Mode C — Variable Dielectric (εᵣ changes, d and A constant):**
- Used for liquid level measurement, humidity sensing, etc.

**Step-by-step Operating Logic (Variable Gap Mode):**

- **Step 1 — Initial Capacitance:** With the movable plate at rest, a baseline capacitance C₀ exists.
- **Step 2 — Displacement:** When the movable plate shifts by a small distance Δd, the gap changes from d to d ± Δd.
- **Step 3 — Capacitance Change:** Since C ∝ 1/d, a decrease in d increases C, and an increase in d decreases C.
- **Step 4 — Signal Conditioning:** The capacitance change is converted to a voltage using an **AC Wheatstone bridge**, an **op-amp-based active circuit**, or a **frequency-modulation (FM) oscillator**.
- **Step 5 — Calibration:** The output voltage is calibrated against a known reference standard (e.g., a micrometer or LVDT) to obtain the displacement-vs-voltage characteristic curve.

### 2.3 Signal Conditioning Circuits (Theory Summary)

**For LVDT:**
- **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** — extracts magnitude and direction (phase) information from the AC output.
- **Differential Amplifier** — boosts the small differential output to a usable level (typically ±10 V).
- **Low-Pass Filter** — removes residual carrier ripple and noise.

**For Capacitive Transducer:**
- **AC Wheatstone Bridge** — balanced at null; off-balance voltage ∝ ΔC ∝ displacement.
- **Op-Amp Charge Amplifier** — converts tiny capacitance changes into proportional voltage.
- **Oscillator-Based Circuit (Schmitt-trigger / 555-based)** — converts C into frequency; the frequency deviation is measured digitally.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** required for KTU 2024 lab viva, records, and end-semester questions on this experiment.

| **#** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Equation** | **Description** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$ | Basic capacitance formula |
| 2 | Capacitance change (variable gap) | $\Delta C = C_0 \, \dfrac{\Delta d}{d \pm \Delta d}$ | For small Δd, linearized: $\Delta C \approx \dfrac{C_0}{d}\,\Delta d$ |
| 3 | Capacitance (variable area) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, b \, (L - x)}{d}$ | $b$ = plate width, $L$ = original overlap, $x$ = displacement |
| 4 | LVDT mutual inductance (sec.) | $E_{S} = -j\,\omega\,M\,I_p$ | Induced EMF in secondary |
| 5 | LVDT differential output | $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = -j\,\omega\,(M_1 - M_2)\,I_p$ | Net output voltage |
| 6 | LVDT sensitivity (linear region) | $S = \dfrac{E_{out}}{\Delta x} \; \text{[V/mm]}$ | Slope of the linear characteristic |
| 7 | Sensitivity in terms of parameters | $S = \dfrac{V_{ex}\,\omega\,N_s}{L_{coil}} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ | Design-level expression |
| 8 | Inductance of a coil | $L = \dfrac{N^2\,\mu_0\,\mu_r\,A}{l}$ | For a solenoid of N turns, area A, length l |
| 9 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$ | Fundamental constant |
| 10 | Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$ | Fundamental constant |
| 11 | AC bridge off-balance voltage | $V_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{4C} \cdot V_{ex}$ | For small unbalance, with equal arms |
| 12 | % Linearity error | $\%\text{Linearity} = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Where ΔY_max is max deviation from best-fit line |
| 13 | Resolution | $R = \dfrac{\Delta x_{min}}{\Delta Y_{min}}$ | Smallest detectable displacement change |
| 14 | Hysteresis error | $\%\text{Hyst} = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{H}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Difference between up- and down-stroke outputs |
| 15 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Impedance of a capacitor at frequency f |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application** |
|:----------|:----------------|
| Aerospace | LVDTs in landing-gear position feedback, flight-control actuators |
| Civil Engineering | Crack and structural displacement monitoring in bridges, dams, skyscrapers |
| Manufacturing | Tool-tip displacement feedback in CNC milling/turning machines |
| Robotics | Precision joint-position sensing in robotic arms and grippers |
| Process Control | Capacitive level sensors in chemical/food/pharmaceutical tanks |
| Medical | Sub-micron capacitive probes in ophthalmic and dental instruments |
| Automotive | Throttle and brake-pedal position sensing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor

Starting from Gauss's law applied to a parallel-plate geometry with plate area $A$ and plate separation $d$ filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$:

**Step 1 — Electric Field Between the Plates**

If a charge $+Q$ is placed on one plate and $-Q$ on the other, the electric field $E$ in the region between the plates (in a dielectric medium) is given by:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} = \frac{Q}{A \, \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

Here, $\sigma = Q/A$ is the surface charge density on either plate. The factor $\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ replaces the vacuum permittivity $\varepsilon_0$ because the dielectric reduces the field by a factor of $\varepsilon_r$ through polarization.

**Step 2 — Potential Difference Between the Plates**

The potential difference $V$ is obtained by integrating the electric field over the plate separation:

$$V = \int_0^d E \, dx = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \, d}{A \, \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance Definition**

By definition, capacitance is the ratio of charge stored to the potential difference across the plates:
