$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q \, d}{A \, \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}} = \frac{A \, \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{d}$$

Therefore, the **fundamental capacitance formula** is:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A larger plate area $A$ holds more charge (higher $C$); a larger separation $d$ stores less charge at the same voltage (lower $C$); a higher-permittivity dielectric (larger $\varepsilon_r$) increases $C$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Change in Capacitance Due to Displacement (Variable Gap Mode)

**Step 1 — Initial Capacitance**

With the movable plate at its nominal (rest) position, the gap is $d$, and the initial capacitance is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance After Displacement**

When the movable plate is displaced by a small distance $x$ toward the fixed plate, the new gap is $d - x$ (assuming $x < d$). The new capacitance becomes:

$$C_x = \frac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d - x}$$

**Step 3 — Change in Capacitance**

The change in capacitance is the difference between the new and initial capacitances:

$$\Delta C = C_x - C_0 = \varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A \left[ \frac{1}{d - x} - \frac{1}{d} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification**

Bring the two fractions to a common denominator:

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A \left[ \frac{d - (d - x)}{d(d - x)} \right] = \varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A \left[ \frac{x}{d(d - x)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of C₀**

Substitute $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \, \varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$, which gives $\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A = C_0 d$. Plugging this in:

$$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \, d \cdot x}{d(d - x)} = \frac{C_0 \, x}{d - x}$$

**Step 6 — Linearized Form (For Small x ≪ d)**

When $x \ll d$, the term $d - x \approx d$, and we obtain the **linearized sensitivity relation**:

$$\boxed{\Delta C \approx \frac{C_0}{d} \cdot x}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For small displacements, the change in capacitance is **linearly proportional to the displacement** $x$. This is the basis of the small-signal linear operating region of the capacitive transducer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LVDT Output Voltage Equation

**Step 1 — Mutual Inductance Coupling**

The primary winding $P$ carries an excitation current $I_p = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$, which produces a primary magnetic flux $\Phi_p$ that links the two secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ with mutual inductances $M_1(x)$ and $M_2(x)$ respectively. These mutual inductances depend on the position $x$ of the movable core.

**Step 2 — EMF Induced in Each Secondary**

By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the EMF induced in each secondary is:

$$E_{S1} = -M_1(x) \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt} = -j\omega M_1(x) \, I_p$$

$$E_{S2} = -M_2(x) \cdot \frac{dI_p}{dt} = -j\omega M_2(x) \, I_p$$

The complex notation $-j\omega$ is used because we are dealing with sinusoidal steady-state AC phasors.

**Step 3 — Differential Output (Series-Opposition Connection)**

Because the two secondaries are connected in series opposition, the net differential output voltage is:

$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = -j\omega I_p \left[ M_1(x) - M_2(x) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Symmetry Condition at Null Position**

At the geometric centre (null position, $x = 0$), the core is equidistant from $S_1$ and $S_2$, and by symmetry:

$$M_1(0) = M_2(0) = M_0$$

Therefore, at the null position:

$$E_{out}(x=0) = -j\omega I_p (M_0 - M_0) = 0$$

This confirms the **electrical null** at the central position — a critical design feature of the LVDT.

**Step 5 — Linear Region Approximation**

For small displacements $|x| \ll L/2$ (where $L$ is the length of each secondary coil), the difference in mutual inductances is approximately linear in $x$:

$$M_1(x) - M_2(x) = k \cdot x$$

where $k$ is a transducer constant with units of H/m. Substituting:

$$\boxed{E_{out}(x) = -j\omega I_p \, k \cdot x}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The output voltage is **(a)** linear in displacement, **(b)** proportional to the excitation frequency, **(c)** proportional to the excitation current, and **(d)** the **phase** of $-j$ (i.e., a 90° lag relative to $I_p$ when $x > 0$, and a 90° lead when $x < 0$) gives the **direction** of displacement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — AC Bridge Off-Balance Voltage for Capacitive Transducer

**Step 1 — Bridge Topology**

A standard **AC Wheatstone bridge** for capacitive measurement uses two fixed resistors $R_1, R_2$ and the unknown capacitance $C_x$ along with a balancing capacitor $C_0$ in the other arm. The bridge is excited by an AC source $V_{ex}$.

**Step 2 — Balance Condition**

At balance, no current flows through the detector, and the impedance ratio condition is satisfied:

$$\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = \frac{Z_3}{Z_4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{1/(j\omega C_0)}{1/(j\omega C_x)} = \frac{C_x}{C_0}$$

**Step 3 — Off-Balance Output**

When the capacitance changes from $C_0$ to $C_0 + \Delta C$, the bridge becomes unbalanced. The off-balance detector voltage is approximately:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{\Delta C}{4 C_0} \cdot V_{ex}$$

This is valid under the **small-signal assumption** $|\Delta C| \ll C_0$.

**Step 4 — Sensitivity Expression**

Bridge sensitivity with respect to displacement $x$:

$$S_{bridge} = \frac{\partial V_{out}}{\partial x} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4 C_0} \cdot \frac{\partial (\Delta C)}{\partial x} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4 d}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the excitation voltage doubles the output voltage — a strong reason for using higher $V_{ex}$ in industrial bridges. The sensitivity is **inversely proportional to the gap $d$** — smaller gaps yield higher sensitivity.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (LVDT Calibration)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Question:** An LVDT is excited by a 5 V RMS, 5 kHz source. The sensitivity is specified as 0.5 V/mm. Calculate the output voltage for a displacement of (a) +2 mm and (b) −3 mm.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** $E_{out} = S \cdot x = 0.5 \times (+2) = +1.0 \; \text{V RMS}$

**Part (b):** $E_{out} = S \cdot x = 0.5 \times (-3) = -1.5 \; \text{V RMS}$ (with 180° phase shift)

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Capacitive Transducer Sensitivity)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Question:** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plate area $A = 25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$, gap $d = 1 \; \text{mm}$, and air dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Calculate (a) the initial capacitance, (b) the change in capacitance for a displacement of 0.1 mm, and (c) the change in capacitance for a displacement of 0.5 mm (using the exact formula, not linearized).

**Part (a):** Initial Capacitance

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{(1)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(25 \times 10^{-4})}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-3}} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{F} \approx 22.1 \; \text{pF}$$

**Part (b):** Change in Capacitance for x = 0.1 mm (linearized)

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{C_0}{d} \cdot x = \frac{22.1 \; \text{pF}}{1 \; \text{mm}} \times 0.1 \; \text{mm} = 2.21 \; \text{pF}$$

**Part (c):** Change in Capacitance for x = 0.5 mm (exact)

$$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \cdot x}{d - x} = \frac{(22.1 \; \text{pF})(0.5)}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{11.05}{0.5} = 22.1 \; \text{pF}$$

Notice that for large $x$ relative to $d$, the exact formula predicts a *much larger* capacitance change than the linearized formula would. This demonstrates the **non-linearity** of the variable-gap mode for large displacements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT-Based Displacement Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Excitation Source<br/>1-10 V RMS, 1-10 kHz] --> B[LVDT Primary Winding P]
    C[Mechanical Displacement<br/>of the Core x mm] --> D[Soft Iron Core<br/>Inside LVDT Bore]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Secondary Winding S1<br/>Mutual Inductance M1]
    B --> F[Secondary Winding S2<br/>Mutual Inductance M2]
    E --> G[Series Opposition<br/>Differential Junction]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Differential AC Output<br/>E_out = E_S1 - E_S2]
    H --> I[Phase Sensitive Demodulator<br/>PSD Module]
    I --> J[Low Pass Filter<br/>Removes Carrier Ripple]
    J --> K[Differential Amplifier<br/>Gain = 10 to 100]
    K --> L[DC Output Voltage<br/>Proportional to Displacement]
    L --> M[Digital Display or<br/>Oscilloscope or DMM]
    N[DC Reference Voltage] --> I
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The chain flows from excitation (top-left) → LVDT coupling → differential AC output → phase-sensitive demodulation → filtering → amplification → readable DC display. The core's mechanical position is the *only* physical input; everything else is signal processing.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capacitive Transducer Measurement System (AC Bridge Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Oscillator 1 kHz to 10 kHz<br/>Sine Wave V_ex] --> B[AC Wheatstone Bridge]
    B --> C[Reference Arm<br/>Fixed Capacitor C_ref]
    B --> D[Unknown Arm<br/>Transducer Capacitor C_x]
    B --> E[Two Ratio Arms<br/>R1 and R2 Equal]
    B --> F[Bridge Output Nodes<br/>a and b]
    F --> G[Differential Amplifier<br/>High CMRR]
    G --> H[Phase Sensitive Detector<br/>PSD or Synchronous Detector]
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter<br/>fc approx 10 Hz]
    I --> J[DC Output Voltage<br/>V_dc Proportional to x]
    K[Mechanical Displacement<br/>of Movable Plate x] --> D
    J --> L[Digital Voltmeter<br/>or Computer DAQ]
    M[Balance Adjustment<br/>Trimmer Capacitor] --> C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A sinusoidal excitation drives the bridge. The transducer's variable capacitance is the unknown arm; the off-balance voltage at the bridge output is amplified, demodulated, filtered, and displayed as a DC voltage directly proportional to displacement.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capacitive Transducer (Variable-Area Mode) Construction Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Plate 1<br/>Width b, Length L] --> B[Overlapping Region<br/>Effective Area A_eff = b L minus b x]
    C[Movable Plate 2<br/>Slides Horizontally] --> B
    B --> D[Output Capacitance<br/>C = epsilon A_eff over d]
    E[Displacement x mm<br/>in horizontal direction] --> C
    D --> F[Signal Conditioning<br/>AC Bridge or Oscillator]
    F --> G[Calibrated DC Output<br/>Linear in x]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The variable-area mode offers a **linear** $C$–$x$ relationship (in contrast to the non-linear variable-gap mode), making it the preferred configuration for medium-range displacement measurements.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — LVDT vs Capacitive Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Displacement Transducer] --> B{Range Required?}
    B -->|Sub-mm to Few mm| C[Variable Gap Capacitive<br/>or LVDT]
    B -->|1 cm to 10 cm| D[Variable Area Capacitive<br/>or Long-stroke LVDT]
    B -->|> 10 cm| E[Potentiometric or<br/>Optical Encoder]
    C --> F{Environment?}
    F -->|Clean, Lab| G[Capacitive Transducer<br/>High Precision]
    F -->|Industrial, Dusty, Hot| H[LVDT<br/>Rugged and Sealed]
    D --> F
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This decision-tree summarizes when to choose LVDT vs capacitive transducer based on range, precision, and environmental robustness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Construction:** An LVDT consists of one primary winding (P) and two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂) wound coaxially on a hollow cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron core slides inside the bore of the former, mechanically coupled to the displacement under measurement. The two secondaries are connected in series opposition.

**Working:** When AC excitation is applied to the primary, an alternating flux is produced. At the central (null) position of the core, the EMFs induced in S₁ and S₂ are equal and opposite, giving zero net output. When the core moves toward S₁, mutual inductance M₁ increases and M₂ decreases, producing a net differential output voltage whose magnitude is proportional to displacement and whose phase indicates direction.

**Advantages (any two, 1 mark):**
1. Infinite resolution (limited only by the readout electronics).
2. Frictionless and contactless operation → long life, no mechanical wear.
3. Excellent linearity over a wide range (±0.5% or better).
4. High sensitivity (typical 0.5–5 V/mm).
5. Bidirectional output with inherent electrical null.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitive transducer and explain how displacement measurement is carried out using the variable-gap method.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Derivation:** For a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$, plate separation $d$, and dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Variable-Gap Displacement Measurement:** When the movable plate is displaced by $x$, the gap changes from $d$ to $d \pm x$, causing a change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \, x}{d - x} \quad \text{(exact)}, \quad \Delta C \approx \frac{C_0}{d} x \quad \text{(for } x \ll d\text{)}$$

This change is measured using an AC Wheatstone bridge or an op-amp charge amplifier, and the output voltage is calibrated against $x$ to obtain displacement. (Note: This relation is non-linear for larger $x$, so the method is restricted to small displacements.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an LVDT-based displacement measurement system and explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.6 V/mm and is excited by a 5 V RMS, 5 kHz source. The linear range is ±5 mm. For a displacement of +3.5 mm, calculate:
(i) The RMS output voltage.
(ii) The percentage of full-scale output.
(iii) The output voltage if the excitation frequency is doubled. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks):**

```
[AC Excitation] → [LVDT Sensor] → [Phase-Sensitive Demodulator] → 
[Low-Pass Filter] → [Differential Amplifier] → [Display/DAQ]
```

| **Block** | **Function** |
|:----------|:-------------|
| AC Excitation Source | Provides stable sinusoidal carrier (1–10 V RMS, 1–10 kHz) to the LVDT primary |
| LVDT Sensor | Converts mechanical displacement into differential AC voltage |
| Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) | Rectifies the AC output using a reference signal; preserves direction information (phase) |
| Low-Pass Filter | Removes the carrier-frequency ripple; produces smooth DC |
| Differential Amplifier | Boosts the small DC signal to a usable level (e.g., 0–10 V) |
| Display / DAQ | Reads the calibrated displacement value |

A reference voltage tapped from the excitation source is also routed to the PSD to enable phase comparison.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Sensitivity $S = 0.6 \; \text{V/mm}$, excitation $V_{ex} = 5 \; \text{V RMS}$ at $f = 5 \; \text{kHz}$, linear range $= \pm 5 \; \text{mm}$, displacement $x = +3.5 \; \text{mm}$.

**(i) RMS Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$E_{out} = S \times x = 0.6 \;\text{V/mm} \times 3.5 \;\text{mm} = 2.1 \;\text{V RMS}$$

**(ii) Percentage of Full-Scale Output (2 marks):**

Full-scale output corresponds to $x = +5 \; \text{mm}$:

$$E_{out,FS} = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0 \; \text{V RMS}$$

$$\% \text{FSO} = \frac{2.1}{3.0} \times 100 = 70\%$$

**(iii) Output Voltage with Doubled Frequency (3 marks):**

The LVDT output is proportional to the excitation frequency (since $E_{out} \propto \omega \cdot I_p$, and $I_p$ depends on $V_{ex}$ and inductive reactance, but the *sensitivity* in V/mm scales linearly with frequency when the current is held constant by the source).

In typical LVDT operation with constant $V_{ex}$, the sensitivity does *not* double with frequency because the primary inductance also changes. However, for the purpose of this problem (and following standard KTU convention), the sensitivity is taken to scale linearly with frequency:

$$S_{new} = 0.6 \times \frac{10}{5} = 1.2 \; \text{V/mm}$$

$$E_{out,new} = 1.2 \times 3.5 = 4.2 \; \text{V RMS}$$

**Alternative interpretation:** If the problem expects the output to remain unchanged (since the sensitivity is a fixed device parameter at the rated frequency), the answer is **2.1 V RMS**. Students should mention both cases in the exam and state the assumption clearly.

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of an AC Wheatstone bridge for the measurement of small capacitance changes in a capacitive displacement transducer. Derive the expression for the off-balance output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has $A = 40 \;\text{cm}^2$, $d = 2 \;\text{mm}$, and air as the dielectric. Calculate:
(i) The initial capacitance.
(ii) The change in capacitance for a displacement of 0.2 mm (exact and linearized).
(iii) The corresponding change in output voltage if the bridge is excited by 10 V RMS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AC Bridge — Working and Derivation (7 Marks):**

The AC bridge has four arms: two ratio arms (resistive, $R_1$ and $R_2$), a reference capacitive arm ($C_{ref}$), and the unknown capacitive arm ($C_x$). An AC source $V_{ex}$ is applied across one diagonal; the detector (high-impedance voltmeter or PSD) is connected across the other diagonal.

**Balance condition:** $C_x = C_{ref} \cdot (R_2/R_1)$. At balance, detector reads zero.

**When $C_x$ changes by $\Delta C$:** The bridge becomes unbalanced, and a small AC voltage appears at the detector. For small $\Delta C \ll C_0$ and equal ratio arms ($R_1 = R_2$), the off-balance output is:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{\Delta C}{4 C_0} \cdot V_{ex}$$

The detector signal is then demodulated, filtered, and displayed as a DC voltage proportional to $\Delta C$, and hence to displacement $x$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $A = 40 \;\text{cm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$, $d = 2 \;\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $V_{ex} = 10 \;\text{V RMS}$, $x = 0.2 \;\text{mm} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$.

**(i) Initial Capacitance (2 marks):**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{(1)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{F} \approx 17.71 \;\text{pF}$$

**(ii) Change in Capacitance (3 marks):**

**Exact formula:**

$$\Delta C = \frac{C_0 \cdot x}{d - x} = \frac{(17.71 \;\text{pF})(0.2)}{2.0 - 0.2} = \frac{3.542}{1.8} = 1.968 \;\text{pF}$$

**Linearized formula:**

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{C_0}{d} \cdot x = \frac{17.71 \;\text{pF}}{2 \;\text{mm}} \times 0.2 \;\text{mm} = 1.771 \;\text{pF}$$

The exact and linearized results differ by ~10% here, illustrating the non-linearity growing as $x$ approaches $d$.

**(iii) Off-Balance Bridge Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{\Delta C}{4 C_0} \cdot V_{ex} = \frac{1.771 \;\text{pF}}{4 \times 17.71 \;\text{pF}} \times 10 \;\text{V} = \frac{1.771}{70.84} \times 10 = 0.25 \;\text{V RMS}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Null Position" with "Zero Output" without qualification** — Always state that at the null position, the *differential* output is zero because the two secondaries are in series opposition. (Loss: ~1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator** — Many students explain LVDT but skip the PSD, which is essential for direction detection. (Loss: ~2 marks in block-diagram questions.)
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for variable-gap capacitance change** — Using $C = C_0 \pm \Delta C$ directly without the $1/d$ dependence loses the derivation marks. Always derive: $\Delta C = C_0 \cdot x/(d-x)$. (Loss: ~2 marks.)
> 4. **Mixing up units** — Capacitance in pF vs F, displacement in mm vs m, frequency in Hz vs kHz. **Always show unit conversions explicitly in the calculation.** (Loss: ~1 mark per incorrect unit.)
> 5. **Not stating assumptions in numericals** — When the linearized formula is used, students must explicitly state "assuming $x \ll d$". When the problem involves an LVDT with frequency scaling, the assumption of constant current must be stated. (Loss: ~1 mark.)
> 6. **Missing the "Why"** — KTU examiners reward conceptual understanding. Just writing an equation without explaining *why* the two secondaries are connected in opposition, or *why* the bridge is excited by AC and not DC, costs marks. (Loss: ~2–3 marks per question.)
> 7. **Forgetting the dielectric constant** — In capacitive derivations, students sometimes drop the $\varepsilon_r$ factor. In air $\varepsilon_r = 1$, but for other dielectrics, it is critical. (Loss: ~1 mark.)
> 8. **Ignoring the sign of displacement** — LVDT output is signed. Forgetting the negative sign or 180° phase reversal for one direction costs accuracy marks. (Loss: ~1 mark.)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Lab Exam / Viva**

- [x] **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer; uses **mutual inductance** variation; three coils (1 primary, 2 secondaries) + 1 movable core.
- [x] **LVDT secondaries are connected in series opposition** → produces a *differential* output that is **zero at the central (null) position** and grows in proportion to displacement.
- [x] **Phase of the LVDT output** encodes the **direction** of displacement; **magnitude** encodes the **amount** of displacement.
- [x] LVDT is **AC-excited** (1–10 V RMS, 1–10 kHz); the output is also AC and must be **demodulated** to DC before display.
- [x] **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** is mandatory for direction detection.
- [x] LVDT advantages: frictionless, infinite resolution, high linearity, bidirectional, long life, rugged.
- [x] LVDT limitations: requires AC excitation, dynamic response limited by carrier frequency, sensitive to stray magnetic fields.
- [x] **Capacitive transducer formula (parallel plate):** $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$.
- [x] **Three modes:** variable gap (non-linear, small range), variable area (linear, medium range), variable dielectric (used for level/humidity).
- [x] **Linearized capacitance change for small gap displacement:** $\Delta C \approx (C_0 / d) \cdot x$.
- [x] **AC Wheatstone bridge** is the standard signal conditioner for capacitive transducers.
- [x] **Bridge off-balance voltage** (small-signal): $V_{out} = (\Delta C / 4C_0) \cdot V_{ex}$.
- [x] **Fundamental constants:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- [x] **LVDT sensitivity unit:** V/mm; **capacitive transducer sensitivity unit:** F/m or V/mm (after signal conditioning).
- [x] **Calibration procedure:** Apply known displacements (using a micrometer or calibrated gauge block), record output, plot calibration curve, compute slope (sensitivity) and linearity.
- [x] **Static characteristics to determine in lab:** Sensitivity, linearity (% non-linearity), range, resolution, hysteresis, repeatability.
- [x] **Dynamic characteristics:** Frequency response, natural frequency, damping ratio.
- [x] **Safety:** Ensure AC excitation levels are within the device rating; handle the LVDT core carefully (do not drop, as it can damage the bore windings).
- [x] **Viva favourites:** *"Why is the LVDT output zero at the central position?"*, *"Why AC excitation and not DC?"*, *"What is the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator?"*, *"Why is the variable-gap capacitive transducer non-linear?"*, *"What is the difference between sensitivity and resolution?"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Street Light (230V bulb and relay)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Street Light (230V bulb and relay)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Street Light System:** An electro-optical control system in which a **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)** acts as a phototransducer, sensing ambient illumination levels and producing a corresponding change in resistance. This resistance change is converted into a proportional voltage signal by a **potential divider network**, compared against a reference voltage by a **voltage comparator** (or transistor switch), and finally used to energize/de-energize an **electromagnetic relay coil**. The relay's switching contacts then control a high-voltage **230 V AC lamp load** safely isolated from the low-voltage DC control circuit.

The experiment demonstrates two fundamental engineering concepts simultaneously:

1. **Transduction** – conversion of optical energy (light intensity, in **lux**) into electrical resistance.
2. **Actuation** – use of an electromagnetic relay to switch a high-power AC load using a low-power DC control signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Night Watchman Circuit":** Imagine a security guard sitting at the gate. He can see very well in daylight but is blind at night. So as soon as the sun sets, he flicks a switch to turn on the street lamps. The LDR is the "guard's eyes" — it has high resistance in the dark and low resistance in light. The comparator is the "guard's brain" that decides what to do, and the relay is his "strong arm" that physically throws the heavy mains switch because his hand is too weak to do it directly.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Mains supply in India:** **230 V AC, 50 Hz** (IS 12360 standard)
- **Standard LDR dark resistance:** ≈ **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ**
- **Standard LDR light resistance:** ≈ **400 Ω to 1 kΩ** (at 1000 lux)
- **Relay coil operating voltage:** typically **5 V DC / 6 V DC / 12 V DC** (lab standard: 5 V)
- **Relay contact rating (lab relay):** **230 V AC, 7 A** (or 10 A) — capable of switching incandescent bulbs
- **Threshold illumination for switching:** typically **50 lux to 200 lux** (twilight range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is mapped to **CO2 – Apply transducers in real-world measurement and control systems** and tests the student's ability to wire a 230 V AC load through a relay with proper isolation, observe LDR characteristics, and tabulate illumination versus resistance values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Wise Operational Breakdown

The automatic street light circuit is best understood in **four functional blocks**:

**Block 1 — Light Sensing Stage (Transducer Stage)**
- An **LDR** (also called a photoconductive cell, CdS type) is connected in a **potential divider** with a fixed resistor **R₁**.
- LDR resistance $R_{LDR}$ is **inversely proportional** to the incident light intensity **L** (in lux):

$$R_{LDR} \approx \frac{A}{L^n}$$

where *A* is a constant, *n ≈ 0.7 to 0.9* for a CdS cell.

**Block 2 — Signal Conditioning & Decision Stage (Comparator Stage)**
- The junction voltage $V_{sense}$ between the LDR and R₁ is fed to the **non-inverting input (+)** of an op-amp comparator (LM358) or to the **base** of a BC547 NPN transistor.
- A **reference voltage** $V_{ref}$ is set using a potentiometer at the inverting input (−) or at the emitter.
- When $V_{sense} > V_{ref}$ → output of comparator **goes HIGH** (or transistor turns ON).
- When $V_{sense} < V_{ref}$ → output **goes LOW** (or transistor turns OFF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a comparator is mandatory (not a direct relay drive):** The voltage at the LDR junction is too weak (and at high impedance) to directly energize a relay coil. The comparator provides a sharp digital decision and sufficient current-driving capability through a transistor buffer.

**Block 3 — Driver Stage (Transistor Switch)**
- An **NPN transistor (e.g., SL100, BC547, 2N2222)** is used in **saturation mode** as a switch.
- The comparator output drives the base through a current-limiting resistor **R_B**.
- When the transistor is in saturation, the full supply voltage appears across the **relay coil**, energizing it.

**Block 4 — Load Switching Stage (Relay + Lamp)**
- Energized relay pulls the armature → the **Common (COM)** contact connects to the **Normally Open (NO)** contact.
- This completes the **230 V AC mains circuit** through the bulb, turning it **ON**.
- When the relay is de-energized, the spring returns the armature to the **Normally Closed (NC)** contact — the bulb is **OFF**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| LDR | Cheapest, most reliable, most linear visible-light transducer for hobbyist/lab use |
| Voltage divider | Converts resistance change into voltage change — easily read by a comparator |
| Comparator | Provides a **Schmitt trigger**-like clean transition; avoids relay chattering at the threshold |
| Transistor | Provides **current amplification** ($I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$) to drive the relay coil |
| Relay | Provides **galvanic isolation** between 5 V DC control and 230 V AC load — the #1 safety feature |
| Flyback diode (1N4007) | Suppresses back-EMF from relay coil ($V_L = -L \frac{di}{dt}$) that can destroy the transistor |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{sense} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$ | LDR voltage divider | V (volt) | Finding sensor output voltage |
| 2 | $V_{ref} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$ | Reference voltage via pot | V | Comparator threshold |
| 3 | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$ | Relay coil current | A | Transistor load calculation |
| 4 | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{coil}}{\beta_{min}}$ | Required base current | A | Base resistor selection |
| 5 | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | Base series resistor | Ω | Component sizing |
| 6 | $P_{coil} = I_{coil}^2 \cdot R_{coil}$ | Coil power dissipation | W | Thermal design |
| 7 | $P_{lamp} = V_{rms}^2 \cdot \cos\phi / R_{lamp}$ | Lamp power (resistive) | W | Load rating check |
| 8 | $R_{LDR} \approx \dfrac{500 \text{ k}\Omega}{\text{Lux}}$ (approx.) | Empirical LDR model | Ω | Quick design estimate |
| 9 | $V_{flyback} = -L \dfrac{\Delta i}{\Delta t}$ | Inductive kick | V | Justification of freewheeling diode |
| 10 | $\tau = R_{LDR} \cdot C$ | Time constant (if RC filter added) | s | Debounce / noise filtering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule (KTU Board Exam):** In your answer sheets, never write absolute values like $|V|$ inside a sentence; use $\vert V \vert$ to keep equations clean. Examiner values neatness.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Lighting:** Smart-city streetlights using LDR + microcontroller + relay.
- **Garden & Parking Lights:** Automatic dusk-to-dawn lighting.
- **Solar-Powered Lamps:** LDR + relay + battery management.
- **Industrial Safety:** Automatic warning lamp activation in low-visibility zones.
- **Energy Savings:** India's **UJALA / SLNP** programme emphasizes automatic streetlight control to save **~1500 GWh/year** of electricity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voltage Divider Output of the LDR Stage

**Problem Setup:** A 5 V DC supply feeds an LDR in series with a fixed resistor $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The LDR resistance $R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ in dim light. Find the voltage $V_{sense}$ at the divider junction.

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit topology.**
The LDR and R₁ form a series loop connected across $V_{CC} = 5$ V. The output is tapped between them.

$$V_{CC} = I \cdot (R_1 + R_{LDR})$$

**Step 2 — Solve for loop current I.**

$$I = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}} = \dfrac{5}{10{,}000 + 50{,}000} = \dfrac{5}{60{,}000}$$

$$I = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 83.33 \text{ μA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{sense}$ across R₁ using Ohm's law.**

$$V_{sense} = I \cdot R_1 = 83.33 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^3$$

$$V_{sense} = 0.8333 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize using the divider rule.**

$$V_{sense} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 50\text{k}} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = 0.833 \text{ V}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** In bright light, $R_{LDR}$ drops to ~1 kΩ, so $V_{sense}$ rises to ~4.5 V (HIGH). In darkness, $R_{LDR}$ rises, $V_{sense}$ drops, and the comparator flips. This **inversion** of behavior is why circuit designers carefully place the LDR at the correct side of the divider.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reference (Threshold) Voltage from a Potentiometer

**Setup:** A 10 kΩ potentiometer is connected between +5 V and GND, with the wiper fed to the comparator's inverting input.

**Step 1 — Write the general expression.**

$$V_{ref} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{lower}}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Assume wiper is at 40 % rotation.** $R_{lower} = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{upper} = 6 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$V_{ref} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{4}{10} = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Decision rule.**
The bulb will turn ON when $V_{sense} < V_{ref}$ (i.e., dark condition). The 2.0 V threshold corresponds to a specific ambient lux level depending on the LDR characteristics.

> **Lab Tip:** The pot is rotated until the bulb just turns ON/OFF at the desired twilight brightness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Base Resistor Sizing for the Transistor Driver

**Given:** Relay coil resistance $R_{coil} = 200 \text{ Ω}$, coil rated at 5 V DC. Transistor $\beta_{min} = 100$. Op-amp output HIGH = 4.5 V. $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V.

**Step 1 — Calculate required coil current.**

$$I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{5 - 0.2}{200} = \dfrac{4.8}{200} = 24 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate required base current for saturation.** We use a forced $\beta = 10$ (rule of thumb: $I_B = I_C / 10$) to ensure hard saturation.

$$I_B = \dfrac{I_{coil}}{10} = \dfrac{24 \text{ mA}}{10} = 2.4 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the base resistor.**

$$R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \dfrac{4.5 - 0.7}{2.4 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{3.8}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_B = 1583 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard value)}$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation check on transistor.**

$$P_T = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{coil} = 0.2 \times 0.024 = 4.8 \text{ mW}$$

The TO-92 package (BC547) is rated for ~500 mW, so this is **safe with huge margin**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Load Power and Relay Contact Rating

**Given:** 230 V AC, 60 W incandescent bulb.

**Step 1 — Compute bulb current.**

$$I_{lamp} = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{60}{230} = 0.2608 \text{ A} = 260.8 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with relay contact rating.** Lab relay typically rated 7 A at 230 V AC. Safety margin:

$$\text{Margin} = \dfrac{7}{0.2608} = 26.8 \times$$

> **Conclusion:** Relay is comfortably overrated — a 100 W bulb (435 mA) would also work, but a 1000 W heater (4.3 A) would not.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Expected LDR Resistance Values (Lab Tabulation)

| Ambient Condition | Approx. Lux | LDR Resistance $R_{LDR}$ | $V_{sense}$ (with 10 kΩ) | Relay State | Lamp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct sunlight | 100,000 | ~100 Ω | 4.95 V | De-energized | OFF |
| Daylight (indoor) | 1,000 | ~1 kΩ | 4.55 V | De-energized | OFF |
| Overcast day | 100 | ~10 kΩ | 2.50 V | Borderline | Flicker zone |
| Twilight (dusk) | 10 | ~50 kΩ | 0.83 V | Energized | ON |
| Dark night | 1 | ~500 kΩ | 0.10 V | Energized | ON |
| Pitch dark | 0.1 | ~5 MΩ | 0.01 V | Energized | ON |

> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** Always tabulate at least **5 readings** of Lux vs Resistance and verify the inverse relationship by plotting a graph.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AMB[Ambient Light Source]
    LDR[LDR Phototransducer]
    DIV[Potential Divider R1 plus LDR]
    CMP[Comparator LM358]
    REF[Potentiometer Reference Voltage]
    TRX[BC547 NPN Transistor Switch]
    FB1[1N4007 Flyback Diode]
    RLY[5V Electromagnetic Relay Coil]
    CON[Relay COM-NO Contact]
    LMP[230V AC 60W Bulb Load]
    MAINS[230V AC 50Hz Mains]

    AMB --> LDR
    LDR --> DIV
    DIV -->|Vsense| CMP
    REF --> CMP
    CMP -->|HIGH or LOW| TRX
    TRX --> RLY
    FB1 -.->|parallel protection| RLY
    RLY -->|mechanical pull| CON
    CON --> LMP
    LMP --> MAINS
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Optical input → resistance change → voltage → digital decision → current amplification → magnetic actuation → mains switching.

---

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Pin Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[plus 5V DC Supply]
    GND[Ground 0V]

    subgraph SENSE[Sensing Stage]
        R1[Resistor R1 10 kilo ohm]
        LDR1[LDR CdS Cell]
        R1 --- LDR1
        NODE_A[Junction Node A Vsense]
    end

    subgraph CMPSTG[Comparator Stage]
        U1[Op-Amp LM358]
        POT[Potentiometer 10 kilo ohm]
        NODE_B[Junction Node B Vref]
    end

    subgraph DRV[Driver Stage]
        RB[Base Resistor RB 1.5 kilo ohm]
        Q1[BC547 NPN Transistor]
        D1[Flyback Diode 1N4007]
        K1[Relay Coil 5V 200 ohm]
    end

    subgraph LOAD[Load Switching Stage]
        SW[Relay SPDT Contact]
        L1[230V AC Bulb 60W]
    end

    VCC --> R1
    VCC --> POT
    LDR1 --> GND
    POT --> GND
    NODE_A -->|pin 3 non inverting| U1
    NODE_B -->|pin 2 inverting| U1
    VCC -->|pin 8| U1
    U1 -->|pin 1 output| RB
    RB --> Q1
    Q1 --> K1
    D1 --> K1
    K1 --> VCC
    Q1 --> GND
    U1 -->|pin 4| GND

    K1 -.->|magnetic coupling| SW
    SW --> L1
    L1 --> MAINS2[230V AC Live]
    SW --> NEUT[230V AC Neutral]
```

> **Safety Note:** The 230 V AC section (bottom-right) must be **physically separated** from the 5 V DC section on the breadboard. In KTU lab exams, examiners deduct marks if the high-voltage wiring is messy or near the low-voltage section.

---

### 4.3 Relay Contact Pinout (SPDT, 5-pin type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COIL1[Coil Pin 1]
    COIL2[Coil Pin 2]
    COM[COM Pin]
    NO[NO Pin Normally Open]
    NC[NC Pin Normally Closed]

    COIL1 --- COIL2
    COM --- NO
    COM --- NC
```

> **Lab Wiring Mnemonic:** "**C**onnect **O**utput to **N**O" — when the relay is energised, the bulb gets connected via the NO contact.

---

### 4.4 LDR Resistance vs Light Intensity Characteristic Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Point A 1000 lux R=1k] --> P2[Point B 100 lux R=10k]
    P2 --> P3[Point C 10 lux R=50k]
    P3 --> P4[Point D 1 lux R=500k]
    P4 --> P5[Point E 0 lux R=10M]
```

> The relationship is **non-linear** and resembles a hyperbolic decay. Students are expected to plot this on **semi-log paper** during the lab viva.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> *Define an LDR. State two advantages and one disadvantage of using an LDR in an automatic street light system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) is a photoconductive transducer whose resistance decreases when incident light intensity increases, and increases in darkness. It is made of **cadmium sulfide (CdS)**.

- **Advantage 1:** Low cost and readily available (~₹10–₹30).
- **Advantage 2:** Simple to interface — no amplifier required for low-frequency switching.
- **Disadvantage:** Slow response time (~50 ms) and spectral sensitivity limited to visible range; not suitable for fast or infrared applications.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> *Why is a relay used to switch the 230 V AC bulb instead of a transistor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A relay provides **galvanic isolation** between the low-voltage DC control circuit (5 V) and the high-voltage AC load (230 V). A transistor cannot directly switch 230 V AC because:
1. Most BJTs are rated ≤ 60 V collector-emitter, far below 230 V AC peak (325 V).
2. BJTs are unidirectional — they conduct only one half-cycle of AC.
3. AC switching also requires zero-crossing detection, which relays handle inherently.
The relay also provides a **common mechanical interface** so the same circuit can switch higher loads (up to its contact rating) without redesign.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 1]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an automatic street light system using an LDR, op-amp comparator, transistor driver, and 5 V relay controlling a 230 V AC bulb. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** For the above system, the LDR has $R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ in dim light and $R_{LDR} = 500 \text{ Ω}$ in bright light. The fixed resistor is $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The potentiometer is set so that $V_{ref} = 2$ V with $V_{CC} = 5$ V. Calculate (i) $V_{sense}$ in dim light, (ii) $V_{sense}$ in bright light, and (iii) explain in which condition the bulb will glow. The comparator is non-inverting (LM358). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

1. **LDR + R₁ divider** produces $V_{sense}$ proportional to ambient light (2 marks).
2. **LM358 comparator** compares $V_{sense}$ with $V_{ref} = 2$ V set by potentiometer (2 marks).
3. **BC547 transistor** in saturation switches the **5 V relay coil ON**; a **1N4007 flyback diode** protects the transistor (2 marks).
4. **Relay SPDT contact** closes the 230 V AC mains circuit through the bulb, turning it ON. The bulb is in series with the NO contact and the live mains line (1 mark).

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Dim light:** $R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$

$$V_{sense} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 50\text{k}} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1}{6} = 0.833 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Bright light:** $R_{LDR} = 500 \text{ Ω} = 0.5 \text{ k}\Omega$

$$V_{sense} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 0.5\text{k}} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{10}{10.5} = 4.762 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Decision rule:** For LM358 non-inverting comparator, output = HIGH when $V_{+} > V_{-}$, i.e., $V_{sense} > V_{ref}$.

- In dim light: $V_{sense} = 0.833$ V $< V_{ref} = 2$ V → output **LOW** → transistor **OFF** → relay **de-energized** → bulb **OFF** *(wait — this is inverted!)*

> **Corrected conclusion (exam-safe wording):** In the configuration above with LDR at the top of the divider, the bulb will **glow in bright light** (V_sense = 4.76 V > 2 V). To make the bulb glow in **darkness**, the LDR and R₁ must be **interchanged**, so that $V_{sense}$ is high when the LDR resistance is high (dark). This is the **standard KTU exam trap** — students often misread which side of the divider the LDR is on.

**Final answer (after correction):** The bulb glows when $V_{sense} > V_{ref}$, i.e., in **darkness**, provided the LDR is connected to GND (bottom of divider) and R₁ to V_CC (top of divider). With the given component values, swap R₁ and LDR to obtain the standard dusk-to-dawn operation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 2 — Alternative]**
> 
> **(a)** Draw the pinout of a 5 V SPDT relay. Identify the coil terminals, COM, NO, and NC pins. Explain the role of a freewheeling diode across the relay coil. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** A relay coil has $R_{coil} = 240 \text{ Ω}$ and is driven by a BC547 transistor ($\beta = 100$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2$ V) from a 5 V supply. The op-amp output HIGH level is 4.6 V and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V. Calculate the (i) coil current, (ii) minimum base current, (iii) value of base resistor, and (iv) verify that the transistor remains in saturation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

1. **5-pin SPDT relay pinout** (drawn as a rectangle with 5 pins): two for coil, one COM, one NO, one NC. **(2 marks)**
2. **Coil pins:** When 5 V DC is applied across the coil, magnetic flux pulls the armature. **(1 mark)**
3. **COM–NO–NC behaviour:** In de-energized state, COM connects to NC. When energised, COM moves to NO. **(2 marks)**
4. **Flyback diode (1N4007):** Placed in reverse bias across the coil. When the transistor switches OFF, the coil's collapsing magnetic field induces a back-EMF of the form $V = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$, which can reach hundreds of volts and destroy the transistor. The diode provides a freewheeling path for the inductor current, clamping the spike to ~0.7 V. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Coil current:**

$$I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{5 - 0.2}{240} = \dfrac{4.8}{240} = 0.020 \text{ A} = 20 \text{ mA}$$

**(ii) Minimum base current for saturation (forced $\beta = 10$):**

$$I_{B(min)} = \dfrac{I_{coil}}{10} = \dfrac{20 \text{ mA}}{10} = 2 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Base resistor:**

$$R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \dfrac{4.6 - 0.7}{2 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{3.9}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1950 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.8 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard)}$$

**(iv) Saturation verification:**
Actual $I_B$ with $R_B = 1.8 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$I_{B(actual)} = \dfrac{3.9}{1800} = 2.167 \text{ mA} > 2 \text{ mA} = I_{B(min)}$$

Since $I_{B(actual)} > I_{B(min)}$, the transistor is **firmly in saturation**. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Divider orientation trap:** Marks are cut if the student places the LDR on the wrong side of the divider and gets an inverted response without explanation.
> 2. **Missing flyback diode:** Examiners specifically check for the 1N4007 across the relay coil. -1 mark if absent.
> 3. **Coil current calculation:** Forgetting to subtract $V_{CE(sat)}$ from $V_{CC}$ when computing $I_{coil}$ costs full marks in the numerical.
> 4. **Forced $\beta$ vs natural $\beta$:** Students write $I_B = I_C / \beta$ instead of $I_B = I_C / 10$. Always use forced $\beta = 10$ for **hard saturation**.
> 5. **230 V AC safety wiring:** In the viva, examiners ask "where is the fuse?" and "how did you isolate the mains from the control circuit?" — be ready with the answer: **relay contacts + physical separation + insulation tape**.
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** Writing $R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ kΩ}$ as "50 K" or "50,000" without the unit costs a mark. Always include units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Automatic Street Light (230 V Bulb + Relay)**
> 
> - **Core transducers:** LDR (CdS) — resistance falls as light increases.
> - **Sensing element location:** Place LDR such that streetlight illumination does **not** fall back on it (avoid positive feedback / hysteresis).
> - **Decision element:** Op-amp comparator (LM358) with a potentiometer-set reference.
> - **Driver element:** NPN BJT in saturation, base current = $I_C / 10$ (forced $\beta$).
> - **Switching element:** 5 V electromagnetic relay — provides galvanic isolation.
> - **Protection element:** **1N4007 freewheeling diode** in reverse bias across relay coil.
> - **Load element:** 230 V AC, 60 W incandescent bulb, switched through NO contact.
> - **Key formulas:** $V_{sense} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_{LDR}}$ and $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$.
> - **Lab tabulation:** Lux vs $R_{LDR}$ — expect a **hyperbolic** inverse relationship.
> - **Safety first:** Mains wiring must be physically separated; use insulated wires; never touch the 230 V section while the circuit is energised.
> - **Viva questions to prepare:** (i) Why relay instead of triac/SCR? (ii) Why LDR and not photodiode? (iii) What is the role of hysteresis in this circuit? (iv) What happens if the LDR fails open / short?
> - **Common modifications:** Add an **astable multivibrator** for blinking effect, replace relay with **triac** for silent operation, add **GSM module** for SMS alerts on lamp failure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Temperature and Humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Temperature and Humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Temperature** is a physical quantity that quantitatively expresses the hotness or coldness of a body, measured using thermometric scales. In SI units, temperature is measured in **Kelvin (K)**, though Celsius (°C) and Fahrenheit (°F) are commonly used in practice. The thermodynamic definition relates temperature to the average kinetic energy of the particles in a system.

**Humidity** refers to the concentration of water vapour present in the air. The most commonly measured parameter is **Relative Humidity (RH)**, defined as the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in a gas to the saturation vapour pressure at a given temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment combines two distinct physical quantities into a single measurement platform, requiring the use of **semiconductor-based integrated sensors** (such as LM35 for temperature and DHT11/DHT22 for humidity) interfaced with a microcontroller (Arduino/equivalent) to study transducer characteristics like sensitivity, linearity, and accuracy.

### Mathematical Formulation of RH

$$RH(\%) = \frac{P_{w}}{P_{ws}(T)} \times 100$$

Where:
- $P_w$ = Actual partial pressure of water vapour in air
- $P_{ws}(T)$ = Saturation pressure of water vapour at the same temperature $T$

> [!NOTE]
> The saturation pressure $P_{ws}$ is strongly temperature-dependent, which is why humidity measurement is **always coupled with simultaneous temperature measurement** for accurate results.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

### Temperature Analogy — "The Speedometer of Molecules"

Imagine air molecules in a room as tiny, invisible cars moving randomly in all directions:
- **Hot air (high temperature)** → molecules are like sports cars speeding around quickly → high average kinetic energy
- **Cold air (low temperature)** → molecules are like parked cars barely moving → low average kinetic energy

A temperature sensor is essentially a **molecular speedometer** — it senses how vigorously the particles are moving and gives us a numerical readout.

### Humidity Analogy — "The Sponge in the Air"

Think of air as a giant, invisible sponge that can soak up water vapour:
- **Dry air (low RH)** → the sponge is almost dry, it can still absorb a lot more moisture
- **Humid air (high RH)** → the sponge is nearly full, it can absorb very little more
- **100% RH (saturation)** → the sponge is completely full; any extra moisture will condense as dew

A humidity sensor measures **how "full" the air-sponge is** with water vapour.

### Real-World Engineering Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters in Industry:**
> - **HVAC systems** (air conditioners, refrigerators) require precise T and RH control for human comfort and equipment protection
> - **Food & pharmaceutical storage** mandates specific T/RH ranges (e.g., vaccine cold chain requires $2^\circ C$ to $8^\circ C$)
> - **Greenhouse automation** uses T/RH feedback to regulate misting systems and ventilation
> - **Weather stations** and **IoT-based smart agriculture** depend entirely on such sensor modules

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Standard Temperature | $T_0 = 273.15$ **K** |
| Standard Pressure | $P_0 = 101.325$ **kPa** |
| Temperature Range (DHT11) | $0$ to $50$ **°C** |
| Humidity Range (DHT11) | $20$ to $90$ **% RH** |
| LM35 Scale Factor | **$10$ mV/°C** |
| Saturation Pressure at $100^\circ C$ | $101.325$ **kPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> The LM35 sensor is one of the most popular temperature transducers in KTU lab experiments because of its **linear output of 10 mV per °C** and direct Celsius calibration without requiring offset subtraction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temperature Measurement — Operating Principles

### A. LM35 Precision Centigrade Temperature Sensor

The **LM35** is an integrated-circuit temperature transducer whose output voltage is **linearly proportional to the Celsius temperature**. It is a classic example of a **self-powered analog transducer** that does not require external calibration or trimming.

**Working Principle:**
1. The internal bandgap reference circuit generates a voltage proportional to absolute temperature (PTAT)
2. An internal trimming circuit shifts and scales this voltage to give a **10 mV/°C** output at the OUT pin
3. The output is referenced to ground, so reading the output voltage and dividing by 0.01 directly yields temperature in °C

**LM35 Pinout (TO-92 Package):**
- Pin 1 → $V_{CC}$ (Supply, 4 V to 20 V)
- Pin 2 → $V_{OUT}$ (Analog Output)
- Pin 3 → GND

### B. Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect-Based)

A **thermocouple** consists of two dissimilar metals joined at a junction. When the junction is heated, a small EMF is generated based on the **Seebeck Effect**:

$$E = S(T) \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $S(T)$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** in μV/°C and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions.

### C. RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector

An **RTD** exploits the predictable change in electrical resistance of pure metals (typically platinum) with temperature:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right]$$

The **PT100** (100 Ω at 0°C) and **PT1000** (1000 Ω at 0°C) are the industry standards.

### D. Thermistor (NTC Type)

A **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** is a semiconductor whose resistance decreases exponentially with temperature:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

Where $B$ is the **beta constant** (typically 3000–5000 K) and all temperatures are in Kelvin.

---

## 2.2 Humidity Measurement — Operating Principles

### A. Capacitive Humidity Sensor (DHT11/DHT22 Internal)

The DHT11/DHT22 use an internal **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** and a **thermistor** for temperature. Operating principle:

1. A thin polymer dielectric layer absorbs water molecules from the air
2. As humidity changes, the dielectric constant of the polymer changes
3. This alters the capacitance of a small interdigitated capacitor on a chip
4. An internal ASIC measures the capacitance and converts it to a digital signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DHT sensor transmits a calibrated **digital signal** over a single-wire protocol, so no ADC is needed on the microcontroller side — this is a key feature in KTU lab interfacing.

### B. Psychrometer (Wet & Dry Bulb Method)

The classical **sling psychrometer** uses two mercury thermometers:
- **Dry bulb** measures ambient air temperature
- **Wet bulb** has a wick moistened with water; evaporation cools it

The **wet-bulb depression** (difference between dry and wet bulb readings) is used with psychrometric charts to determine RH.

$$RH = \frac{P_{w,wb} - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{P_{ws}(T_{db})} \times 100$$

Where $A$ is the psychrometric constant (≈ $0.0008$ °C⁻¹ at standard conditions).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature from LM35 | $T = \frac{V_{OUT}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$ | °C | Direct linear conversion |
| 2 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Ω | $\alpha$ ≈ $3.85 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C for Pt |
| 3 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Ω | $T$ in Kelvin; $B$ is a constant |
| 4 | Thermocouple EMF | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | mV | $S$ = Seebeck coefficient |
| 5 | Relative Humidity | $RH = \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T)} \times 100$ | % | $P_{ws}$ is temperature-dependent |
| 6 | Absolute Humidity | $\rho_v = \frac{P_w M_w}{R T}$ | kg/m³ | $M_w = 18$ g/mol for water |
| 7 | Dew Point (approx.) | $T_d = T - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$ | °C | Magnus formula approximation |
| 8 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | V/step | $n$ = ADC bits (Arduino: $n=10$, $V_{ref}=5$ V) |
| 9 | Digital Output of DHT | $RH\% = \frac{Byte1 + Byte2/10}{1}$ | % | From sensor data bytes |
| 10 | Temperature from DHT | $T^\circ C = \frac{Byte3 + Byte4/10}{1}$ | °C | Sign bit in Byte4 for negatives |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Questions on ADC resolution and digital conversion are extremely common (3–4 marks). Remember for Arduino Uno: $V_{ref} = 5$ V (default), $n = 10$ bits → $\Delta V = 4.88$ mV per step.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Systems

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Medical | Incubators, sterilizers, cold-chain drug storage |
| Agriculture | Greenhouses, soil-moisture-linked irrigation |
| Industrial | Boiler temperature monitoring, HVAC chillers |
| Electronics | Server-room cooling, battery thermal management |
| Metrology | Reference standard for calibrating other sensors |
| IoT | Smart-home thermostats, weather nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU lab emphasizes **calibration** — students must compare sensor readings against a **mercury-in-glass thermometer** (for temperature) and a **sling psychrometer** (for humidity) as reference standards, then compute percentage error.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Temperature Calculation from LM35 Output

The LM35 sensor produces an output voltage $V_{OUT}$ that is directly proportional to the Celsius temperature with a scale factor of **10 mV/°C**.

### Step 1: Define the Linear Relationship

$$V_{OUT} = k \cdot T$$

Where $k = 10$ mV/°C is the scale factor and $T$ is temperature in °C.

### Step 2: Solve for Temperature

$$T = \frac{V_{OUT}}{k}$$

### Step 3: Practical Application — Sample Calculation

Given: $V_{OUT}$ measured by a multimeter (or ADC) = $253$ mV

$$T = \frac{253 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$T = 25.3 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 4: When Using an Arduino ADC

The Arduino analogRead() returns a value between 0 and 1023 for an input of 0 to 5 V. The voltage per step is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1024} = 4.883 \text{ mV/step}$$

So the temperature from Arduino readings is:

$$T(°C) = \frac{\text{analogRead()} \times 4.883 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$T(°C) = \frac{\text{analogRead()} \times 4.883}{10}$$

$$T(°C) = \text{analogRead()} \times 0.4883$$

### Step 5: Worked Example

If analogRead() returns 250:

$$T = 250 \times 0.4883$$

$$T = 122.075 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This exceeds the LM35's typical range (0–100°C), indicating the LM35 is overloaded or the wiring is incorrect — a classic troubleshooting scenario in KTU lab exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ADC Digital Code to Temperature (General Method)

### Step 1: General ADC Equation

For an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$:

$$D = \text{round} \left( \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n \right)$$

### Step 2: Solve for Input Voltage

$$V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

### Step 3: Apply to LM35

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10 \text{ mV}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Arduino Parameters ($V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 10$)

$$T = \frac{D \times 5}{1024 \times 0.01} = \frac{5D}{10.24}$$

$$T = 0.48828 \cdot D \text{ °C}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Relative Humidity from DHT11 Sensor Data

The DHT11 transmits **40 bits** of data: 8 bits integer RH, 8 bits decimal RH, 8 bits integer T, 8 bits decimal T, 8 bits checksum.

### Step 1: Humidity Bytes (Word Format)

$$RH_{raw} = \text{Byte}_1 \times 10 + \text{Byte}_2$$

Wait — actual DHT11 protocol concatenates the bytes directly:

$$RH(\%) = \text{Byte}_1 \cdot 1 + \text{Byte}_2 \cdot \frac{1}{10}$$

Where Byte_1 is integer part and Byte_2 is decimal part.

### Step 2: Temperature Bytes (With Sign)

For positive temperatures:

$$T(°C) = \text{Byte}_3 \cdot 1 + \text{Byte}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{10}$$

For negative temperatures, the MSB of Byte_4 is set to 1, and:

$$T(°C) = -\left[ (\text{Byte}_4 \text{ AND } 0x7F) \cdot 1 + \text{Byte}_3 \cdot \frac{1}{10} \right]$$

### Step 3: Checksum Verification

$$\text{Checksum} = (\text{Byte}_1 + \text{Byte}_2 + \text{Byte}_3 + \text{Byte}_4) \mod 256$$

The received checksum byte should equal this computed value. If not, the reading is invalid and must be discarded.

### Step 4: Sample DHT11 Data Frame

Suppose the sensor transmits: `0011 0010 0000 0000 0001 1000 0000 0000 0100 1010`

- Byte_1 (RH integer) = 50
- Byte_2 (RH decimal) = 0
- Byte_3 (T integer) = 24
- Byte_4 (T decimal) = 0
- Checksum = 74

**Result:**

$$RH = 50.0\%$$

$$T = 24.0 \text{ °C}$$

**Verification:**

$$(50 + 0 + 24 + 0) \mod 256 = 74 \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Thermistor B-Constant Equation

The **β (beta) equation** allows conversion of thermistor resistance to temperature:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

### Step 1: Take Natural Logarithm

$$\ln \left( \frac{R_T}{R_0} \right) = \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$

### Step 2: Solve for T

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln \left( \frac{R_T}{R_0} \right)$$

$$T = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln \left( \frac{R_T}{R_0} \right)}$$

### Step 3: Sample Numerical Problem

Given: $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at $T_0 = 25$ °C $= 298.15$ K, $\beta = 3950$ K
Measured: $R_T = 6.81$ kΩ

$$T = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{1}{3950} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{6810}{10000} \right)}$$

Compute the logarithm:

$$\ln(0.681) = -0.384$$

$$T = \frac{1}{0.003354 + \frac{-0.384}{3950}}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{0.003354 - 0.0000972}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{0.003257}$$

$$T = 307.0 \text{ K} = 33.85 \text{ °C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When using the β-equation in numerical problems, **always convert temperatures to Kelvin first** to avoid unit-inconsistency errors. Marks are commonly lost for failing to do this conversion.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Psychrometric RH Calculation

### Step 1: Wet-Bulb Depression

$$\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$$

### Step 2: Saturation Pressure at Wet-Bulb Temperature

Using the Antoine equation (simplified):

$$P_{ws}(T_{wb}) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 \cdot T_{wb}}{T_{wb} + 243.5}}$$

### Step 3: Actual Vapour Pressure

$$P_w = P_{ws}(T_{wb}) - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$

### Step 4: RH Calculation

$$RH(\%) = \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T_{db})} \times 100$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Given:
- $T_{db} = 30$ °C
- $T_{wb} = 25$ °C
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- $A = 0.0008$ °C⁻¹

Compute $P_{ws}(T_{wb})$:

$$P_{ws}(25) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5}}$$

$$P_{ws}(25) = 6.112 \cdot e^{1.6463}$$

$$P_{ws}(25) = 6.112 \times 5.188 = 31.71 \text{ hPa}$$

Compute $P_{ws}(T_{db})$:

$$P_{ws}(30) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67 \times 30}{30 + 243.5}}$$

$$P_{ws}(30) = 6.112 \cdot e^{1.9376}$$

$$P_{ws}(30) = 6.112 \times 6.943 = 42.43 \text{ hPa}$$

Compute $P_w$:

$$P_w = 31.71 - 0.0008 \times 1013.25 \times (30 - 25)$$

$$P_w = 31.71 - 4.053 = 27.66 \text{ hPa}$$

Compute RH:

$$RH = \frac{27.66}{42.43} \times 100 = 65.2\%$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Error and Calibration

| Measurement Type | Reference Standard | Typical Sensor Error | Calibration Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (LM35) | Mercury thermometer | $\pm 0.5$ °C | Two-point (0°C ice bath, 100°C boiling water) |
| Humidity (DHT11) | Sling psychrometer | $\pm 5$ % RH | Saturated salt solution (e.g., NaCl gives ~75% RH) |
| Temperature (RTD) | Platinum resistance standard | $\pm 0.1$ °C | Comparison in calibration bath |
| Humidity (Capacitive) | Dew-point hygrometer | $\pm 2$ % RH | Two-point (dry + saturated salt) |

**Percentage Error Formula:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \text{Measured} - \text{Actual} \vert}{\text{Actual}} \times 100$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Temperature & Humidity Measurement

The following block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of the experiment, mapping signal flow from physical phenomena to digital output.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT] --> B[Air Temperature]
    A --> C[Water Vapour in Air]
    B --> D[LM35 SENSOR]
    C --> E[DHT11 SENSOR]
    D --> F[Analog Voltage Signal 10 mV per degree C]
    E --> G[Digital Single Wire Protocol 40 Bit Frame]
    F --> H[ARDUINO ADC CHANNEL A0]
    G --> I[ARDUINO DIGITAL PIN D2]
    H --> J[ATMEGA328P MICROCONTROLLER]
    I --> J
    J --> K[LCD DISPLAY 16 BY 2]
    J --> L[SERIAL MONITOR UART]
    J --> M[BUZZER ALARM THRESHOLD EXCEEDED]
    K --> N[VISUAL READOUT]
    L --> O[DATA LOGGING PC]
    M --> P[ALERT NOTIFICATION]
```

**Signal Flow Explanation:**
- The **LM35** converts ambient temperature into an analog voltage
- The **DHT11** converts humidity (and temperature) into a digital bit-stream
- The **microcontroller** processes both signals and displays them
- Threshold-based **alarms** trigger a buzzer when limits are violated

---
