The effective arm resistance is the **sum**: $S = R_x + 50\ \Omega$. > > 3. **Reporting Galvanometer Current as Non-Zero at Balance:** A **common 1-mark deduction** occurs when students calculate $I_g \neq 0$ in a balanced bridge. The balance condition *by definition* means $I_g = 0$. > > 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always report resistance in $\Omega$, current in mA or $\mu$A, and sensitivity in $\mu\text{A}/\Omega$. Mixing k$\Omega$ and $\Omega$ in the same equation leads to **2-mark deductions**. > > 5. **Missing the Thevenin Steps in Derivations:** When the question asks to "derive using Thevenin's theorem", students often use only KVL/KCL. The examiner specifically looks for: (a) computation of $V_{th}$ with galvanometer removed, (b) computation of $R_{th}$ with source shorted, and (c) final expression $I_g = V_{th}/(R_{th} + R_g)$. > > 6. **Sign Convention Errors in $I_g$:** The sign of $(QR - SP)$ determines the direction. If the question asks for the **direction**, do not just report the magnitude. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - **Core Balance Equation:** $P \cdot S = Q \cdot R$ — the **most important equation** in this module. Memorize it cold. - **Ratio Form:** $\dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{S}$ — used when the bridge is described in terms of ratio arms. - **Unknown Resistance Formula:** $R_x = \dfrac{Q \cdot R}{P}$ (when $R_x$ is arm $S$). - **Galvanometer at Balance:** Always **zero** — no exceptions. - **Bridge Components:** Four arms, one galvanometer, one DC source, connecting wires, breadboard. - **DRB = Standard Arm:** The Decade Resistance Box replaces the known standard resistance. - **Thevenin's Theorem:** $I_g = V_{th}/(R_{th} + R_g)$; the $V_{th}$ comes from the open-circuit bridge, $R_{th}$ from the source-shorted bridge. - **Sensitivity Maximum:** Achieved when ratio arms satisfy $a = \sqrt{R \cdot (R_g + R)}$; in practice, **unity ratio** is used. - **Measurement Range:** $1\ \Omega$ to $1\ \text{M}\Omega$ reliably; outside this range, use Kelvin Bridge (low) or Megger (high). - **Real-World Applications:** Strain gauges, load cells, RTD interfaces, pressure sensors — all use Wheatstone-derived bridge circuits. - **Common Units:** Resistance in $\Omega$, current in mA or $\mu$A, sensitivity in $\mu\text{A}/\Omega$, source voltage in V. - **Lab Procedure Order:** Identify arms → Set ratio → Place unknown → Vary DRB → Null achieved → Record → Calculate → Compare with multimeter. - **Error Sources:** Contact resistance, DRB tolerance, galvanometer resolution, temperature drift, source voltage fluctuation. - **KTU Viva Questions:** "Why is galvanometer current zero at balance?", "What is Thevenin's theorem?", "What is the role of ratio arms?", "How does this differ from Kelvin Bridge?". - **Excitation Voltage:** Standard values in KTU labs are $5\text{ V}$ or $10\text{ V}$ DC. - **Galvanometer Sensitivity Class:** Moving-coil (PMMC) type, sensitivity in the range $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ A/division. - **Difference from Potentiometer:** Potentiometer measures voltage; Wheatstone Bridge measures resistance. Both use null-deflection principle.

topics.title: JK and Master Slave JK FF using NAND Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 4
> - **Topic:** JK and Master Slave JK FF using NAND Gates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

A **JK Flip-Flop** is a sequential digital bistable device constructed using **NAND gates** that overcomes the forbidden state limitation of the conventional SR flip-flop. When both inputs $J = 1$ and $K = 1$ are asserted simultaneously, the JK flip-flop does not enter an invalid state; instead, it executes a **toggle operation**, complementing its current output ($Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$).

A **Master-Slave JK Flip-Flop** is a cascaded configuration of two JK flip-flops (a *master* stage and a *slave* stage) coupled through an *inverted clock* signal. This architecture eliminates the **race-around condition** that plagues the basic JK flip-flop during a single clock pulse when the propagation delay is less than the clock pulse width.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The lab experiment tests the student's ability to (i) construct JK and Master-Slave JK flip-flops from discrete NAND gate ICs (typically IC 7400), (ii) verify the characteristic truth table experimentally, (iii) observe and plot the timing diagram on a CRO/DSO, and (iv) demonstrate the elimination of the race-around condition in the master-slave configuration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

### The JK Flip-Flop — "The Bistable Light Switch with Toggle"
Imagine a **staircase light switch** (the kind that can be toggled from either the top or bottom of a stairwell). The JK flip-flop behaves identically:
- **Set input (J = 1, K = 0):** Light turns **ON** and stays ON.
- **Reset input (J = 0, K = 1):** Light turns **OFF** and stays OFF.
- **No input (J = 0, K = 0):** Light **remembers** its current state (memory).
- **Both pressed (J = 1, K = 1):** Light **toggles** (ON becomes OFF, OFF becomes ON).

The genius of the JK flip-flop is this last behavior — pressing both buttons simultaneously no longer creates a "forbidden" or unpredictable state; instead, it gracefully **flips** the state.

### The Master-Slave JK — "The Two-Stage Locking Mechanism"
Think of a **bank vault with two doors**:
- The **outer door (master)** opens first, accepting your input.
- The **inner door (slave)** only opens *after* the outer door has closed, allowing the stored value to reach the vault.
- This sequential opening ensures the system **never processes a changing input while its output is being read**, eliminating feedback chaos (race-around).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key IC and Timing Parameters (TTL Family — IC 7400 Quad 2-Input NAND):**
> - **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** $\mathbf{10 \text{ ns}}$ (typical for 74LS00)
> - **Clock Pulse Width Minimum ($t_w$):** $\mathbf{20 \text{ ns}}$
> - **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** $\mathbf{20 \text{ ns}}$
> - **Hold Time ($t_h$):** $\mathbf{5 \text{ ns}}$
> - **Maximum Clock Frequency ($f_{max}$):** $\mathbf{35 \text{ MHz}}$
> - **Operating Voltage ($V_{CC}$):** $\mathbf{+5 \text{ V DC}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Rule:** The race-around condition occurs when the **clock pulse width ($t_w$) is greater than the propagation delay ($t_{pd}$) of the flip-flop**, i.e., $t_w > t_{pd}$. The Master-Slave configuration removes this dependency.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Basic JK Flip-Flop Using NAND Gates

The basic JK flip-flop is built from exactly **four 2-input NAND gates**, arranged in a feedback cross-coupled topology:

- **Gates G1 and G2** form a standard cross-coupled SR latch (the storage element).
- **Gates G3 and G4** form the input steering network, which accepts $J$, $K$, and the *feedback* from $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$.

The fundamental input equations at the latch are:
$$\overline{S} = \overline{J \cdot \overline{Q} \cdot \overline{Clk}}$$
$$\overline{R} = \overline{K \cdot Q \cdot \overline{Clk}}$$

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Case 1: $J = 0$, $K = 0$ (Hold State)**
- Both G3 and G4 outputs are logic HIGH (since one input to each is LOW).
- The cross-coupled NAND latch receives $\overline{S} = 1$ and $\overline{R} = 1$, which is the *hold* condition.
- **Result:** $Q_{next} = Q$ (no change).

**Case 2: $J = 1$, $K = 0$ (Set State)**
- G3 output depends on $Q$ and $J = 1$. If $\overline{Q} = 1$ (i.e., $Q = 0$), G3 forces $\overline{S} = 0$, setting the latch.
- **Result:** $Q_{next} = 1$.

**Case 3: $J = 0$, $K = 1$ (Reset State)**
- G4 activates, forcing $\overline{R} = 0$, resetting the latch.
- **Result:** $Q_{next} = 0$.

**Case 4: $J = 1$, $K = 1$ (Toggle State)**
- Both G3 and G4 are enabled. The feedback from $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ ensures only one of G3 or G4 produces a LOW output.
- If $Q = 0$: G3 activates (since $J = 1$ and $\overline{Q} = 1$), setting the latch. New $Q = 1$.
- If $Q = 1$: G4 activates (since $K = 1$ and $Q = 1$), resetting the latch. New $Q = 0$.
- **Result:** $Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$.

## 2.3 The Race-Around Condition

When $J = K = 1$ and the clock pulse width $t_w$ is **longer** than the propagation delay $t_{pd}$ of the gates, the output will continuously toggle multiple times during a single clock HIGH interval. This phenomenon is called the **race-around condition**.

**Mathematical Constraint for Race-Around:**
$$t_w \gg t_{pd}$$

The number of spurious toggles is given by:
$$N_{toggles} = \left\lfloor \frac{t_w}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The toggle problem is the entire reason the Master-Slave JK flip-flop exists.** This is a high-yield KTU exam point.

## 2.4 The Master-Slave JK Flip-Flop Architecture

The Master-Slave configuration uses **two JK flip-flops in cascade** with an **inverting clock buffer** between them:

- **Master Stage:** Clocked by the *direct* clock signal $Clk$. Active during the **HIGH** level of $Clk$.
- **Inverter:** Produces $\overline{Clk}$.
- **Slave Stage:** Clocked by $\overline{Clk}$. Active during the **LOW** level of $Clk$ (i.e., when master is inactive).

**Operational Sequence:**
1. **During $Clk = 1$:** Master is *enabled* and accepts $J$ and $K$ inputs. Slave is *disabled* (since $\overline{Clk} = 0$), so the external output $Q$ holds its previous value.
2. **During $Clk = 0$:** Master is *disabled* and *isolates* its outputs from further input changes. Slave is *enabled* and copies the master's final stored value to $Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The crucial insight:** The slave is **only ever enabled after** the master has finished processing. This breaks the feedback loop and entirely eliminates the race-around condition.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Condition / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Characteristic Equation (JK FF) | $Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$ | Boolean algebra form |
| Excitation Table — J | $J = Q_{next} \cdot \overline{Q} + \overline{Q_{next}} \cdot Q$ | Toggle condition |
| Excitation Table — K | $K = \overline{Q_{next}} \cdot \overline{Q} + Q_{next} \cdot Q$ | Reset condition |
| Toggle Frequency (divide-by-2) | $f_{out} = f_{clk} / 2$ | When $J = K = 1$ |
| Race-Around Toggle Count | $N = \left\lfloor t_w / (2 t_{pd}) \right\rfloor$ | Times per clock pulse |
| Master Enable Condition | $Clk = 1$ (HIGH) | Level-triggered |
| Slave Enable Condition | $Clk = 0$ (LOW) | Level-triggered inverted |
| Setup Time | $t_{su} \geq 20$ ns | TTL standard |
| Hold Time | $t_h \geq 5$ ns | TTL standard |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{pd} \approx 10$ ns | Per gate (74LS00) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the JK Flip-Flop Characteristic Equation

We begin with the **SR latch characteristic equation** (active-LOW inputs):
$$Q_{next} = S + \overline{R} \cdot Q \quad \text{with constraint } S \cdot R = 0$$

For the JK flip-flop, the inputs to the internal SR latch are:
$$S = J \cdot \overline{Q} \cdot Clk$$
$$R = K \cdot Q \cdot Clk$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $S$ and $R$ into the latch equation. Treating $Clk = 1$ (the active period):
$$Q_{next} = (J \cdot \overline{Q}) + \overline{(K \cdot Q)} \cdot Q$$

**Step 2:** Apply De Morgan's theorem to $\overline{(K \cdot Q)}$:
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + (\overline{K} + \overline{Q}) \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the second term:
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q + \overline{Q}Q$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the Boolean identity $\overline{Q}Q = 0$ (a variable ANDed with its complement is always 0):
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q + 0$$

**Step 5:** The simplified **JK Characteristic Equation** is:
$$\boxed{Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **No constraint equation is required** ($S \cdot R = 0$ is automatically satisfied because $J \cdot \overline{Q} \cdot K \cdot Q = 0$ identically). This is precisely why the JK flip-flop has *no forbidden state*.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Excitation Table

The excitation table gives the required $J$ and $K$ inputs to transition the flip-flop from $Q$ to $Q_{next}$.

**Transition: $Q = 0 \rightarrow Q_{next} = 0$ (Hold at 0)**
Required: Set must not activate. So $J = 0$. $K$ can be 0 or 1.
$$J = 0, \quad K = X \text{ (don't care)}$$

**Transition: $Q = 0 \rightarrow Q_{next} = 1$ (Set)**
Required: $J\overline{Q}$ term must dominate. Since $Q = 0$, $\overline{Q} = 1$, so $J = 1$ sets. $K$ can be anything.
$$J = 1, \quad K = X$$

**Transition: $Q = 1 \rightarrow Q_{next} = 0$ (Reset)**
Required: $\overline{K}Q$ term must be 0, so $\overline{K} = 0$, meaning $K = 1$. $J$ can be anything.
$$J = X, \quad K = 1$$

**Transition: $Q = 1 \rightarrow Q_{next} = 1$ (Hold at 1)**
Required: No toggle. $J$ can be anything, $K = 0$.
$$J = X, \quad K = 0$$

**For Toggle ($Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$):** Both $J$ and $K$ must equal **1**.

## 3.3 The Complete JK Flip-Flop Truth Table

| $J$ | $K$ | $Clk$ | $Q$ | $Q_{next}$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $\uparrow$ | 0 | 0 | Hold (No Change) |
| 0 | 0 | $\uparrow$ | 1 | 1 | Hold (No Change) |
| 0 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| 0 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 1 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | $\uparrow$ | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 0 | $\uparrow$ | 1 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 0 | 1 | Toggle |
| 1 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 1 | 0 | Toggle |

## 3.4 Master-Slave Timing Analysis (Quantitative)

Consider a clock signal with pulse width $t_w = 100$ ns and gate propagation delay $t_{pd} = 10$ ns.

**For a basic JK flip-flop with $J = K = 1$:**
- Number of toggles per clock pulse:
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{t_w}{2 \cdot t_{pd}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{100}{2 \times 10} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 5 \right\rfloor = 5 \text{ toggles}$$

This means the output changes **5 times** during a single clock pulse, producing an unpredictable final state.

**For the Master-Slave JK flip-flop:**
- During $Clk = 1$ ($50$ ns duration): Master toggles $\lfloor 50 / 20 \rfloor = 2$ times internally.
- At the falling edge ($Clk = 0$): The slave latches the **final** master value and presents it to $Q$.
- **Result:** Exactly **1 deterministic toggle** per clock cycle, regardless of $t_w / t_{pd}$ ratio.

$$\boxed{Q_{MS} \text{ toggles exactly once per clock cycle} \iff J = K = 1}$$

## 3.5 Conversion from JK to D-Type and T-Type Flip-Flops

**D-type from JK:** Tie $K = \overline{J}$ (i.e., $J = D$, $K = \overline{D}$).
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q = D\overline{Q} + DQ = D(\overline{Q} + Q) = D$$
$$\therefore Q_{next} = D$$

**T-type from JK:** Tie $J = K = T$.
$$Q_{next} = T\overline{Q} + \overline{T}Q = T \oplus Q$$
For $T = 1$: $Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$ (toggle). For $T = 0$: $Q_{next} = Q$ (hold).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Conversion:** Students should be able to derive the conversion logic for converting a JK FF into a D FF or T FF in the lab viva. This is a recurring 7-mark question pattern.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** What is the race-around condition in a JK flip-flop? How is it eliminated in a master-slave JK flip-flop?

**Model Answer:**
The **race-around condition** occurs in a basic JK flip-flop when $J = K = 1$ and the clock pulse width $t_w$ is greater than the propagation delay $t_{pd}$ of the gates. During this time, the output toggles multiple times within a single clock pulse, leading to an **unpredictable final state**.

In a **master-slave JK flip-flop**, two JK flip-flops are cascaded: a *master* (clocked by $Clk$) and a *slave* (clocked by $\overline{Clk}$). The master is enabled only when $Clk = 1$, and the slave is enabled only when $Clk = 0$. Since the slave is disabled while the master is active, the feedback path to the master is broken during the active clock phase. The slave copies the master's final state only when the master is isolated. This sequential activation eliminates the race-around condition. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Write the characteristic equation of a JK flip-flop and explain why the JK flip-flop has no forbidden state unlike the SR flip-flop.

**Model Answer:**
The **characteristic equation** of a JK flip-flop is:
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$$

In an SR flip-flop, the input condition $S = R = 1$ is **forbidden** because it forces both outputs to logic 0, violating the complementary relationship $Q \neq \overline{Q}$. In a JK flip-flop, the internal cross-coupling ensures that $J = K = 1$ results in a valid **toggle** operation rather than an invalid state. The feedback signals $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ guarantee that only one of the two input NAND gates can be activated at any time, making the $J = K = 1$ case deterministic. Thus, **no input combination produces a forbidden state**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Pattern, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat logic diagram, explain the operation of a **JK flip-flop using NAND gates**. Derive its characteristic equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **master-slave JK flip-flop** in detail. Show how it eliminates the race-around condition with a suitable timing diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Logic Diagram:** The JK flip-flop consists of four 2-input NAND gates (G1, G2, G3, G4). Gates G3 and G4 form a cross-coupled SR latch. Gates G1 and G2 act as input steering logic, accepting $J$, $K$, $Clk$, and feedback from $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$.

**Operation:**
- $J = 0$, $K = 0$: Both G1 and G2 outputs are HIGH, latch holds previous state. $Q_{next} = Q$.
- $J = 1$, $K = 0$: G1 is enabled (since $\overline{Q}$ may be 1), $\overline{S} = 0$ sets the latch. $Q_{next} = 1$.
- $J = 0$, $K = 1$: G2 is enabled (since $Q$ may be 1), $\overline{R} = 0$ resets the latch. $Q_{next} = 0$.
- $J = 1$, $K = 1$: Both G1 and G2 are enabled. The feedback from $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ ensures only one steering gate activates. If $Q = 0$, G1 sets it to 1. If $Q = 1$, G2 resets it to 0. **Result:** $Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$ (toggle).

**Characteristic Equation Derivation:**

The SR latch equation (active-LOW inputs):
$$Q_{next} = S + \overline{R}Q$$

Substitute $S = J\overline{Q}$ and $R = KQ$ (during $Clk = 1$):
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{KQ} \cdot Q$$
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + (\overline{K} + \overline{Q})Q$$
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q + \overline{Q}Q$$
$$Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q + 0$$
$$\boxed{Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q}$$

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Master-Slave JK Flip-Flop Architecture:**
The master-slave JK flip-flop consists of two cascaded JK flip-flops. The first is the *master*, clocked by the direct clock signal $Clk$. The second is the *slave*, clocked by $\overline{Clk}$ (inverted clock). The outputs of the master feed the inputs of the slave.

**Clock Phase Operation:**

**Phase 1: $Clk = 1$ (Master active, Slave isolated)**
- The master accepts the $J$ and $K$ inputs and updates its internal state.
- The slave, clocked by $\overline{Clk} = 0$, is disabled. Its outputs $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ remain frozen.
- The external output $Q$ does not change, breaking the feedback path that causes race-around.

**Phase 2: $Clk = 0$ (Master isolated, Slave active)**
- The master, now clocked by $Clk = 0$, is disabled and isolates its outputs.
- The slave, now clocked by $\overline{Clk} = 1$, is enabled. It reads the master's final stored value and transfers it to the external outputs $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$.

**Why Race-Around is Eliminated:**
In the basic JK flip-flop, when $J = K = 1$, the output feeds back to the input during the same clock HIGH interval, causing multiple toggles. In the master-slave configuration, the feedback from $Q$ to the master's input is taken from the **slave's output**, which is **frozen** while the master is active. Therefore, the master sees a *constant* feedback value during the entire HIGH phase of the clock, ensuring exactly **one deterministic toggle per clock cycle**. The propagation delay of the master is irrelevant because its output is not connected back to itself in a destabilizing way.

**Timing Diagram (Description):**
- Clock: square wave alternating between 0 and 1.
- $J = K = 1$: held constant.
- $Q_m$ (master): toggles during $Clk = 1$ but is isolated from the output.
- $Q$ (slave output): updates only at the **falling edge** of $Clk$, copying the master's final value.
- Result: A clean, single toggle at every falling clock edge, with no race-around.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **JK flip-flop using NAND gates** and explain its operation with the help of a **truth table**. Discuss the **drawbacks** of this configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **T flip-flop using a master-slave JK flip-flop** and explain its operation. What is the **maximum clock frequency** at which the flip-flop can be reliably operated if the propagation delay of each gate is 15 ns? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Circuit Diagram:** The JK flip-flop uses four 2-input NAND gates. G1 and G2 are the input steering gates that combine $J$/$K$ inputs with feedback from $Q$/$\overline{Q}$, gated by $Clk$. G3 and G4 are the cross-coupled NAND latch that stores the bistable state.

**Truth Table:**

| $J$ | $K$ | $Q$ | $Q_{next}$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Hold |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Hold |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Reset |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Toggle |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Toggle |

**Drawbacks:**
1. **Race-around condition:** When $J = K = 1$ and $t_w > t_{pd}$, the output toggles multiple times within one clock pulse, producing an indeterminate final state.
2. **Propagation delay sensitivity:** The output is highly sensitive to gate delays, making timing analysis critical.
3. **Direct feedback instability:** The output directly feeds the input logic, creating a closed loop that is prone to oscillation under certain input conditions.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**T Flip-Flop Design from Master-Slave JK FF:**

A T (Toggle) flip-flop is obtained by tying both $J$ and $K$ inputs to a common input $T$:
$$J = K = T$$

The characteristic equation becomes:
$$Q_{next} = T\overline{Q} + \overline{T}Q = T \oplus Q$$

**Operation:**
- $T = 0$: $Q_{next} = Q$ (no change, hold).
- $T = 1$: $Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$ (toggle).

The master-slave configuration ensures that the toggle is deterministic and race-around free, regardless of the clock pulse width.

**Maximum Clock Frequency Calculation:**

For reliable operation, the clock period $T_{clk}$ must be at least **twice the propagation delay** through the master-slave chain (one delay for the master, one for the slave). Given $t_{pd} = 15$ ns per gate and the master-slave uses 2 stages of equivalent propagation:

$$T_{clk(min)} = 2 \times t_{pd} = 2 \times 15 \text{ ns} = 30 \text{ ns}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^{-9}} = 33.33 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 33.33 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $t_w > t_{pd}$:** Students often state "race-around occurs when $J = K = 1$" without specifying the timing condition. Always mention that $t_w$ must exceed $t_{pd}$.
> 2. **Drawing only the block diagram, not the NAND gate-level diagram:** KTU lab exams specifically require the **gate-level circuit** for full marks (typically 4 marks allocated for the diagram).
> 3. **Incorrect feedback labeling:** The feedback in a JK flip-flop is from $Q$ to the $K$-side NAND gate and from $\overline{Q}$ to the $J$-side NAND gate. Swapping these loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not showing clock inversion in master-slave:** Many students forget to draw the NOT gate between the master's clock input and the slave's clock input. This is worth 2 marks in the timing diagram.
> 5. **Confusing "level-triggered" with "edge-triggered":** The master-slave JK FF is **level-triggered** (master on HIGH, slave on LOW), not edge-triggered. A true edge-triggered JK FF requires a different three-stage design. The master-slave is sometimes called a "pulse-triggered" FF.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — JK and Master-Slave JK FF Using NAND Gates**

- **JK FF** is a 4-NAND-gate bistable device that **eliminates the forbidden state** of the SR flip-flop.
- The **characteristic equation** is $Q_{next} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$.
- When $J = K = 1$, the output **toggles** ($Q_{next} = \overline{Q}$).
- The **race-around condition** occurs when $J = K = 1$ AND clock pulse width $t_w$ > propagation delay $t_{pd}$.
- The number of spurious toggles is $N = \lfloor t_w / (2 t_{pd}) \rfloor$.
- The **Master-Slave JK FF** uses two cascaded JK flip-flops with an **inverted clock** to the slave.
- **Master is active during $Clk = 1$; Slave is active during $Clk = 0$.**
- The slave's output is **frozen** during the master's active phase, breaking the feedback loop and **eliminating race-around**.
- The **feedback** to the master comes from the **slave's output**, not the master's own output.
- The Master-Slave JK FF is **level-triggered** (pulse-triggered), not edge-triggered.
- **Setup time** $t_{su} \geq 20$ ns; **Hold time** $t_h \geq 5$ ns; **Propagation delay** $t_{pd} \approx 10$ ns (TTL).
- **IC 7400** (quad 2-input NAND) is the standard chip; 2 ICs needed for one master-slave JK FF.
- **Conversion:** $J = D, K = \overline{D}$ converts JK to D FF; $J = K = T$ converts JK to T FF.
- A T FF made from a master-slave JK FF with $T = 1$ acts as a **frequency divider by 2**: $f_{out} = f_{clk}/2$.
- **Maximum clock frequency** for reliable operation: $f_{max} = 1 / (2 \cdot t_{pd})$.
- The lab experiment uses a **breadboard**, IC 7400, IC 7404 (inverter), 5V DC supply, function generator for clock, and LEDs (with current-limiting resistors) to observe $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$.
- **Expected output verification:** When $J = K = 1$, $Q$ and $\overline{Q}$ LEDs should toggle at half the clock frequency, with **no flicker or oscillation** during a single clock pulse (confirming race-around elimination).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asynchronous Counter: 3 Bit Up/Down Counter, Realization of Mod N Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 4 > - **Topic:** Asynchronous Counter: 3 Bit Up/Down Counter, Realization of Mod N Counter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Asynchronous Counter (Ripple Counter) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** An **asynchronous counter** (also called a **ripple counter**) is a sequential logic circuit in which the clock input is applied **only to the first flip-flop**, and each subsequent flip-flop is triggered by the output (or a derived function of the output) of the preceding flip-flop. The clock signal therefore *ripples* through the chain, causing a propagation delay that accumulates with the number of stages. | Property | Value | |---|---| | Clock Source | First flip-flop only | | Triggering | Edge-triggered, cascaded | | Maximum Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot t_{pd}}$ | | Typical ICs | 7490, 7493, 74192, 74193 | ### 1.2 3-Bit Up/Down Counter A **3-bit counter** has $2^3 = \mathbf{8}$ distinct states. An **Up/Down counter** is a bidirectional counter whose counting direction is governed by a single control input (commonly labelled `MODE` or `UP/DN`). - **Up Mode:** Sequence $000 \rightarrow 001 \rightarrow 010 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow 111 \rightarrow 000$ - **Down Mode:** Sequence $111 \rightarrow 110 \rightarrow 101 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow 000 \rightarrow 111$ ### 1.3 Mod-N Counter > [!NOTE] > A **Mod-N (Modulo-N) counter** is a counter that cycles through exactly **N unique states** before returning to its initial state. The "modulus" N defines the count cycle length. For a counter built with $n$ flip-flops, the **natural (full) modulus** is $N = 2^n$. **Real-world examples of Mod-N counters:** | Application | Modulus (N) | Description | |---|---|---| | 12-hour clock | 12 | Hours cycle 1 → 12 | | 60-second timer | 60 | Seconds cycle 0 → 59 | | BCD decade counter | 10 | Digits 0 → 9 | | Hex counter | 16 | Digits 0 → F | ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (The Odometer Model) > [!TIP] > **The Car Odometer Analogy:** Imagine a mechanical odometer with three rotating wheels (digits). The rightmost wheel spins freely with every kilometre travelled. The middle wheel only advances **one position** when the rightmost wheel completes a full revolution and snaps back from 9 to 0. Similarly, the leftmost wheel only moves when the middle wheel resets. Each digit wheel "waits" for its neighbour — this is exactly how a ripple counter operates. The **up/down** feature is like a reversible ratchet: a single lever determines whether the odometer counts forward (forward gear) or backward (reverse gear). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle of a 3-Bit Asynchronous Up Counter The 3-bit asynchronous **up counter** is constructed using three **negative-edge-triggered JK flip-flops** (or T flip-flops) configured in toggle mode ($J = K = 1$). **Step-by-step ripple behaviour:** 1. **FF0 (LSB):** The external clock $CLK$ is fed to $CK_0$. On every falling edge, $Q_0$ toggles. 2. **FF1:** $CK_1 = Q_0$. On every falling edge of $Q_0$, $Q_1$ toggles. 3. **FF2 (MSB):** $CK_2 = Q_1$. On every falling edge of $Q_1$, $Q_2$ toggles. 4. Because each flip-flop triggers on the **falling (1 → 0)** transition of its clock, the count **advances** naturally in binary. ### 2.2 Conversion to Down Counter To obtain a **down counter**, the clock inputs of the subsequent flip-flops must be driven by the **complemented output** ($\bar{Q}$) of the previous stage. Since $\bar{Q}$ falls from 1 to 0 when $Q$ rises from 0 to 1, the next stage toggles on the **rising** transitions, producing a descending sequence. ### 2.3 Up/Down Counter with Mode Control > [!IMPORTANT] > A common design uses an **XOR gate** between the mode input $M$ and the previous output $Q_i$. The clock for the next stage becomes $CK_{i+1} = Q_i \oplus M$. > - When $M = 0$: $CK_{i+1} = Q_i$ → **Up Count** > - When $M = 1$: $CK_{i+1} = \bar{Q_i}$ → **Down Count** ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of states (full modulus) | $N = 2^n$ | dimensionless | $n$ = number of flip-flops | | 2 | Maximum count value | $N_{max} = 2^n - 1$ | decimal equivalent | Largest decimal value | | 3 | Flip-flops required for Mod-N | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | dimensionless | Smallest integer $\geq \log_2 N$ | | 4 | Maximum operating frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot t_{pd}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $t_{pd}$ = propagation delay of one FF | | 5 | Total propagation delay | $t_{total} = n \cdot t_{pd}$ | seconds (s) | Cumulative ripple delay | | 6 | Mod-N reset condition (truncated) | Detect $N$ in binary, AND outputs, feed to $\overline{CLEAR}$ | logical | For Mod-N $< 2^n$ | | 7 | Duty cycle of MSB | $50\%$ (full modulus only) | percent | Square-wave output | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Frequency Dividers:** A Mod-$2^n$ asynchronous counter divides the input clock frequency by $2^n$. For example, a 3-bit counter gives $\div 8$ division at $Q_2$. - **Digital Clocks & Timers:** Cascaded Mod-6 and Mod-10 counters form seconds/minutes counters. - **Event Counting:** Industrial batch counters, traffic sensors, and revolution counters. - **Address Generation:** Memory scanning in microcontrollers, address sequencers. - **Automotive Applications:** Odometer pulses, RPM measurement (with transducer interface). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Truth Table — 3-Bit Asynchronous Up Counter | Clock Pulse | $Q_2$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_0$ | Decimal Equivalent | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 | | 3 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 | | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 | | 5 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 5 | | 6 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 6 | | 7 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 7 | | 8 (rollover) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ### 3.2 Derivation of Modulus and Flip-Flop Count **Given:** Required modulus $N$. **Step 1 — Establish the binary word length requirement.** The number of distinct states must be at least $N$. $$2^n \geq N$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the minimum integer $n$.** $$n \geq \log_2 N$$ $$n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$$ **Step 3 — Verify with example: Mod-6 counter.** $$n = \lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = \lceil 2.585 \rceil = 3 \text{ flip-flops}$$ **Step 4 — The natural cycle length of 3 FFs is 8.** Since $8 > 6$, the counter must be **truncated** by forcing an asynchronous reset when the count reaches $N = 6$ (binary `110`). **Step 5 — Reset Logic Derivation.** Detect the illegal state `110`: $$R = Q_2 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0}$$ This signal is fed to the asynchronous $\overline{CLEAR}$ input of all three flip-flops, instantly returning the counter to `000` as soon as count `110` is reached. Therefore, the **actual counted sequence** is: $$000 \rightarrow 001 \rightarrow 010 \rightarrow 011 \rightarrow 100 \rightarrow 101 \rightarrow 000 \rightarrow \ldots$$ This is exactly **6 unique states**, confirming Mod-6 operation. ### 3.3 Derivation of Up/Down Mode Control Logic **Given:** Mode input $M$, output of FF0 is $Q_0$. **Step 1 — Recall the clock equation for the next stage.** For **up counting** ($M = 0$): $$CK_1 = Q_0$$ For **down counting** ($M = 1$): $$CK_1 = \overline{Q_0}$$ **Step 2 — Express both conditions with a single equation using XOR.** $$CK_1 = Q_0 \oplus M = Q_0 \overline{M} + \overline{Q_0} M$$ **Step 3 — Verify the two cases:** - If $M = 0$: $CK_1 = Q_0 \oplus 0 = Q_0$ ✓ (Up mode) - If $M = 1$: $CK_1 = Q_0 \oplus 1 = \overline{Q_0}$ ✓ (Down mode) **Step 4 — Generalise to subsequent stages:** $$CK_{i+1} = Q_i \oplus M, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-2$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Counter Types | Feature | Synchronous Counter | Asynchronous Up | Asynchronous Down | Asynchronous Up/Down | |---|---|---|---|---| | Clock distribution | All FFs clocked together | $CK_0$ external; $CK_i = Q_{i-1}$ | $CK_0$ external; $CK_i = \bar{Q}_{i-1}$ | $CK_i = Q_{i-1} \oplus M$ | | Propagation delay | $1 \cdot t_{pd}$ | $n \cdot t_{pd}$ | $n \cdot t_{pd}$ | $n \cdot t_{pd}$ | | Speed | **Fast** | Slow | Slow | Slow | | Hardware complexity | High (combinational logic) | **Low** | Low | Moderate (XOR gates) | | Glitches | Few | Common | Common | Common | | KTU relevance | High | **Very High** | Very High | **Very High** | ### 3.5 Worked Example: Design of Mod-10 (Decade) Counter **Problem:** Design a Mod-10 asynchronous counter using JK flip-flops. **Step 1 — Determine number of FFs.** $$n = \lceil \log_2 10 \rceil = 4$$ **Step 2 — Identify reset state.** The counter must reset on count `1010` (binary 10). **Step 3 — Derive reset equation.** $$R = Q_3 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_2} \cdot \overline{Q_0}$$ **Step 4 — The state `1010` is momentarily present (a "glitch state")** but the asynchronous clear forces the counter back to `0000` within one propagation delay. The visible states are `0000` through `1001` — exactly 10 states, as required. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — 3-Bit Asynchronous Up Counter ```mermaid graph LR clkIn["EXTERNAL CLOCK"]:::clk --> ff0["FF0 - LSB
J=K=1
Negative Edge"]:::ff ff0 -- "Q0" --> ff1["FF1 - MID
J=K=1
Clocked by Q0"]:::ff ff1 -- "Q1" --> ff2["FF2 - MSB
J=K=1
Clocked by Q1"]:::ff ff0 -- "Q0 output" --> out0["Q0 LSB"]:::out ff1 -- "Q1 output" --> out1["Q1"]:::out ff2 -- "Q2 output" --> out2["Q2 MSB"]:::out classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ff fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — 3-Bit Asynchronous Up/Down Counter with Mode Control ```mermaid graph LR clkIn["EXTERNAL CLOCK"]:::clk --> ff0["FF0 - LSB
J=K=1"]:::ff modeIn["MODE INPUT M
M=0: UP
M=1: DOWN"]:::ctrl --> xor1["XOR GATE 1"]:::xor modeIn --> xor2["XOR GATE 2"]:::xor ff0 -- "Q0" --> xor1 xor1 -- "Q0 XOR M" --> ff1["FF1 - MID
J=K=1"]:::ff ff1 -- "Q1" --> xor2 xor2 -- "Q1 XOR M" --> ff2["FF2 - MSB
J=K=1"]:::ff ff0 --> out0["Q0 LSB"]:::out ff1 --> out1["Q1"]:::out ff2 --> out2["Q2 MSB"]:::out classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ff fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef xor fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 State Transition Diagram — 3-Bit Up Counter ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> s000 s000 --> s001 : Clock 1 s001 --> s010 : Clock 2 s010 --> s011 : Clock 3 s011 --> s100 : Clock 4 s100 --> s101 : Clock 5 s101 --> s110 : Clock 6 s110 --> s111 : Clock 7 s111 --> s000 : Clock 8 Rollover ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Generic Mod-N Counter Realization ```mermaid graph LR clkIn["EXTERNAL CLOCK"]:::clk --> counter["N-BIT ASYNCHRONOUS
COUNTER 2 TO THE N"]:::ff counter -- "Q0 to Qn-1" --> decoder["STATE DECODER
AND GATE LOGIC"]:::decoder counter -- "Q0 to Qn-1" --> outputs["PARALLEL
STATE OUTPUTS"]:::out decoder -- "RESET PULSE
AT COUNT N" --> counter classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ff fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decoder fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.5 Timing Waveform Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR A["Waveform Set Description"]:::note --> B["CLK: Square wave continuous pulses"]:::note B --> C["Q0: Toggles every CLK pulse frequency divided by 2"]:::note C --> D["Q1: Toggles on falling edge of Q0 frequency divided by 4"]:::note D --> E["Q2: Toggles on falling edge of Q1 frequency divided by 8"]:::note classDef note fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Lab Tip:** When implementing on a breadboard, observe the **ripple delay** using a logic analyser or even a slow-clock source. You will clearly see $Q_0$ change first, then $Q_1$ a few nanoseconds later, then $Q_2$ — the visible "ripple" that gives this counter its name. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define an asynchronous counter. Why is it also called a *ripple counter*? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **asynchronous counter** is a sequential circuit in which the clock input is connected only to the first flip-flop (LSB), and each subsequent flip-flop is clocked by the output of the preceding flip-flop. *(1 mark)* It is called a **ripple counter** because the clock signal *ripples* through the chain of flip-flops — each flip-flop triggers the next one, similar to a wave propagating through water. *(1 mark)* The total propagation delay accumulates as $t_{total} = n \cdot t_{pd}$, which limits the maximum operating frequency to $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot t_{pd}}$. *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is a **Mod-N counter**? Give two real-world examples. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Mod-N counter** is a counter that cycles through exactly **N unique states** before resetting to its initial value. The integer N is called the *modulus* of the counter. *(1 mark)* The number of flip-flops required is $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$. *(1 mark)* **Real-world examples:** 1. **Mod-12 counter** — used in 12-hour digital clocks to count hours from 1 to 12. *(0.5 marks)* 2. **Mod-60 counter** — used in digital clocks and watches to count seconds (0–59) or minutes (0–59). *(0.5 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Design and explain the working of a **3-bit asynchronous up counter** using JK flip-flops. Draw the logic diagram, timing diagram, and state table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **Mod-6 asynchronous counter** using JK flip-flops. Show the reset logic derivation and explain why a state-glitch occurs. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 3-Bit Asynchronous Up Counter (7 marks):** **Circuit Description (2 marks):** Three negative-edge-triggered JK flip-flops ($FF_0$, $FF_1$, $FF_2$) are cascaded. The $J$ and $K$ inputs of all flip-flops are tied to logic **1** (toggle mode). The external clock $CLK$ is applied to $CK_0$. The output $Q_0$ drives $CK_1$, and $Q_1$ drives $CK_2$. **State Table (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1. **Timing Diagram Description (2 marks):** - $Q_0$ toggles on every falling edge of $CLK$ → frequency $\div 2$. - $Q_1$ toggles on every falling edge of $Q_0$ → frequency $\div 4$. - $Q_2$ toggles on every falling edge of $Q_1$ → frequency $\div 8$. **Working Explanation (1 mark):** Because each subsequent flip-flop is triggered by the falling edge of the previous output, the count increments by 1 in binary on every clock pulse. After 8 clock pulses, the counter rolls over from `111` back to `000`. **Part (b) — Mod-6 Asynchronous Counter (7 marks):** **Step 1 — FF count calculation (1 mark):** $$n = \lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = \lceil 2.585 \rceil = 3 \text{ flip-flops}$$ **Step 2 — Natural modulus of 3 FFs is 8** — we must truncate to 6. **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Reset logic derivation (3 marks):** The counter must reset when it reaches binary `110` (decimal 6). $$R = Q_2 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0}$$ This is fed asynchronously to the $\overline{CLEAR}$ input of all three flip-flops. **Step 4 — State-glitch explanation (2 marks):** The state `110` is briefly present at the outputs before the asynchronous clear activates. This momentary presence is called a **glitch** or **spurious state**. The width of this glitch equals the propagation delay from the moment the FF outputs settle to the moment the clear signal is asserted — typically a few nanoseconds. In high-speed applications, this glitch can cause false triggering of downstream logic and must be handled using synchronous reset designs. **Counted sequence:** $$000 \rightarrow 001 \rightarrow 010 \rightarrow 011 \rightarrow 100 \rightarrow 101 \rightarrow 000 \rightarrow \ldots$$ (6 unique states ✓) --- #### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram and truth table, explain the working of a **3-bit asynchronous up/down counter** using JK flip-flops and a mode control input. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A digital clock uses cascaded Mod-6 and Mod-10 counters to count seconds. **(i)** Determine the number of flip-flops required in each stage. **(ii)** Derive the reset logic for each stage. **(iii)** Explain why Mod-6 and Mod-10 are chosen (not Mod-60 directly). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 3-Bit Up/Down Counter (7 marks):** **Circuit Description (2 marks):** Three JK flip-flops in toggle mode, cascaded through **XOR gates** controlled by mode input $M$. The clock equation for each stage is: $$CK_{i+1} = Q_i \oplus M$$ **Truth Table (3 marks):** | CLK Pulse | M | $Q_2$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_0$ | Mode | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Up | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Up | | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Up | | ... | 0 | ... | ... | ... | Up | | 7 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Up | | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Up (rollover) | | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Down | | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | Down | | ... | 1 | ... | ... | ... | Down | **Working Explanation (2 marks):** When $M = 0$, the XOR gates pass $Q_i$ directly → up-count. When $M = 1$, the XOR gates invert $Q_i$ → down-count. The LSB ($Q_0$) always toggles with the clock regardless of $M$. **Part (b) — Cascaded Mod-6 / Mod-10 Counter (7 marks):** **(i) FF count (2 marks):** - Mod-6 stage: $n = \lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = 3$ FFs - Mod-10 stage: $n = \lceil \log_2 10 \rceil = 4$ FFs **(ii) Reset logic (2 marks):** - Mod-6 reset: $R_1 = Q_2 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0}$ (reset at count `110`) - Mod-10 reset: $R_2 = Q_3 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_2} \cdot \overline{Q_0}$ (reset at count `1010`) **(iii) Justification (3 marks):** Mod-60 directly would require $n = \lceil \log_2 60 \rceil = 6$ flip-flops, but would produce a 64-state cycle with 4 unused states, requiring complex decoding. **Cascading Mod-6 × Mod-10 = Mod-60** uses only 3 + 4 = 7 flip-flops total, with cleaner BCD-decadable architecture. It also matches the natural human time-base (60 seconds = 60 minutes), making the decoded outputs directly meaningful for display drivers. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $N$ with $n$:** $N$ is the modulus (number of states), $n$ is the number of flip-flops. Students frequently interchange them, leading to a 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function:** Always use $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$, never round down. For Mod-10, $\log_2 10 \approx 3.32$, not 3. > 3. **Missing the glitch explanation in Mod-N designs:** Examiners specifically look for awareness of the spurious state at the reset condition. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Wrong clock input for down counter:** Down counters need $\overline{Q_{i-1}}$ as clock, not $Q_{i-1}$. Many students write the up-counter formula for both cases. > 5. **Not mentioning propagation delay:** The defining limitation of asynchronous counters is the cumulative $n \cdot t_{pd}$ delay. Always include this in descriptive answers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 4** - ✅ An **asynchronous counter** clocks only the first flip-flop; subsequent FFs are clocked by previous outputs. - ✅ It is called a **ripple counter** because the clock *ripples* through the chain. - ✅ A **3-bit counter** has $2^3 = \mathbf{8}$ states (0 to 7). - ✅ **Up counter** uses $Q_{i-1}$ as clock for the next stage (negative-edge triggered). - ✅ **Down counter** uses $\overline{Q_{i-1}}$ as clock for the next stage. - ✅ **Up/Down counter** uses $CK_{i+1} = Q_i \oplus M$ (XOR with mode input $M$). - ✅ **Mod-N counter** cycles through N states: $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ FFs required. - ✅ **Mod-N reset logic** uses an AND gate to detect the count N and force asynchronous clear. - ✅ A **glitch state** briefly appears at the reset value in truncated (non-power-of-2) counters. - ✅ **Maximum frequency** of an asynchronous counter: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot t_{pd}}$. - ✅ **MSB output** of a Mod-$2^n$ counter is a square wave with **50% duty cycle** at frequency $f_{CLK} / 2^n$. - ✅ **Cascading** Mod-6 and Mod-10 = Mod-60, used in digital clocks. - ✅ JK flip-flops are used in **toggle mode** ($J = K = 1$) for counter design. - ✅ The LSB **always toggles** on every clock pulse, irrespective of mode. ---

topics.title: Synchronous Counter: Realization of 4-bit Up/Down Counter, Realization of Mod-N Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Counter: Realization of 4-bit Up/Down Counter, Realization of Mod-N Counters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Synchronous Counter?

A **Synchronous Counter** (also called a **Parallel Counter**) is a sequential logic circuit in which **all flip-flops are triggered simultaneously by the SAME common clock pulse**. Unlike asynchronous (ripple) counters where clock pulses propagate through stages sequentially, the synchronous design eliminates propagation delay between stages, allowing the counter to operate at **much higher clock frequencies** with **zero cumulative ripple delay**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** A synchronous counter is a digital counter circuit in which the clock inputs of all flip-flops are tied to a common clock signal, and the state transitions of the flip-flops are determined by combinational logic driven by the current state outputs.

## 1.2 The Two Sub-Topics in This Experiment

**A. 4-bit Up/Down Counter:**
A bidirectional counter capable of counting in ascending order (**0 → 15**) or descending order (**15 → 0**), selected by a single control input called the **Mode (M)** or **Up/Down** line.

**B. Mod-N Counter:**
A counter that cycles through exactly **N distinct states** before returning to its initial state. Here, **N** is called the **Modulus** of the counter. For example, a **Mod-10** counter (also called a **Decade Counter**) counts 0 to 9 and then resets.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Synchronized Relay Race

Imagine a **4-member relay team** at the starting line:

- **Asynchronous (Ripple) Counter:** Each runner waits for the baton to physically reach them. The handoff causes tiny delays — runner 2 starts only when runner 1 has finished, and so on. This is *slow* and *cumulative*.
- **Synchronous Counter:** All 4 runners listen to the **SAME starting pistol shot** simultaneously. Each runner's movement (whether to run or stay) is **predetermined by the prior state of the team** (e.g., runner 3 only runs if runners 1 and 2 have already run). Everyone starts at the same instant — *fast and parallel*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The synchronous counter is the **industry-preferred** design because it eliminates **clock skew** and **glitches**, making it the backbone of **frequency dividers, digital clocks, timers, ADCs, and address generators** in KTU lab assessments.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulus (N)** | Number of unique states per cycle | $N = 2^n$ for natural n-bit counter |
| **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$)** | External clock applied to all flip-flops | Typically **1 Hz to 100 MHz** in lab |
| **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** | Time between clock edge and output change | **~5–10 ns** per TTL flip-flop |
| **Maximum Count** | Highest state reached in the cycle | $2^n - 1$ (for n-bit natural counter) |
| **Mode Control (M)** | Selects Up (M=1) or Down (M=0) | Logic 0 or Logic 1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of a 4-bit Synchronous Up Counter

A 4-bit synchronous up counter uses **4 T (Toggle) flip-flops** (or equivalently, **JK flip-flops with J=K=1**). The key design insight is:

> **A flip-flop toggles ONLY when all lower-order bits are 1.**

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **FF0 (LSB):** Toggles on **EVERY clock pulse** → $T_0 = 1$
- **FF1:** Toggles when $Q_0 = 1$ → $T_1 = Q_0$
- **FF2:** Toggles when $Q_0 \cdot Q_1 = 1$ → $T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$
- **FF3 (MSB):** Toggles when $Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2 = 1$ → $T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$

This produces the natural binary counting sequence: **0000 → 0001 → 0010 → ... → 1111 → 0000**

## 2.2 Operational Principle of a 4-bit Synchronous Down Counter

The same hardware, but the toggle conditions use **complemented (Q') outputs**:

- $T_0 = 1$
- $T_1 = Q_0'$
- $T_2 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1'$
- $T_3 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdot Q_2'$

This produces: **1111 → 1110 → 1101 → ... → 0000 → 1111**

## 2.3 Operational Principle of a 4-bit Up/Down Counter

A **2:1 MUX** (or equivalently, an **XOR gate with Mode input**) selects between $Q$ and $Q'$:

$$T_i = M \cdot Q_{i-1} + M' \cdot Q_{i-1}'$$

$$T_i = M \oplus Q_{i-1} \quad \text{(for adjacent pair selection)}$$

When **M = 1** → Up counting (uses $Q$ outputs)
When **M = 0** → Down counting (uses $Q'$ outputs)

## 2.4 Operational Principle of a Mod-N Counter

A Mod-N counter requires $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ flip-flops. Two main design approaches:

**Approach 1: Reset Method (Most Common in KTU Labs)**
- Design a natural $2^n$ counter where $2^{n-1} < N \le 2^n$
- Add a combinational **reset/clear circuit** that activates when the count reaches $N$
- Example: **Mod-10 (Decade) counter** — count 0 to 9, then reset to 0 on the 10th clock

**Approach 2: Gating Method (For Arbitrary N)**
- Detect the count value $N$ and force all flip-flops to 0 on the next clock
- Used when the modulus is **not a power of 2**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of flip-flops for natural counter | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | N = desired modulus |
| 2 | Maximum count of n-bit counter | $N_{max} = 2^n - 1$ | Natural binary sequence |
| 3 | Modulus of n-bit counter | $N = 2^n$ | Total states in one cycle |
| 4 | Toggle input for synchronous up counter (bit $i$) | $T_i = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdots Q_{i-1}$ | For $i \ge 1$; $T_0 = 1$ |
| 5 | Toggle input for synchronous down counter (bit $i$) | $T_i = Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdots Q_{i-1}'$ | For $i \ge 1$; $T_0 = 1$ |
| 6 | Toggle input for up/down counter (bit $i$) | $T_i = M \cdot Q_{i-1} + M' \cdot Q_{i-1}'$ | M = mode control |
| 7 | Output frequency at FF $i$ | $f_{out,i} = f_{clk} / 2^{i+1}$ | Frequency division |
| 8 | Maximum operating frequency | $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{pd,\,FF} + t_{pd,\,gating})$ | Limited by gating delay |
| 9 | Reset condition for Mod-N counter | Detect count $= N$, apply CLEAR | Using AND/OR gate |
| 10 | Decade counter modulus | $N = 10$, requires $n = 4$ FFs | Counts 0 to 9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Synchronous counters are the **heart of every digital system clock**, **CPU program counter (PC)**, **timer ICs (e.g., 8253/8254)**, **digital voltmeters**, and **traffic light controllers**. The up/down variant is used in **rotary encoders, position tracking, and CNC machine tools**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Toggle Equations for 4-bit Synchronous Up Counter

### Step 1: Define the Counting Sequence

We need the counter to follow the sequence $0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 15$ in binary. Let $Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ be the outputs from MSB to LSB.

### Step 2: Build the Excitation Table for T Flip-Flops

For a T flip-flop: $Q_{next} = T \oplus Q$, which means:
- If $T = 0$ → state **holds** ($Q_{next} = Q$)
- If $T = 1$ → state **toggles** ($Q_{next} = Q'$)

We list when each flip-flop must toggle to advance the count by 1.

### Step 3: Determine Toggle Conditions for Each FF

**For FF0 (LSB):** The LSB toggles on every clock edge.

$$T_0 = 1 \quad \text{(constant, always toggle)}$$

**For FF1:** FF1 toggles when $Q_0$ transitions from 0 to 1, i.e., when the present $Q_0 = 1$.

$$T_1 = Q_0$$

**For FF2:** FF2 toggles when both $Q_0$ and $Q_1$ are 1 in the present state.

$$T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$$

**For FF3 (MSB):** FF3 toggles when $Q_0$, $Q_1$, and $Q_2$ are all 1.

$$T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$$

### Step 4: Generalize the Pattern

$$\boxed{T_i = \prod_{j=0}^{i-1} Q_j \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1, \quad T_0 = 1}$$

This is the **canonical KTU board derivation** for any n-bit synchronous up counter.

## 3.2 Derivation of Up/Down Counter Equations

### Step 1: Combine Up and Down Conditions

We need a single expression that becomes the up-counter equation when $M=1$ and the down-counter equation when $M=0$.

### Step 2: Use Multiplexer Logic

For each toggle input, select between $Q_{i-1}$ (up mode) and $Q_{i-1}'$ (down mode):

$$T_i = M \cdot (Q_{i-1}) + M' \cdot (Q_{i-1}')$$

### Step 3: Apply Boolean Algebra (XOR equivalence)

The expression $M \cdot X + M' \cdot X'$ is the canonical form of an XOR gate when applied to a single bit:

$$T_i = M \oplus Q_{i-1} \quad \text{(for bit i where i} \ge 1\text{)}$$

For multi-bit products, the XOR is generalized:

$$T_i = M \cdot (Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdots Q_{i-1}) + M' \cdot (Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdots Q_{i-1}')$$

### Step 4: Final Equations for 4-bit Up/Down Counter

$$T_0 = 1$$
$$T_1 = M \cdot Q_0 + M' \cdot Q_0'$$
$$T_2 = M \cdot (Q_0 \cdot Q_1) + M' \cdot (Q_0' \cdot Q_1')$$
$$T_3 = M \cdot (Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2) + M' \cdot (Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdot Q_2')$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Mod-N Counter Reset Logic (Mod-10 Example)

### Step 1: Determine Number of Flip-Flops

We need $2^{n-1} < 10 \le 2^n$. Since $2^3 = 8 < 10 \le 16 = 2^4$, we need $n = 4$ flip-flops.

### Step 2: Identify the Reset State

The natural 4-bit counter counts 0 to 15. To make it count only 0 to 9, we must reset it to 0 when the count reaches **10** (binary $1010$).

$$10_{10} = 1010_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0 = 1010$$

### Step 3: Generate the Reset Signal

The reset (CLEAR) signal must be active when $Q_3 = 1$ and $Q_1 = 1$ (since $Q_2 = Q_0 = 0$ in state 1010):

$$\text{CLEAR} = Q_3 \cdot Q_1$$

### Step 4: Truth Table Verification

| Clock | $Q_3$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_0$ | CLEAR ($Q_3 \cdot Q_1$) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ... | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Normal count |
| 9 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Count = 9 |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **1** | **RESET to 0000** |
| 11 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Restart cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Some designs use the count value $N$ itself as the trigger (e.g., CLEAR = $Q_3 \cdot Q_1$ for Mod-10). Others detect the count $N-1$ and reset on the next clock. Always **state your assumption** explicitly in the exam.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Asynchronous (Ripple) | Synchronous (Parallel) | Up/Down Synchronous | Mod-N Synchronous |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock connection** | To FF0 only; output drives next FF | Common clock to all FFs | Common clock to all FFs | Common clock to all FFs |
| **Propagation delay** | $n \cdot t_{pd}$ (cumulative) | $t_{pd,\,FF} + t_{pd,\,gate}$ | Same as synchronous | Same as synchronous |
| **Max clock frequency** | Low (limited by ripple) | **Very High** | Very High | High |
| **Gating complexity** | None | Moderate (AND gates) | Higher (MUX/XOR) | Moderate + Reset logic |
| **Number of FFs for Mod-N** | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ |
| **Glitches/Spikes** | Yes (on output) | No (parallel update) | No | Depends on reset method |
| **Power consumption** | Lower (fewer gates) | Higher (more combinational logic) | Highest | High |
| **Application** | Simple frequency dividers | **CPU counters, timers** | Position encoders, ADCs | Digital clocks, decimal displays |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a 4-bit Synchronous Up Counter

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the parallel clocking architecture and the gating logic for each flip-flop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    CLK[Common Clock Pulse] --> FF0[FF0: T Flip-Flop, T0=1]
    CLK --> FF1[FF1: T Flip-Flop, T1=Q0]
    CLK --> FF2[FF2: T Flip-Flop, T2=Q0.Q1]
    CLK --> FF3[FF3: T Flip-Flop, T3=Q0.Q1.Q2]

    FF0 --> Q0_OUT[Q0: LSB Output]
    FF1 --> Q1_OUT[Q1 Output]
    FF2 --> Q2_OUT[Q2 Output]
    FF3 --> Q3_OUT[Q3: MSB Output]

    Q0_OUT -. feeds .-> FF1
    Q1_OUT -. feeds .-> FF2
    Q2_OUT -. feeds .-> FF3

    AND1[AND Gate: Q0.Q1] --> FF2
    AND2[AND Gate: Q0.Q1.Q2] --> FF3
    Q0_OUT --> AND1
    Q0_OUT --> AND2
    Q1_OUT --> AND1
    Q1_OUT --> AND2
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of a 4-bit Up/Down Counter

This diagram shows the Mode control logic and how it selects between $Q$ and $Q'$ outputs to drive each flip-flop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Mode Control M] --> SEL1[2:1 MUX Stage 1]
    M --> SEL2[2:1 MUX Stage 2]
    M --> SEL3[2:1 MUX Stage 3]

    Q0[Q0 from FF0] --> SEL1
    Q0C[Q0-bar from FF0] --> SEL1
    Q1[Q1 from FF1] --> SEL2
    Q1C[Q1-bar from FF1] --> SEL2
    Q0 --> SEL2
    Q0C --> SEL2
    Q2[Q2 from FF2] --> SEL3
    Q2C[Q2-bar from FF2] --> SEL3
    Q1 --> SEL3
    Q1C --> SEL3
    Q0 --> SEL3
    Q0C --> SEL3

    SEL1 --> T1[T1 input to FF1]
    SEL2 --> T2[T2 input to FF2]
    SEL3 --> T3[T3 input to FF3]

    FF0[FF0: T0=1, always toggles] --> Q0
    FF0 --> Q0C
    FF1 --> Q1
    FF1 --> Q1C
    FF2 --> Q2
    FF2 --> Q2C
    FF3[FF3: receives T3] --> Q3[Q3: MSB Output]
    FF1 --> Q1
    FF2 --> Q2

    CLK2[Common Clock] --> FF0
    CLK2 --> FF1
    CLK2 --> FF2
    CLK2 --> FF3
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture of a Mod-N Counter (Mod-10 Example)

The diagram shows how a 4-bit natural counter is converted into a Mod-10 counter by adding reset gating.

```mermaid
graph LR
    CLK3[Clock Input] --> FFA[FF0: T=1]
    CLK3 --> FFB[FF1: T=Q0]
    CLK3 --> FFC[FF2: T=Q0.Q1]
    CLK3 --> FFD[FF3: T=Q0.Q1.Q2]

    FFA --> QA[Q0]
    FFB --> QB[Q1]
    FFC --> QC[Q2]
    FFD --> QD[Q3]

    QA --> ANDG[AND Gate: Q3 AND Q1]
    QD --> ANDG
    QB --> ANDG
    QC --> ANDG

    ANDG --> CLR[Asynchronous CLEAR Signal]
    CLR --> FFA
    CLR --> FFB
    CLR --> FFC
    CLR --> FFD

    QA --> OUTA[Display: Bit 0]
    QB --> OUTB[Display: Bit 1]
    QC --> OUTC[Display: Bit 2]
    QD --> OUTD[Display: Bit 3: MSB]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Counting Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON: All FFs Reset to 0000] --> B[Apply Clock Pulse]
    B --> C{Clock Edge Detected?}
    C -- Yes --> D[All FFs Sample Inputs Simultaneously]
    C -- No --> B
    D --> E{For Each FF: Evaluate T Input}
    E -- T=1 --> F[Toggle FF Output]
    E -- T=0 --> G[Hold FF Output]
    F --> H[Update Display / Next State]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Count Reaches Modulus N?}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[Trigger Reset Logic]
    J --> K[Force FFs to 0000]
    K --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Viva:** When asked to "draw the circuit," students often forget to show the **AND gate chain** feeding into FF2 and FF3. Always label **every gate output** and **every FF input/output** explicitly to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a synchronous counter. State two advantages of a synchronous counter over an asynchronous (ripple) counter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **synchronous counter** is a digital counter circuit in which the **clock inputs of all flip-flops are connected to a common clock signal**, and the state transitions of the flip-flops are controlled by combinational logic based on the current outputs.

**Two Advantages over Asynchronous Counter:**
1. **Faster operation / Higher clock frequency:** Since all flip-flops are clocked simultaneously, the total propagation delay equals only **one flip-flop delay + one gating delay**, instead of $n \times t_{pd}$ in a ripple counter.
2. **No cumulative ripple delay / No glitches:** All output bits change simultaneously on the active clock edge, eliminating timing skew and false intermediate counts (glitches).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is a Mod-N counter? For a Mod-12 counter, determine the number of flip-flops required and the maximum count value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Mod-N counter** is a counter that has exactly **N distinct stable states** in its counting cycle, after which it **resets to the initial state**. The integer N is called the **modulus** of the counter.

For **Mod-12 counter:**
- Number of flip-flops: $n = \lceil \log_2 12 \rceil = \lceil 3.585 \rceil = \mathbf{4}$
- Maximum count value: $N_{max} = N - 1 = \mathbf{11}$

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for FF calculation + 1 mark for max count = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Design a 4-bit synchronous up counter using T flip-flops. Derive the toggle equations and draw the logic diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the number of flip-flops** — For a 4-bit counter, $n = 4$ flip-flops are required (FF0, FF1, FF2, FF3).

**Step 2: Construct the Excitation Table:**

For a T flip-flop: $Q_{next} = T \oplus Q$, so $T = 1$ toggles and $T = 0$ holds.

| Present State ($Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$) | Next State | $T_3$ | $T_2$ | $T_1$ | $T_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0000 | 0001 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 0001 | 0010 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 0010 | 0011 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 0011 | 0100 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 0100 | 0101 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 0101 | 0110 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 0110 | 0111 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 0111 | 1000 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| ... (pattern continues) | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Step 3: Derive Toggle Equations from the Pattern:**

$$T_0 = 1$$
$$T_1 = Q_0$$
$$T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$$
$$T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$$

**Step 4: Logic Diagram:** Common clock to all 4 FFs; $T_0 = 1$ (connected to Vcc); $T_1 = Q_0$; $T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$ (2-input AND); $T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$ (3-input AND).

*(1 mark for FF count + 2 marks for excitation table + 3 marks for equations + 1 mark for diagram = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Design a 4-bit synchronous up/down counter with a mode control input M. Explain the operation in both modes and derive all toggle equations.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the Mode Function**
- When **M = 1** → Up counting (incrementing sequence 0000 → 1111)
- When **M = 0** → Down counting (decrementing sequence 1111 → 0000)

**Step 2: Derive Toggle Equations for Up Mode (M=1):**
$$T_0 = 1, \quad T_1 = Q_0, \quad T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1, \quad T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$$

**Step 3: Derive Toggle Equations for Down Mode (M=0):**
$$T_0 = 1, \quad T_1 = Q_0', \quad T_2 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1', \quad T_3 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdot Q_2'$$

**Step 4: Combine Using Multiplexer Logic:**

For each toggle input, select between $Q$ (up) and $Q'$ (down) using M:

$$T_0 = 1$$

$$T_1 = M \cdot Q_0 + M' \cdot Q_0'$$

$$T_2 = M \cdot (Q_0 \cdot Q_1) + M' \cdot (Q_0' \cdot Q_1')$$

$$T_3 = M \cdot (Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2) + M' \cdot (Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdot Q_2')$$

**Step 5: Hardware Implementation:**
- 4 T (or JK) flip-flops with common clock
- 3 **2:1 MUXes** (or 2-input AND-OR gates) for selecting between $Q$ and $Q'$
- M connected as the **select line** of all MUXes

**Operation Verification:**
- M = 1, Present state = 0100 (4) → Next state = 0101 (5) ✓
- M = 0, Present state = 0100 (4) → Next state = 0011 (3) ✓

*(2 marks for separate up/down equations + 3 marks for combined equations + 2 marks for explanation/hardware = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the design of a Mod-10 (Decade) counter using a 4-bit synchronous up counter. Derive the reset condition and draw the circuit diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine Number of Flip-Flops**
For Mod-10: $2^3 = 8 < 10 \le 16 = 2^4$, so $n = 4$ flip-flops are required.

**Step 2: Design the Natural 4-bit Up Counter (as in Question A)**
The 4-bit synchronous up counter naturally counts: 0, 1, 2, ..., 15.

**Step 3: Identify the Reset State**
To limit the count to 0–9, we must reset the counter when it reaches **10 (binary 1010)**:
$$10_{10} = 1010_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_3 = 1, Q_2 = 0, Q_1 = 1, Q_0 = 0$$

**Step 4: Derive the Reset Equation**
The reset (CLEAR) signal must be active ONLY in state 1010:

$$\text{CLEAR} = Q_3 \cdot Q_1$$

This requires a 2-input AND gate with inputs $Q_3$ and $Q_1$.

**Step 5: Circuit Diagram Description**
- 4 T flip-flops (FF0 to FF3) with common clock
- Toggle inputs: $T_0 = 1$, $T_1 = Q_0$, $T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$, $T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$
- A 2-input AND gate detecting $Q_3 \cdot Q_1$
- The AND output is fed to the **asynchronous CLEAR** input of all 4 flip-flops

**Step 6: Counting Sequence Verification**

| Clock | $Q_3 Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ | Decimal | CLEAR Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000 | 0 | No reset |
| 9 | 1001 | 9 | No reset |
| **10** | **1010** | **10** | **RESET to 0000** |
| 11 | 0000 | 0 | Count restarts |

*(1 mark FF count + 1 mark base counter design + 2 marks reset equation + 3 marks for circuit + verification = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Design a Mod-6 counter using synchronous design technique. Draw the timing diagram and explain the operation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine Number of Flip-Flops**
For Mod-6: $2^2 = 4 < 6 \le 8 = 2^3$, so $n = 3$ flip-flops are required.

**Step 2: Define the Counting Sequence**
The counter must cycle through: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, then reset to 0.

| Count | $Q_2$ | $Q_1$ | $Q_0$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **6** | **1** | **1** | **0** | → **RESET TRIGGERED** |
| 0 (restart) | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3: Derive Toggle Inputs Using Excitation Table**

For T flip-flops, $T = 1$ toggles and $T = 0$ holds.

- **FF0:** Toggles every clock → $T_0 = 1$
- **FF1:** Toggles when transitioning $00 \to 01$ (i.e., $Q_0 = 1$) AND $10 \to 11$ (i.e., $Q_2 \cdot Q_0$)

  Analyzing all transitions: $T_1 = 1$ when going from 0→1, 1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→5
  - $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0 = 001 \to 010$: $T_1 = 1$ (Q0=1)
  - $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0 = 011 \to 100$: $T_1 = 1$ (Q0=1)
  - $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0 = 101 \to 000$ (reset): depends

  Using the **reset method** simplifies design:
  $T_1 = Q_0$ (same as natural 3-bit counter)
  $T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$ (same as natural 3-bit counter)

**Step 4: Reset Logic**
Detect count = 6 (binary 110) and apply CLEAR:

$$\text{CLEAR} = Q_2 \cdot Q_1$$

**Step 5: Timing Diagram (Qualitative Description)**
- $Q_0$: toggles at every clock edge (highest frequency output)
- $Q_1$: toggles every 2nd clock
- $Q_2$: toggles every 4th clock, but stays at 0 after clock 5 (since 6 is reset)
- The CLEAR signal goes HIGH momentarily at clock 6, then LOW

**Step 6: Applications**
Mod-6 counters are used in **divide-by-6 frequency dividers** and **3-phase clock generators**.

*(2 marks sequence + 2 marks toggle equations + 2 marks reset logic + 1 mark timing diagram = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the common clock line:** Students often draw asynchronous-style circuits with the previous FF output feeding the next FF's clock. **Always show a SINGLE clock line going to ALL flip-flops** in synchronous designs. *(−2 marks if missed)*
> 2. **Confusing $T_i$ for $J_i$ and $K_i$:** When using JK flip-flops, the equations $J_i = K_i = T_i$ hold. Writing only $J_i$ without $K_i$ is incomplete. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Wrong reset state in Mod-N counter:** Some students reset at $N-1$ instead of $N$, or forget that the reset is **asynchronous** (active immediately, not on next clock). **Always clarify synchronous vs asynchronous reset** in your answer. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Missing AND gate chain for higher bits:** The expression $Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$ requires a **3-input AND gate**, not two 2-input gates in series. If the question asks for gate-level details, show the **cascaded structure** explicitly. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Not labeling the Mode input:** In up/down counters, forgetting to label the **M (Mode)** input on the MUX select line is a frequent error. *(−1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synchronous counter** uses a **common clock** for all flip-flops; ripple counter uses cascaded clocks.
- **4-bit up counter toggle equations:** $T_0 = 1$, $T_1 = Q_0$, $T_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$, $T_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$.
- **4-bit down counter toggle equations:** $T_0 = 1$, $T_1 = Q_0'$, $T_2 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1'$, $T_3 = Q_0' \cdot Q_1' \cdot Q_2'$.
- **Up/Down counter** uses $T_i = M \cdot Q_{i-1} + M' \cdot Q_{i-1}'$ (or XOR form: $T_i = M \oplus Q_{i-1}$ for single-bit).
- **Mode M = 1** → Up counting; **Mode M = 0** → Down counting.
- **Mod-N counter** requires $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ flip-flops.
- **Mod-N reset equation** is derived by detecting the count value $N$ and feeding it to the **CLEAR** input.
- **Frequency division:** Each flip-flop divides input frequency by 2; the $i$-th FF output has $f_{clk} / 2^{i+1}$.
- **Synchronous counters are FASTER** than asynchronous (ripple) counters because the propagation delay is independent of the number of bits.
- **Decade (Mod-10) counter** is the most common Mod-N counter; used in digital clocks, frequency counters, and decimal displays.
- **JK flip-flops with J = K = 1** act as T flip-flops — most KTU labs implement counters this way using IC 7476.
- **IC Pin Reference for Lab:** 7473 (dual JK), 7476 (dual JK with preset/clear), 74161 (4-bit synchronous up counter), 74191 (4-bit synchronous up/down counter).
- **Common ICs used in KTU lab experiments:** **IC 7476** for JK flip-flops, **IC 7408** for AND gates, **IC 7432** for OR gates, **IC 7486** for XOR gates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra:** *"Common clock, gated toggles, parallel truth — that's how synchronous counters uncouple the delay chain."* Remember this for viva voce and short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ring Counter and Johnson Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL307 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 4
> - **Topic:** Ring Counter and Johnson Counter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Shift Register

A **Shift Register** is a sequential logic circuit consisting of a cascade of flip-flops (typically D-type or JK flip-flops) that share a common clock signal. Data shifts serially from one flip-flop to the next on each clock pulse, functioning as a temporary storage device for binary information.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A shift register is a group of flip-flops connected in series such that the output of one flip-flop becomes the input of the next flip-flop on the application of clock pulses. It is used for data storage, data transfer, and data manipulation.

## 1.2 Ring Counter – Formal Definition

A **Ring Counter** is a special type of shift register in which the output of the **last flip-flop** is connected (fed back) to the **input of the first flip-flop**, creating a closed loop or "ring." This feedback arrangement causes a single '1' (or '0') to circulate around the loop indefinitely, generating a predetermined sequence of states.

- An **n-bit ring counter** has exactly **n** valid (unique) states.
- It is a **synchronous sequential circuit**.
- It requires a **PRESET** (initialization) to load the starting pattern, typically `100...0`.
- No decoding logic is required — the outputs themselves act as decoded states.

## 1.3 Johnson Counter – Formal Definition

A **Johnson Counter** (also called a **Twisted Ring Counter** or **Switch-Tail Ring Counter**) is a modified ring counter in which the **complemented output ($\overline{Q}$)** of the last flip-flop is fed back to the input of the first flip-flop. This twisted feedback results in a sequence of **2n unique states** for an n-bit counter, effectively doubling the modulus of a standard ring counter.

- An **n-bit Johnson counter** has exactly **2n** valid (unique) states.
- It is a **modulo-2n counter**.
- The count sequence is symmetric and predictable.
- Used extensively in phase generation, frequency division, and digital waveform synthesis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ring and Johnson counters are classified as **Shift Register Counters** because they generate a count sequence by continuously shifting data. The modulus of a Ring counter = n, and the modulus of a Johnson counter = 2n.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 1.4.1 Ring Counter – The "Circular Conveyer Belt" Analogy
Imagine a circular conveyor belt in a factory with **4 holding stations** (flip-flops). A single red ball (the '1' bit) is placed at station 1, while stations 2, 3, and 4 remain empty (the '0' bits). Every time the manager presses a button (clock pulse), the belt rotates by one position. The ball jumps from station 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1 → 2 ... forever. Each station lights up one at a time in sequence. This is exactly how a 4-bit ring counter works — a single '1' circulates through the flip-flops.

### 1.4.2 Johnson Counter – The "Boat Trailer Reversed" Analogy
Take the same conveyor belt, but now connect the **exit station to the entry station in REVERSE** — like hooking up a trailer back-to-front. Now the ball enters the system, fills each station progressively, stays at the far end, then "negatively" propagates back. Over 8 clock cycles, the pattern goes: empty, ¼ full, ½ full, ¾ full, completely full, then ¾ empty, ½ empty, ¼ empty, then back to empty. This 8-state "filling and emptying" cycle is the Johnson counter.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** 
> - **Ring Counter** = "Same feed back" → Modulus = n
> - **Johnson Counter** = "Complement feed back" → Modulus = 2n

## 1.5 Key Standard Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of flip-flops | n | Positive integer (2, 3, 4...) |
| Ring counter modulus | $M_{ring}$ | **n** |
| Johnson counter modulus | $M_{johnson}$ | **2n** |
| Frequency division factor | — | **n** (ring) / **2n** (Johnson) |
| Required initialization | — | Reset/Preset before operation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a 4-bit Ring Counter

A 4-bit ring counter is constructed from four D flip-flops (FF1, FF2, FF3, FF4) connected in series. The **Q output of the last flip-flop (FF4)** is directly fed back to the **D input of the first flip-flop (FF1)**.

**Logic Equations:**
- $D_1 = Q_4$ (Serial input from last stage)
- $D_2 = Q_1$
- $D_3 = Q_2$
- $D_4 = Q_3$

**Operation Logic:**

1. **Initialization:** Apply a PRESET pulse to FF1, forcing $Q_1 Q_2 Q_3 Q_4 = 1000$. All other flip-flops are cleared.
2. **Clock Pulse 1:** The '1' shifts from FF1 to FF2. State: $0100$.
3. **Clock Pulse 2:** The '1' shifts from FF2 to FF3. State: $0010$.
4. **Clock Pulse 3:** The '1' shifts from FF3 to FF4. State: $0001$.
5. **Clock Pulse 4:** The '1' from FF4 is fed back to FF1. State: $1000$ (cycle repeats).
6. After 4 clock pulses, the pattern repeats → **Modulus-4 counter**.

## 2.2 Architecture of a 4-bit Johnson Counter

A 4-bit Johnson counter is constructed from four D flip-flops connected in series. The **complemented output ($\overline{Q_4}$)** of the last flip-flop is fed back to the **D input of the first flip-flop**.

**Logic Equations:**
- $D_1 = \overline{Q_4}$ (Twisted/Complement feedback)
- $D_2 = Q_1$
- $D_3 = Q_2$
- $D_4 = Q_3$

**Operation Logic:**

1. **Initialization:** Clear all flip-flops, $Q_1 Q_2 Q_3 Q_4 = 0000$. Since $\overline{Q_4} = 1$, the input to FF1 is 1.
